experiments.title: Can Highlighting the Monthly Impact Boost Recurring Giving?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a monthly giving callout above the donation form on the main donation page will increase monthly donor conversions, as framing monthly giving as impactful and rewarding will motivate recurring support without displacing larger one-time donations. Framing monthly giving as impactful and rewarding will motivate recurring support without displacing larger one-time donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/26/2025 - 10/26/2025
The organization’s main donation page serves both committed donors and new visitors, playing a central role in overall giving. Unlike acquisition-focused pages meant to convert new audiences, this page often attracts high-value donors and returning supporters. As such, it presents a high-opportunity but high-risk space for testing.
In previous tests on acquisition donation pages, the organization found that adding a monthly gift callout that highlights the recurring impact and donor benefits increased the number of donors who chose to give monthly. Those tests indicated that, with the right framing, some page visitors can be persuaded to commit to becoming long-term donors.
This experiment asked whether that insight would transfer to the main donation page (without cannibalizing revenue from larger one-time gifts). Would prompting donors to “consider a monthly gift” increase monthly conversions? Or would it cause high-capacity donors to downshift from large one-time gifts to smaller recurring ones?
The Control used the standard donation page layout, which defaults to a one-time gift and includes no additional prompts about monthly giving.
The Treatment introduced a soft callout above the form that read: “Would you consider a monthly donation? Your monthly gift of $15 ensures families in spiritual crisis can find trusted help the moment they need it—and receive biblical support year-round. Plus, you’ll get to choose a new free resource every month for your family.”
No other changes were made to the page layout or the form’s functionality. We randomly split traffic 50/50 between both versions and ran this test for 1 month.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test demonstrated that a soft monthly giving prompt above the donation form can significantly increase recurring donor conversions—without suppressing large gifts and with potential upside for smaller one-time donations.
The Treatment produced a 125% increase in monthly donor conversions (2.1% vs. 0.9%), with a statistically significant 99.3% level of confidence.
 	Recurring donor volume more than doubled (33 vs. 15), while the average monthly gift remained steady ($30 on Treatment vs. $31 on Control, after removing an outlier)
 	While the Treatment had fewer gifts over $1,000 (35 vs. 41), this difference was not statistically significant (44% level of confidence), indicating no reliable evidence that large one-time gifts were cannibalized.
smaller one-time gifts (under $1,000)
The Treatment saw a 9.5% lift in volume, with an 86.4% level of confidence -- this is a strong directional signal, though not statistically conclusive
 	The average gift amount also increased, from $132 on the Control to $140 on the Treatment
Next Steps for Testing:
Explore alternate monthly ask amounts (e.g., $20, $25, or $30) to assess the revenue impact of shifting the default suggested amount.
 	Refine the callout language or visual design to test whether urgency, impact framing, or reward emphasis further improves performance.
 	Test this approach on other key donation pathways (e.g., campaign pages, seasonal asks) to evaluate if the effect carries over beyond the main donation page.

experiments.title: How defaulting a donation module to monthly giving impacts conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting to monthly for potential donors will improve conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/31/2025 - 08/13/2025
Throughout the year, we tested a donation module as a replacement for a hero image and saw increases in conversions across the board. The next iteration of this test was to determine whether defaulting to monthly donations affected conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings from the Experiment:

1. Defaulting to monthly donations led to a significant increase in conversions for potential donors.

2. The treatment group saw a 9.7% increase in donations for all traffic with a high level of statistical confidence (99.5%).

How to Use What Was Learned in Future Experimentation:

1. Consider defaulting to monthly donations in future donation modules to potentially improve conversion rates.

2. Continue to experiment with different variables such as donation amounts, payment methods, and messaging to further optimize conversion rates.

3. Monitor and analyze the results of future experiments to continuously improve the donation process and maximize fundraising efforts.

experiments.title: How adding a monthly callout impacts conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a visual call out for monthly giving for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/17/2026 - 09/26/2026
With monthly giving a big focus for Save the Children, we want to test whether a graphic call-out highlighting the impact of monthly giving would increase conversion rates. We ran 3 different variants for this test, only for desktop traffic (since the buttons stacked on mobile):
Control
Variant 1- monthly call out text below the monthly/one-time array button
Variant 2- monthly call out text above the monthly/one-time array button
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test aimed to assess the impact of adding a visual monthly callout on the donation form. We hypothesized that this would strengthen the proposition for monthly giving.
Key Results:
This test showed that the visual monthly callout made the recurring option more salient and easier to process. By reducing cognitive friction and highlighting it as a “default-worthy” choice, users were nudged to see monthly as the standard, or even preferred, behavior.
Rather than deterring one-time donors, the callout acted as a commitment anchor. Donors compared themselves against the recurring option and felt reassured in choosing either. The result is a “lift all boats” effect: greater clarity increases confidence, which increases giving.
This suggests that visual emphasis on giving types does more than shift behavior between options. It can grow the whole pie by lowering decision friction and reinforcing giving as the normative action.

experiments.title: How Adding a One-Time Gift Option to a Slide-Up Promoting Recurring Giving Impacted Transactions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a one-time gift option to the slide-up will lead to more transactions overall, while maintaining a similar number of sustaining gifts. This approach is designed to attract individuals who may not be ready to commit to a sustaining gift, while still capturing those who were already inclined to make a sustaining contribution.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/08/2025 - 02/14/2025
This experiment was conducted for a public radio station serving North Carolina, during their February Fund Drive. We aimed to test two different slide-ups: one was a previous version that encouraged prospects to make a sustaining gift, while in the test version, we added an option for a one-time gift to see if it would result in more overall gifts, including sustaining contributions.
Everything else remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In conclusion, the results of this experiment indicate that the control version, which emphasized monthly sustaining donations, outperformed the treatment version, which included both one-time and sustaining gift options. The control version generated 53 gifts and $1,510 in revenue, compared to the treatment version's 23 gifts and $595 in revenue.
Breaking down the results further:
The control version had 15 one-time gifts and 38 recurring gifts.
 	The treatment version had 6 one-time gifts and 17 recurring gifts.
 	The control version also saw more first-time gifts, with 35 first-time gifts compared to the treatment's 13.
A key finding was that the control version resulted in a 58% higher donation volume across all traffic than the treatment version, with 100% statistical confidence. This suggests that focusing solely on sustaining donations was more effective at driving contributions, as adding a one-time gift option did not increase the total number of donations.
Additionally, four sustainers made another one-time donation, all through the control version.
Moving forward, it would be beneficial to continue emphasizing the importance and impact of monthly sustaining gifts, as this approach appears to resonate more effectively with prospects. Further testing could explore different messaging strategies and donation options to identify the most effective combination for future fundraising campaigns. These insights will be key to optimizing the approach to maximizing donations in the future.

experiments.title: How does pre-selecting monthly on a tabbed form impact recurring gifts in paid media?
experiments.researchQuestion:
We believe that
defaulting the tabbed form to monthly for paid media users on instant donation pages will achieve an increase in recurring giving, while not negatively impacting overall transactions because it treats monthly giving like a priority, suggesting it as the primary way of giving, versus an after-thought or add-on.
because  it treats monthly giving like a priority, suggesting it as the primary way of giving, versus an after-thought or add-on.
because  it treats monthly giving like a priority, suggesting it as the primary way of giving, versus an after-thought or add-on.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/23/2025 - 08/25/2025
Time of Grace had recently tested into and rolled out a valid increase in overall transactions, when using a tabbed form for paid media traffic. We hypothesize that defaulting the tab to monthly would passively increase recurring giving while not having a negative impact on overall transactions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Based on this data, defaulting the tabbed form to monthly giving for paid media users on instant donation pages significantly increased recurring donations by 1,354.5%.
In addition, Overall Transactions were not negatively impacted; instead, there was a slight bump in giving overall to the treatment. Indicating that this experience is not misleading, and of the 300+ donors who participated, donors were selecting the type of gift they wanted to give—whether that was monthly or one-time. Total transactions and validity of the lift can be seen below:
Overall Transactions:
Control: 3,452 Samples | 151 Donations |
Treatment: 3,652 Samples | 172 Donations | +10% Increase with 64% LOC.
Finally, within the time frame of this experiment being run only one donor reached out to cancel their monthly gift. The impact of this experiment is that Time of Grace can now begin passively acquiring recurring donors from their paid media traffic, without any increase to budget or a separate monthly campaign.

experiments.title: Redesigning the Recurring Ask Module: Can a More Distinct and Motivating Post-Gift Offer Increase Monthly Donor Revenue?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making the recurring ask module more visually distinct, emotionally motivating, and easier to act on for recent one-time donors will increase revenue per visitor by prompting more monthly gifts or higher-value commitments, because reducing friction and reinforcing urgency and donor identity leverages post-gift momentum and encourages deeper investment.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/15/2025 - 11/12/2025
CitizenGO is a political advocacy organization that mobilizes nearly 20 million citizens around the world to take action in defense of life, family, and freedom. Their digital engagement model invites users to sign petitions aligned with their values, then make a one-time donation to support the movement. After donating, supporters land on a receipt page that includes an additional opportunity: to become a monthly donor and sustain the fight long-term.
In an effort to increase the rate of monthly donor conversion, we wanted to test into how we could acquire more monthly gifts on these receipt pages. And in our review of the current recurring gift ask on the receipt page, we identified some key areas of opportunity. The existing module blended into the page layout and offered three preset gift amounts plus a custom field, which may have created friction and choice overload. It also lacked strong motivational framing, focusing mainly on organizational independence.
Because recurring conversions on this page are typically low, the team recognized that minor copy or layout tweaks would likely be insufficient to produce a measurable change. Instead, we pursued a holistic redesign to increase both the visibility and motivational clarity of the recurring ask.
The Treatment featured a complete visual and copy refresh: a bright blue design to visually separate it from the receipt content, simplified choice architecture (one default gift amount with optional customization), and more emotionally compelling language. The new copy emphasized urgency, opposition, and donor identity—positioning monthly supporters as critical to winning ongoing battles. This design was based off of a well-performing recurring gift popup from a donation platform that CitizenGO is not using.
One important thing to note is that the Treatment did not use any higher gift amount than the Control. Instead, the default amount shown in the Treatment’s recurring ask matched the middle ask option from the Control experience. So if a user would have been asked on the Control for $7, $10, or $20 a month, the Treatment would just ask for $10/month.
This test ran in five languages—English, Spanish, French, German, and Italian—and, while expected to have low absolute conversion volume, aimed to determine whether a more distinct, motivating, and streamlined module could better convert new donors into committed monthly givers.
experiments.keyLearnings:
1. Change in Recurring Gifts
Across all languages, the Treatment increased the number of recurring gifts by 17%, but with a confidence level of 82%. While this does not meet the 95% threshold for statistical significance, it represents a strong directional lift, especially given the relatively low sample size.
No individual language segment reached statistical confidence, suggesting that more traffic is needed to validate conversion differences at the segment level.
2. Average Gift Amount
Donors who gave monthly in the Treatment variant contributed higher average gift amounts, rising from €13.93 in the Control to €16.83 in the Treatment. This lift contributed meaningfully to the overall revenue gains and suggests that the redesigned module not only encouraged giving, but also positioned the monthly commitment in a way that inspired greater generosity.
3. Revenue Per Visitor
This was the most compelling outcome of the test.
•	Among the EN (UK) audience, the Treatment drove a 67% lift in revenue per visitor (€0.78 → €1.30) with 98% statistical confidence.
•	The Italian segment saw an 83% lift in revenue per visitor (€0.73 → €1.35), with 93% confidence—just shy of statistical significance, but strongly directional.
•	Most importantly, in aggregate across all languages, the Treatment drove a 39.5% increase in revenue per visitor (from €0.66 to €0.91), with 98.3% statistical confidence.
🎉 This is a major win for CitizenGO, showing that the new module significantly increased the value of post-donation traffic. The combination of higher conversion volume (directionally) and higher gift size resulted in meaningful revenue growth.
⸻
This test shows us that a thoughtfully designed, psychologically grounded upsell experience can convert new donors into higher-value monthly supporters—especially when the ask is timely, low-friction, and tied to the donor’s identity and urgency.
For future testing, we can exlore more angles of personalization, value proposition, or increased ask amounts to drive more engagement and revenue.

experiments.title: How adding an impact callout to a pre-selected monthly tab affects recurring donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a callout on the monthly tab, highlighting the impact of monthly giving for donation page visitors, will increase recurring donations because the callout will help users understand the potential impact they can have on the FUS mission and encourage them to make a recurring donation.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/26/2025 - 10/08/2025
Franciscan University has content offers of their video series’ running on Facebook, which perform really well. They get a lot of donations from these offers, so we wanted to look into ways we could get more recurring donors from audiences coming to the donation pages.
For this test, we had the monthly tab pre-selected and we added a callout to hover above the tab that had language about how monthly giving was the most impactful way they could help FUS continue doing their mission.
This was tested on the donation page of the offers currently running for them in Facebook.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:
1. Adding a callout on the monthly tab highlighting the impact of monthly giving led to a 1.5% increase in Recurring Donations for All Traffic at a 100.0% level of statistical confidence.
2. However, this increase in recurring donations was accompanied by a 211% decrease in Transactions with a 96% level of confidence and a 411% decrease in One-Time Donations with a 100% level of confidence.
In future experimentation, it is crucial to carefully weigh the benefits of increasing recurring donations against the significant decline observed in one-time donations and transactions. Further analysis of why one-time donations and transactions dropped could reveal ways to promote recurring giving without causing large declines in other donation types.
Test different callout messages or positions to optimize recurring donations without hurting other metrics. Segment the audience by behavior and preferences to tailor the approach and maximize total donations.
Continue experimenting with new strategies and closely monitor all donation metrics. Aim to increase recurring donations while maintaining overall donation levels.

experiments.title: How defaulting a form to monthly & adding a monthly callout affects donor conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting the donation form to Monthly giving and adding a clear, impact-focused callout for users on acquisition offer donation pages will achieve an increase in monthly donor conversion because default bias nudges people toward the pre-selected choice, while a tangible impact message increases perceived efficacy and reduces decision friction—making monthly giving feel both easier and more meaningful.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/19/2025 - 08/29/2025
Focus on the Family (FOTF) uses free video series offers, promoted primarily on Facebook and Instagram, to acquire new subscribers and donors. These campaigns have recently become highly efficient at driving new support, as optimizations significantly lowered the gross cost per donor (GCPD) and increased the volume of one-time donors acquired.
With this success, the team sought to improve the quality of donor acquisition by increasing the number of recurring donors acquired through this same channel. The key question: could making monthly giving more visually and textually prominent on the donation form encourage more people to begin a recurring commitment at the point of acquisition?
The Control used the standard acquisition donation form, which defaulted to a $30 one-time gift and contained no additional monthly-specific messaging.
The Treatment donation form was identical in design and copy but defaulted to $30 monthly giving. It also added a callout above the form that read:
“Would you consider a monthly donation? Your monthly gift of $30 will enable 50 people every month to get signed up for this free video series.”
The test ran across all active video series acquisition donation pages for 10 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Defaulting the donation form to Monthly and adding an impact-focused callout led to a 187.7% increase in monthly gifts (99.9% confidence), showing that defaults and impact framing can strongly influence recurring donor acquisition. The average gift also increased slightly ($33 → $36), though not with statistical confidence.
At the same time, one-time gifts decreased by 34.2% (99.9% confidence). However, this is because many of the people who would have given a one-time donation chose to give a monthly gift instead.
The takeaway: monthly defaults can meaningfully grow recurring donors but risk suppressing one-time gifts. Whether this is a net positive depends on the lifetime value of new monthly donors compared to the lost one-time gifts.
Future tests could evaluate long-term revenue impact, try highlighting monthly giving without changing the default, or explore post-donation monthly upgrade prompts.

experiments.title: How a monthly first ask on a high urgency campaign impacts recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a monthly first ask for Fall Share giving page visitors will achieve an increase in recurring giving because more donors will choose to give monthly because the option is presented as the recommended and manageable choice. By also highlighting the benefits of recurring giving, donors will be further motivated to see monthly giving. because  more donors will choose to give monthly because the option is presented as the recommended and manageable choice. By also highlighting the benefits of recurring giving, donors will be further motivated to see monthly giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/16/2025 - 09/26/2025
Each year, Moody Radio runs a Fall Share campaign to inspire generosity among listeners.
This year, we made a strategic shift: instead of prioritizing one-time gifts, we focused on growing Moody’s recurring donor file. To accomplish this, we tested a monthly-first ask during the Fall Share campaign.
The goal was to build their recurring file while minimizing the negative impact on overall conversion on the giving page.
The test ran for 11 days, with traffic evenly split between each variant.
Note: The majority of participants came from known audiences. While a separate non-donor–specific donation page was available, many of the web assets directed to this general campaign page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that implementing a monthly-first ask for Fall Share donation page visitors significantly increased recurring giving. The treatment group experienced a 168.2% increase in donations compared to the control group, with a 99.7% level of confidence in the results.
Furthermore, with this large increase in recurring giving, we wanted to be sure it wasn't at the expense of overall transactions. After further analysis, we discovered the treatment was resulting in a 5.5% decrease in overall transactions at a 42% level of confidence. Based on these results, the value of the more recurring donors outweighs the slight (and invalid) decrease in overall giving.
In future experimentation, we should continue to prioritize the monthly-first ask strategy, as it proved to be highly effective in increasing recurring donor conversions without significantly reducing overall transactions. This approach capitalizes on the convenience and appeal of monthly giving for donors, leading to a higher likelihood of acceptance of the pre-set option and a perception of monthly giving as both the recommended and more manageable choice. By consistently incorporating this strategy in future campaigns and experiments, we can continue to grow our recurring donor file and maximize the impact of our fundraising efforts.

experiments.title: How the addition of the recurring interrupter on acquisition pages impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of the recurring interrupter for Moody Radio acquisition giving page visitors will achieve an increase in recurring giving without negatively impacting overall transactions because  it invites donors to commit to a smaller gift today that multiplies into greater long-term impact, we can grow recurring revenue without reducing immediate generosity. because   it invites donors to commit to a smaller gift today that multiplies into greater long-term impact, we can grow recurring revenue without reducing immediate generosity.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/08/2025
To strengthen Moody’s monthly partner program, we introduced a recurring gift interrupter on Moody Radio’s acquisition giving pages.
This interrupter was displayed to donors initiating a one-time gift under $250, with a dynamic ask based on their original selection:
$200–$250 gift → Suggested $25 monthly commitment
$100–$199 gift → Suggested $15 monthly commitment
Under $100 gift → Suggested $10 monthly commitment
The primary goal was to increase the number of Moody Radio’s monthly partners—building sustainable revenue—while ensuring there was no negative impact on overall one-time giving transactions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key findings from this experiment demonstrate that adding a recurring gift interrupter to Moody Radio’s acquisition giving pages led to a 217.7% increase in monthly donations with a 98.3% level of statistical confidence. This shows that inviting donors—who initially intended to give a one-time gift under $250—to consider a smaller monthly commitment successfully grew the number of monthly partners without reducing one-time giving.
Importantly, overall transactions were not negatively affected. In fact, we observed a 1.7% increase in total transactions (18.8% LOC), providing further evidence that the interrupter did not harm conversion rates on the giving page.
In future experimentation, it is important to continue implementing similar strategies that encourage donors to consider converting their one-time gifts into recurring monthly commitments. This can be achieved by carefully tailoring the suggested monthly amounts based on the original one-time gift selection, as demonstrated in this experiment. Additionally, it would be beneficial to further analyze donor behavior and preferences to continue optimizing the recurring gift interrupter for maximum effectiveness. By leveraging the success of this experiment and continuously testing and refining our approaches, we can further grow Moody Radio’s monthly partner program and enhance sustainable revenue for the organization.

experiments.title: How defaulting a form to monthly & adding a monthly callout affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting the donation form to Monthly giving and adding a clear, impact-focused callout for users on acquisition offer donation pages will achieve an increase in monthly donor conversion because default bias nudges people toward the pre-selected choice, while a tangible impact message increases perceived efficacy and reduces decision friction—making monthly giving feel both easier and more meaningful.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/19/2025 - 08/29/2025
An organization uses free video series offers, promoted primarily on Facebook and Instagram, to acquire new subscribers and donors. These campaigns have recently become highly efficient at driving new support, as optimizations significantly lowered the gross cost per donor (GCPD) and increased the volume of one-time donors acquired.
With this success, the team sought to improve the quality of donor acquisition by increasing the number of recurring donors acquired through this same channel. The key question: could making monthly giving more visually and textually prominent on the donation form encourage more people to begin a recurring commitment at the point of acquisition?
The Control used the standard acquisition donation form, which defaulted to a $30 one-time gift and contained no additional monthly-specific messaging.
The Treatment donation form was identical in design and copy but defaulted to $30 monthly giving. It also added a callout above the form that read:
“Would you consider a monthly donation? Your monthly gift of $30 will enable 50 people every month to get signed up for this free video series.”
The test ran across all active video series acquisition donation pages for 10 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Defaulting the donation form to Monthly and adding an impact-focused callout led to a 187.7% increase in monthly gifts (99.9% confidence), showing that defaults and impact framing can strongly influence recurring donor acquisition. The average gift also increased slightly ($33 → $36), though not with statistical confidence.

At the same time, one-time gifts decreased by 34.2% (99.9% confidence). However, this is because many of the people who would have given a one-time donation chose to give a monthly gift instead.

The takeaway: monthly defaults can meaningfully grow recurring donors but risk suppressing one-time gifts. Whether this is a net positive depends on the lifetime value of new monthly donors compared to the lost one-time gifts.

Future tests could evaluate long-term revenue impact, try highlighting monthly giving without changing the default, or explore post-donation monthly upgrade prompts.

experiments.title: How defaulting the monthly tab on an acquisition offer affects recurring conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting to the monthly tab will achieve an increase in recurring conversion rates because it reduces friction by aligning with the donor’s default behavior, subtly encourages sustained giving through visual anchoring, and reframes the donation as an ongoing commitment rather than a one-time gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/01/2025 - 08/18/2025
The Heritage Foundation is offering a free Guide to the Constitution. In this experiment, they wanted to test how defaulting to the monthly tab on the acquisition donation page of this offer would affect the rate of recurring giving and overall transactions.
50% of traffic was randomly assigned to each variation (Control vs Treatment). The primary goal was to determine whether defaulting the donation format to monthly giving (rather than one-time giving) would influence the recurring conversion rate.
The idea to run this test stemmed from two core insights:
Behavioral Economics Research:
People tend to go with the default option presented to them. When monthly giving is presented as the default tab, it subtly frames that behavior as the norm or recommended path.
Organizational Goal: Boost Recurring Revenue:
Like many nonprofits, Heritage is invested in growing their base of recurring donors. Monthly gifts provide more predictable, long-term revenue.
By making “Make a Monthly Gift” the default selection (as seen in the treatment version), the hypothesis was that donors would be more likely to choose that option simply because it required no extra clicks or decisions to get there.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:
1. Defaulting to the monthly tab significantly increased the Transactions - Monthly for All Traffic by 1,001.5% with a 100.0% level of confidence. This indicates that making monthly giving the default option can effectively boost the number of recurring donors.
2. The treatment version, which defaulted to the monthly giving option, also saw a 20% increase in overall transactions compared to the control version with a 68.7% level of confidence. This indicates that defaulting to monthly first had no negative effect on one-time giving.
The substantial increase in recurring conversion rates supports the hypothesis that defaulting to the monthly tab can influence donor behavior and encourage sustained giving.

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that directly asking for a monthly gift in the Killing Sin Premium Donation Page copy, combined with a donation form that defaults to monthly giving, will increase the number of recurring gifts by reducing decision ambiguity, framing monthly giving as the expected norm, and reinforcing the value of sustained impact.

For this test we wanted to see if we can position the donation page on one of their premiums to be monthly focused copy and defaulting the form to monthly, making it the standard choice for users who land on the page.

experiments.title: How a slideup with a gift array selection vs a stickybar affects monthly donations for a campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding in a slideup with gift array for website visitors will achieve an increase in donations because that slideup with the gift array would lead them directly to the donation page with their gift pre-filled, hence reducing a good amount of friction.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/17/2025 - 02/28/2025
Time of Grace had their recurring campaign in the last two weeks of February. We wanted to test how a slideup with a gift array selection vs a stickybar would affect the donor conversion rate. The language on the sticky bar and the slideup was the same, so the elements of the sticky bar and slideup were being tested against each other.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings from the Experiment:

1. The addition of a slideup with a gift array selection significantly increased the Recurring Donation for All Traffic by 122.7% with a high level of confidence (96.0%). This indicates that the slideup with the gift array was more effective in encouraging donations compared to the sticky bar.

Recommendations for Future Experimentation:

1. Consider the trade-off between increased donations and user engagement when implementing slideups with gift arrays. It may be beneficial to test different variations to find the optimal balance between the two.

2. Use the learnings from this experiment to inform future strategies for maximizing donations while maintaining user engagement. This could involve testing different placements, designs, or messaging to find the most effective approach.

3. Consider conducting follow-up experiments to further refine the approach and iterate on the findings from this experiment. This will help in continuously optimizing the donation process and improving overall performance.

Overall, the key takeaway is to balance the trade-off between increasing donations and maintaining user engagement when implementing similar strategies in the future. The success of the slideup with gift array highlights the importance of reducing friction in the donation process, but it should be done in a way that complements overall website engagement.

experiments.title: How does defaulting appeal-language to a monthly ask impact overall transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting to a monthly ask for users receiving the premium appeal will achieve an increase in recurring gifts without decreasing overall transactions because it prioritizes recurring, while still giving the opportunity to make a one-time gift if they choose..
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/28/2025
Every month, Time of Grace shares an opportunity to receive the latest monthly book offer as a thank-you for a gift. However, donors also have the ability to request the monthly resource every single month when they begin a recurring gift. Each month, this ask neatly lends itself to a recurring ask. We hypothesized that defaulting the appeal language to a monthly gift, while still allowing the donor to make a one-time gift if they choose, will increase recurring gifts while not negatively impacting overall transactions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment was responsible for the only 2 recurring gifts received from this appeal, however, it was still below the level needed for statistical validity. Additionally, the experiment disproved our hypothesis that defaulting to monthly would not negatively impact overall transactions. We saw a 64% decrease in transactions with a 96% level of confidence. 

In terms of next steps, this experiment provides us with key pieces of data for actions items:

Users will make a recurring gift to the monthly premium if they are asked. This alone is valuable. Typically, Time of Grace only presents a Grace Partner ask to users during the annual high-urgency push for monthly donors. However, the two gifts here indicate that there is an opportunity to highlight the monthly ask and perks in the standard premium appeal that takes place every month. However, composing the email around the Grace Partner ask has a decrease in transactions overall that outweighs the gain. Which leads us to the second action item.

Time of Grace should explore a way of prioritizing monthly that isn't so intrusive to the experience of donors who would've made a one-time ask. Defaulting to a Grace Partner ask could cause donors to exclude themselves: The ministry is asking for monthly. If I can't give monthly, then maybe I shouldn't give at all. An opportunity for further testing could be focusing our efforts on the donation page. Keeping standard language in email and on the donation page, but prioritizing monthly or the premium-related perks when selecting gift frequency. This will ensure we don't discourage clicks and traffic overall at the top of funnnel, while still highlighting monthly for those who would be inclined to start a recurring gift.

experiments.title: How not including the monthly donation program name and description on a donation page affects the conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Not adding the monthly donation name and description on a donation page will increase the conversion rate because the name of the program takes more description to explain than it does to say “become a monthly donor.”
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/15/2025
Mountain States Legal Foundation protects property rights and economic liberty, defends the right to keep and bear arms, champions free speech and association, and upholds the principle of equal protection.
We ran this test on a donation page form connected to a high-urgency email asking supporters to become monthly donors. Audience: MSLF email house file.
Our goal was to determine whether including the monthly donation program name and description would lead to more people becoming monthly donors, or if simply asking them to give monthly—without mentioning the program name—would be just as effective.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that not adding the monthly donation program name and description on a donation page led to a significant increase in donations. The treatment group, which did not include the program name and description, saw a 366.7% increase in donations compared to the control group. This suggests that simplifying the donation ask to just "become a monthly donor" without additional information about the program may be more effective in encouraging supporters to donate.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the impact of adding extra information or details to donation appeals. This experiment indicates that a more concise and straightforward donation ask could lead to higher conversion rates. It may be beneficial to continue testing different messaging approaches to see what resonates best with supporters and drives the most donations. Additionally, the results of this experiment can help guide future communication strategies and donation page designs to optimize for conversions and incentivize supporters to become monthly donors.

experiments.summary:

The Heritage Foundation is running their annual President’s Match Challenge campaign to members and non-members of Heritage. In this experiment, they wanted to test how defaulting to the monthly tab on the acquisition donation page of an ebook offer as apart of the campaign would affect the rate of recurring giving.
50% of traffic was randomly assigned to each variation (Control vs Treatment). The primary goal was to determine whether defaulting the donation format to monthly giving (rather than one-time giving) would influence the recurring conversion rate.
The idea to run this test stemmed from two core insights:
Behavioral Economics Research:
People tend to go with the default option presented to them. When monthly giving is presented as the default tab, it subtly frames that behavior as the norm or recommended path.
Organizational Goal: Boost Recurring Revenue:
Like many nonprofits, Heritage is invested in growing their base of recurring donors. Monthly gifts provide more predictable, long-term revenue.
By making “Make a Monthly Gift” the default selection (as seen in the treatment version), the hypothesis was that donors would be more likely to choose that option simply because it required no extra clicks or decisions to get there.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that defaulting the donation format to monthly giving can significantly influence the rate of recurring conversion. In this case, defaulting to the monthly tab led to a 1.6% increase in Transactions - Monthly for all traffic with a high level of statistical confidence.
In future experimentation, we should continue to leverage the insights from behavioral economics research and organizational goals to design and implement tests that capitalize on the tendency for people to go with the default option presented to them. By defaulting to monthly giving, we can subtly frame this behavior as the recommended path and encourage sustained giving through visual anchoring.
Additionally, we can use the learnings from this experiment to inform future campaign strategies and donation page designs. By understanding the impact of default options on donor behavior, we can optimize our fundraising efforts to maximize recurring revenue and overall donor engagement.

experiments.title: How defaulting the monthly tab on an instant donation page affects recurring conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting to the monthly tab for potential donors will achieve an increase in recurring conversion rates because  it reduces friction by aligning with the donor’s default behavior, subtly encourages sustained giving through visual anchoring, and reframes the donation as an ongoing commitment rather than a one-time gift. because  .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/19/2025 - 07/18/2025
This experiment was conducted as a split test on the instant donation page immediately following the Heritage Foundation’s Vote of Confidence offer. The test was live for one month, with 50% of traffic randomly assigned to each variation (control vs. treatment). The primary goal was to determine whether defaulting the donation format to monthly giving (rather than one-time giving) would influence the recurring conversion rate.
This experiment is a classic behavioral nudge test—leveraging default bias by changing which giving tab a donor sees first.
The idea to run this test stemmed from two core insights:
Behavioral Economics Research:
People tend to go with the default option presented to them. When monthly giving is presented as the default tab, it subtly frames that behavior as the norm or recommended path.
Organizational Goal: Boost Recurring Revenue:
Like many nonprofits, Heritage is invested in growing their base of recurring donors. Monthly gifts provide more predictable, long-term revenue.
While past tests may have encouraged recurring giving through messaging or pre-selecting a checkbox, this test directly adjusted the default donation modality itself.
By making “Make a Monthly Gift” the default selection (as seen in the treatment version), the hypothesis was that donors would be more likely to choose that option simply because it required no extra clicks or decisions to get there.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment suggest that defaulting to the monthly giving tab can significantly impact donor behavior and increase recurring conversion rates. By aligning with the donor's default behavior, visually anchoring the idea of sustained giving, and reframing the donation as an ongoing commitment, the treatment version achieved a 0.9% increase in Transactions - Monthly for All Traffic with a 99.9% statistical level of confidence.
In future experimentation, these learnings should be considered to optimize donation pages and increase recurring revenue for the Heritage Foundation:
1. Default to Monthly Giving: Based on the success of defaulting to the monthly giving tab in this experiment, consider implementing this strategy on other donation pages or communication materials. This can help nudge donors towards becoming recurring givers.
2. Visual Anchoring: Use visual cues and language that emphasize the benefits of sustained giving. Highlighting the impact of monthly donations and showcasing the value of recurring contributions can further encourage donors to choose the monthly giving option.
3. A/B Testing: Continuously test different strategies, messaging, and default settings to identify what resonates best with donors. A/B testing allows for data-driven decision-making and can uncover new insights to optimize donation processes.
4. Donor-Centric Approach: Consider donor preferences and behaviors when designing donation pages. Making the giving process convenient, personalized, and aligned with donor values can increase engagement and support.
Overall, the success of this experiment highlights the importance of behavioral nudges and strategic design in influencing donor decisions. By leveraging default bias and emphasizing the benefits of recurring giving, nonprofits can potentially increase their base of recurring donors and secure more predictable, long-term revenue.

experiments.title: How defaulting donation form to recurring impacts recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting the donation page for Bible Direct Ask donation page visitors to recurring will achieve an increase in recurring donations because it reduces decision fatigue and makes it feel like the expected choice, encouraging donors to follow through with the default option.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/11/2025 - 06/27/2025
In an effort to generate more recurring donors for IMB, we opted to default their Bible Direct Ask donation form to recurring transactions for 50% of traffic.
The only change made on the treatment was the form default.
This test ran for about 2 weeks.
We anticipated seeing some level of increase in recurring gifts, but our goal was to also not see a large drop off in overall transactions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are: Defaulting the donation page to recurring for Bible Direct Ask donation page visitors led to a significant increase in recurring donations by 647.9% at a valid 99.8% level of confidence. These results indicate that defaulting to recurring on a donation form will, in fact, lead to more recurring gifts on this funnel.

We did see a 14.5% decrease in overall transactions at a 53% LOC, but ultimately, a recurring donor is much more valuable than an episodic donor; therefore, the overall trade-off is worth the cost.

We kept the copy and value proposition consistent between the control and treatment to test how the form itself impacted recurring gifts, but in future experimentation, we plan to test using recurring language on the donation page along with a recurring defaulted form.

experiments.title: How defaulting the monthly tab on an instant donation page affects recurring conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting to the monthly tab for survey takers will achieve an increase in recurring conversion rates because it reduces friction by aligning with the donor’s default behavior, subtly encourages sustained giving through visual anchoring, and reframes the donation as an ongoing commitment rather than a one-time gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/01/2025 - 07/18/2025
This experiment was conducted as a split test on the instant donation page immediately following the Heritage Foundation’s ICE survey. The test was live for three weeks, with 50% of traffic randomly assigned to each variation (control vs. treatment). The primary goal was to determine whether defaulting the donation format to monthly giving (rather than one-time giving) would influence the recurring conversion rate.
This experiment is a classic behavioral nudge test—leveraging default bias by changing which giving tab a donor sees first.
The idea to run this test stemmed from two core insights:
Behavioral Economics Research:
People tend to go with the default option presented to them. When monthly giving is presented as the default tab, it subtly frames that behavior as the norm or recommended path.
Organizational Goal: Boost Recurring Revenue:
Like many nonprofits, Heritage is invested in growing their base of recurring donors. Monthly gifts provide more predictable, long-term revenue.
While past tests may have encouraged recurring giving through messaging or pre-selecting a checkbox, this test directly adjusted the default donation modality itself.
By making “Make a Monthly Gift” the default selection (as seen in the treatment version), the hypothesis was that donors would be more likely to choose that option simply because it required no extra clicks or decisions to get there.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that defaulting to the monthly donation tab significantly increased the conversion rate for recurring donations. By setting "Make a Monthly Gift" as the default option, donors were more likely to choose this option, leading to a 795.9% increase in Transactions - Monthly for All Traffic with a 98.9% level of confidence.
Moreover, the treatment also resulted in a 671% increase in total Transactions with a 97% confidence level and a 149.7% increase in Revenue with a 100% level of confidence. This showcases the impact of nudging donors towards monthly giving by adjusting the default option.
In future experimentation, this learning can be applied by defaulting to the monthly donation tab on the donation page to continue encouraging recurring giving. Additionally, further tests can be conducted to optimize the donation page layout, test different messaging strategies, or explore other behavioral nudges to increase donor engagement and drive more sustainable revenue for the organization.

experiments.title: The impact of a monthly gift array pushdown on recurring gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a recurring gift pushdown for visitors to the homepage of Heritage.org will achieve a lift in monthly gifts to The Heritage Foundation because of the prioritization of recurring giving and the supporting copy emphasizing the need for sustained support.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/28/2025 - 06/01/2025
The Heritage membership team tested a monthly gift pushdown on the homepage of heritage.org. The test was visible to all desktop users on every visit and was run during the last 3 days of the May Leaders Club campaign. The pushdown emphasized the need for fearless patriots to help sustain the mission of the Heritage Foundation before time ran out. It featured 3 clickable buttons with suggested ask amounts and 1 button with “other”. After clicking the user was redirected to a landing page with only a recurring  gift option. In conjunction with the test, a typical campaign banner add was featured below also asking the user to activate their Leaders Club membership.
The test ran as a 20/80 split because of time restraints, with the control receiving 20% of the traffic and the treatment receiving 80%. Overall there was no difference in the number of transactions, but the treatment received 7 monthly gifts while the control received none. Although if the test had been allowed to run longer and the control had received a monthly gift, than these results would not be valid.
Further testing, for a longer period of time is suggested.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:
1. Adding a recurring gift pushdown to the homepage of Heritage.org resulted in a 0.1% increase in monthly gifts to The Heritage Foundation.
2. The treatment version, which emphasized the need for sustained support through recurring giving, outperformed the control version.
3. The treatment version had a statistically significant increase in monthly gifts with a 99.2% level of confidence.
4. The treatment received 7 monthly gifts while the control received none, indicating that the recurring gift pushdown was effective in prompting donors to choose monthly giving options.
In future experimentation, we should:
1. Continue to prioritize recurring giving options on the homepage or key landing pages to encourage sustained support.
2. Utilize language that emphasizes the impact of recurring gifts and the urgent need for support in order to motivate donors to take action.
3. Consider running tests for longer periods to gather more data and ensure the validity of results, especially when looking to compare against a control group that may generate unexpected outcomes given more time.
4. Explore additional elements such as different suggested ask amounts or variations in the messaging to further optimize the performance of recurring gift pushdowns.
Overall, the success of this experiment highlights the effectiveness of emphasizing recurring giving options and the importance of crafting compelling copy that resonates with donors.

How to Use Learnings in Future Experimentation:

2. Continue to test different types of callout language and visual cues to see which ones resonate most with donors.
3. Conduct further experiments to understand the specific elements within the callout that were most effective in driving donations.
4. Apply these learnings to other areas of the website or marketing materials to see if similar tactics can be successful in different contexts.
Overall, the success of this experiment highlights the importance of creative and strategic elements in donation pages to motivate and inspire donors to contribute to the cause.

experiments.keyLearnings:

This demonstrates that simplifying the ask to "become a monthly donor" without overcomplicating it with additional program details can be more effective in driving engagement from supporters.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing different variations of messaging to see what resonates best with supporters. This could include testing different language, formatting, and call-to-action strategies to optimize click-through rates and ultimately increase monthly donors. Additionally, applying the principle of simplicity and clarity in messaging could continue to be a successful strategy in driving action from supporters.

experiments.title: How defaulting to a recurring gift on an acquisition donation page, Living with Contagious Joy, impacts recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting to a recurring gift and emphasizing its broader impact for the donors will increase the number of recurring donations because it presents a manageable commitment and highlights the significance of ongoing support, encouraging more donors to commit to long-term giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/25/2025 - 04/10/2025
To increase recurring donations, we tested defaulting to a recurring gift option on the donation page for the high-traffic “Living with Contagious Joy” acquisition funnel, an ideal setting to measure the impact of this default on donor conversion.
We hypothesized that defaulting to a recurring gift option and emphasizing the impact of monthly donations would increase the number of donors choosing recurring contributions, ultimately boosting donor retention and long-term support for the mission.
For this iteration, we configured a VWO Split URL test with a new control and a treatment page, each receiving 50% of traffic. This setup ensured users blocking VWO redirects were excluded, preventing data distortion in the control group and yielding more accurate results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for 17 days and tested the impact of defaulting to a recurring gift option on the donation page within the “Living with Contagious Joy” acquisition funnel.

While the treatment successfully increased recurring transactions by 313.8% with a 98.2% level of confidence, it also led to a 45.2% decrease in one-time donations, resulting in an overall 33% drop in total transactions. This indicates that although more donors opted for monthly giving when it was pre-selected, the change significantly discouraged one-time donations.

Given these results, the test highlights a valuable trade-off: optimizing for recurring giving can improve long-term donor value, but may reduce total donor acquisition in the short term. Future testing could explore ways to elevate the visibility and value of monthly giving —such as using stronger messaging, layout hierarchy, or design emphasis. Additionally, tracking the retention and lifetime value of these newly acquired monthly donors will be critical to understanding if the short-term loss in total transactions is outweighed by long-term donor value.

experiments.title: Does adding a monthly donate button in the main navigation impact conversion rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a second monthly donate button for potential donors will achieve an increase in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/17/2024 - 02/06/2024
In this test we aimed to increase the overall conversion rate by introducing two separate donation buttons, leading to distinct forms for one-time and recurring donations. We hypothesized that users would be more inclined to donate if they received a more explicit message about the available donation options, reducing ambiguity. Additionally, pre-determining the type of donation before entering the donation form would minimize friction during the process.
Our main hypothesis was that including an additional button specifically for recurring donations, in addition to the standard red donate button, is likely to have a positive impact on our overall conversion rate. Not only will it reduce the number of cancellations after the first donation, but it will also make the donation process more convenient for potential donors who prefer to make recurring contributions. This will ultimately lead to increased qualified donors and a higher conversion rate.
In this experiment we had 2 treatment variants; the first had a ‘donate’ button that led to the default combo form (one-time default), while the ‘give monthly’ button led to the same form, but with monthly pre-selected.
The second treatment had the same functionality for the ‘donate’ button, but the ‘give monthly’ button linked to a form that only offered recurring giving as the main option (there was a secondary link the form to give one-time).
experiments.keyLearnings:
Data analysis revealed that both variants substantially increased overall revenue and conversion rates. The improvements were primarily observed in desktop users, indicating that the changes implemented significantly impacted this segment. Significant results from this segment were:
Variant B:
-       +45% statistically significant lift (98%) in desktop one-time conversion rate.
-       +126% statistically significant lift (99%) in desktop one-time RPV.
-       +118% statistically significant lift (96%) in desktop recurring conversion rate.
Variant C:
-       +42% statistically significant lift (98%) in desktop one-time conversion rate.
-       +92% statistically significant lift (99%) in desktop one-time RPV.
-       +146% statistically significant lift (98%) in desktop recurring conversion rate.
These results support our hypotheses that (1) Offering users two distinct donation buttons, leading to separate forms for one-time and recurring donations, can increase the conversion rate. (2) A more explicit message about the available donation options, with less ambiguity, can further improve the likelihood of user conversion. (3) Pre-determining the type of donation before entering the form may also reduce friction during the process.

experiments.title: How defaulting to recurring gift on a premium offer donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting to a recurring gift and emphasizing its broader impact for FamilyLife donors will increase the number of recurring donations because it presents a more manageable commitment and highlights the significance of ongoing support, encouraging more donors to commit to long-term giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/14/2025 - 03/14/2025
FamilyLife is a Christian organization focused on strengthening marriages and families by providing resources, events, and guidance rooted in biblical principles, helping people navigate the challenges of family life, and fostering lasting, healthy relationships. In this experiment, we aimed to test whether defaulting to a recurring gift option and emphasizing its impact would increase recurring donations.
The test involved two variations on the donation page. The Control version defaulted to a one-time gift of $50 and included a call-out stating, “Equip 39 families with practical insights to strengthen the relationships that matter most.” Conversely, the Treatment defaulted to a $25 recurring gift and featured a more compelling call-out: “Equip 233 families with practical insights to strengthen the relationships that matter most.” This A/B test was split 50/50 and was tested on Seeing Beautiful Again, one of FamilyLife’s best performing offers.
We hypothesize that by highlighting the broader impact of recurring gifts and making it the default option, we would see an increase in the number of donors choosing to make recurring contributions, ultimately boosting donor retention and long-term support for FamilyLife’s mission.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Through this retest, we observed that defaulting to a recurring gift option once again led to an increase in recurring donations. The Treatment group, which featured recurring giving as the default, generated 16 recurring gifts compared to 0 in the Control group. This resulted in a 0.92% recurring conversion rate, validated by a 99.9% level of confidence—providing strong evidence that this approach effectively encourages more donors to commit to ongoing support.
When looking at immediate revenue, there was little difference between the two groups. The Control group generated $902 in revenue, while the Treatment brought in $1,017—a modest increase that is not statistically significant. However, while short-term revenue remained stable, the shift toward recurring gifts has the potential to yield much greater long-term value.
Although overall transactions were slightly lower in the Treatment group (93 gifts) compared to the Control (105 gifts), this was observed at a 66.8% level of confidence. This outcome is to be expected, as introducing a default recurring gift option may cause some one-time donors to reconsider their gift in the moment. However, those who do proceed are more likely to become committed, long-term supporters.
Next Steps:
We recommend that FamilyLife continue to default to a recurring gift on their donation pages. This should be a permanent update to their microsite.Continuing to test and optimize recurring donation strategies: Based on the success of this experiment, it is essential to explore and refine different approaches to promoting recurring gifts. Testing variations in messaging, default options, and impact statements can help further increase recurring donations.Monitor Long-Term Performance: Track the performance of recurring donors to assess their impact on retention and overall organizational goals.

experiments.title: How defaulting to a recurring gift on an acquisition donation page, Proof You Need, impacts recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting to a recurring gift and emphasizing its broader impact for Focus on the Family donors will increase the number of recurring donations because it presents a manageable commitment and highlights the significance of ongoing support, encouraging more donors to commit to long-term giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/05/2025 - 03/05/2025
Focus on the Family is a nonprofit organization dedicated to providing guidance and resources to support families. To increase recurring donations, we tested defaulting to a recurring gift option on the donation page for the high-traffic “Proof You Need” acquisition funnel, an ideal setting to measure the impact of this default on donor conversion.
We hypothesized that defaulting to a recurring gift option and emphasizing the impact of monthly donations would increase the number of donors choosing recurring contributions, ultimately boosting donor retention and long-term support for Focus on the Family’s mission.
This experiment was previously tested from October 16 – November 12, with the original test defaulting to a $15 recurring gift. The results showed that defaulting to recurring significantly increased recurring donations—the treatment group, which defaulted to recurring, had 34 conversions compared to just 8 in the control group, marking a 330.0% increase in recurring donations at a 99.9% confidence level. Based on these results, we initially moved forward with defaulting other pages to recurring gifts. However, as we continued monitoring performance, we observed lower ROIs and encountered limitations in the donation form’s gift array, which restricted customization for one-time and monthly gift amounts. As a result, we ultimately reverted the default back to a one-time gift.
Towards the end of the year, we saw a noticeable increase in sustainer giving, prompting us to revisit this test.
For this iteration, we configured a VWO Split URL test with a new control and a variation, each receiving 50% of traffic. This setup ensured users blocking VWO redirects were excluded, preventing data distortion in the control group and yielding more accurate results.
Control: Defaulted to a one-time gift, with language asking for a one-time donation. The gift array included “$30, $50, $100, and $200,” with no pre-selected amount. The copy specifically asked for $30, while $50 was highlighted as a “popular” gift option. Note: No changes were made to the current live pages for Proof You Need for the control group.
Treatment: Defaulted to a recurring gift, with a gift array of “$15, $30, $50, and $100,” with $30 pre-selected.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Through this experiment, we observed that defaulting to a recurring gift option significantly increased recurring donations. The treatment group, which defaulted to recurring gifts, had 17 more recurring transactions than the control, resulting in an 830.7% increase in recurring transactions at a 100% confidence level.
However, overall transactions decreased slightly, with the treatment group converting 122 donations versus 141 in the control group. Revenue for the control was $5,520, while the treatment generated $4,470. Despite this initial decline in immediate revenue, the additional 17 recurring donors in the treatment group, with an average gift of $30 and an 85% retention rate, project to generate an additional $5,202 in revenue over time—demonstrating the long-term value of sustaining donors.
The decrease in overall transactions is likely due to the shift from one-time gifts to recurring donations. While immediate revenue per visitor declined, this result aligns with previous findings that indicate the long-term value of recurring donors outweighs short-term losses.
Next Steps
Default to Recurring Gifts for This Offer: Given the strong lift in recurring transactions and projected long-term revenue, we will permanently default this offer to recurring gifts.Test on Another Offer (Living with Contagious Joy): To further validate these findings, we will run a similar test on another offer, "Living with Contagious Joy," to assess whether the impact holds across different campaigns.Refine Messaging: Continue optimizing the way we present the recurring gift option to further encourage commitment while minimizing any deterrent effect on one-time donations.Monitor Long-Term Donor Impact: Track retention and lifetime value of these new recurring donors to ensure the strategy continues to drive sustainable growth for Focus on the Family.

experiments.title: Evaluating the Impact of Adding a One-Time Gift Option to a Recurring Gift Slide-Up on Transaction Volume
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a one-time gift option to the slide-up will lead to more transactions overall, while maintaining a similar number of sustaining gifts. This approach is designed to attract individuals who may not be ready to commit to a sustaining gift, while still capturing those who were already inclined to make a sustaining contribution.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/08/2025 - 02/14/2025
This experiment was conducted for WUNC, a public radio station serving North Carolina, during their February Fund Drive. We aimed to test two different slide-ups: one was a previous version that encouraged prospects to make a sustaining gift, while in the test version, we added an option for a one-time gift to see if it would result in more overall gifts, including sustaining contributions.
Everything else remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In conclusion, the results of this experiment indicate that the control version, which emphasized monthly sustaining donations, outperformed the treatment version, which included both one-time and sustaining gift options. The control version resulted in 53 gifts generating $1,510 in revenue, compared to the treatment version's 23 gifts and $595 in revenue.
Breaking down the results further:
The control version had 15 one-time gifts and 38 recurring gifts.The treatment version had 6 one-time gifts and 17 recurring gifts.The control version also saw more first-time gifts, with 35 first-time gifts compared to the treatment's 13.
A key finding was that the control version resulted in a 58% higher donation volume for all traffic compared to the treatment version, with 100% statistical confidence. This suggests that focusing solely on sustaining donations was more effective in driving contributions, as the addition of a one-time gift option did not increase the total number of donations.
Additionally, four sustainers made another one-time donation, and all of them donated through the control version.
Moving forward, it would be beneficial to continue emphasizing the importance and impact of monthly sustaining gifts, as this approach appears to resonate more effectively with prospects. Further testing could explore different messaging strategies and donation options to identify the most effective combination for future fundraising campaigns. These insights will be key to optimizing WUNC's approach to maximizing donations in the future.

experiments.title: How does adding an annual recurring option affect donor conversion rate for prospects?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a tab for an annual recurring  gift for prospects will achieve a lift in annual recurring gifts without affecting the donor conversion rate because members value consistency and the option to set and forget a gift is appealing to some donors. because people value consistency and the option to set and forget a gift is appealing to some donors.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/19/2025
The Heritage membership team tested adding a third Annual Recurring Gift tab to the donation form during their Founders Day campaign. The test went to a nonmember prospect audience and featured 3 emails. The emails and the landing pages contained variable copy for the “Annual recurring gift” treatment asking prospects to “activate your annual recurring gift to The Heritage Foundation”.
Results showed a large decrease in overall transactions but a substantial increase in monthly and annual recurring giving. There was a 451% increase in monthly gifts from prospects who received the annual gift treatment, with the control receiving 2 and the treatment receiving 11. In addition, 18 of the total 32 gifts received on the treatment were an annual recurring gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
1. Adding a tab for an annual recurring gift significantly increased annual and monthly recurring giving from nonmember prospects. This shows that providing the option for donors to set up automatic, recurring donations is appealing and can lead to a substantial increase in long-term giving.
2. Despite the increase in annual and monthly recurring gifts, the overall number of donations decreased by 51.1% for the treatment group. This suggests that some donors may have been deterred by the additional option, impacting the total number of transactions.
3. The control (without the annual recurring gift tab) outperformed the treatment in terms of donations for all traffic, indicating that for this particular audience, the traditional donation form without the additional tab was more effective.
In future experimentation, it is important to consider the trade-off between increasing recurring giving and potentially decreasing the total number of donations. It may be beneficial to conduct further research to understand which audience segments are most receptive to the annual recurring gift option and tailor the donation form accordingly. Additionally, it would be valuable to test different messaging and placement of the annual recurring gift tab to optimize its impact on giving without negatively affecting overall conversion rates.

experiments.title: How defaulting to a recurring gift on an acquisition donation page, Proof You Need, impacts recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting to a recurring gift and emphasizing its broader impact for Focus on the Family donors will increase the number of recurring donations because it presents a manageable commitment and highlights the significance of ongoing support, encouraging more donors to commit to long-term giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/16/2024 - 11/12/2024
Focus on the Family is a nonprofit organization dedicated to providing guidance and resources to support families. To increase recurring donations, we tested defaulting to a recurring gift option on the donation page for the high-traffic “Proof You Need” acquisition funnel, an ideal setting to measure the impact of this default on donor conversion.
We hypothesize that defaulting to a recurring gift option and emphasizing the impact of monthly donations will increase the number of donors choosing recurring contributions, ultimately boosting donor retention and long-term support for Focus on the Family’s mission.
Due to technical constraints, we set this up as a split URL test. Following our development team’s guidance, we configured a VWO Split URL test with a new control and a variation, each receiving 50% of traffic, while the original control received 0%. This configuration allowed us to filter out users blocking VWO redirects, ensuring they didn’t affect the main control group and were excluded from results, yielding more accurate data for redirected pages.
In this test, the control page defaulted to a one-time gift, with language asking for a one-time donation. The treatment page defaulted to a recurring monthly gift of $15, with the gift array adjusted to include $15, $30, $50, and $100, with $15 pre-selected. The treatment page was adjusted to ask for a monthly gift.
We ran a similar test on another acquisition donation page, “Reconnected,” as a separate experiment using the same setup to compare results across different offers to determine if the type of offer would impact the results. You can review the results of that concurrent experiment here.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Through this experiment, we observed that defaulting to a recurring gift option significantly increased recurring donations. The treatment group, which defaulted to recurring gifts, had 34 conversions compared to just 8 in the control group, marking a 330.0% increase in recurring donations at a 99.9% confidence level. However, overall transactions decreased by 15.1%, with the treatment group converting 157 donations versus 187 in the control group. Additionally, revenue per visitor dropped by 22.4%, but this result only reached a 5.3% level of confidence, indicating that the difference is not statistically significant and suggesting no substantial negative impact on immediate revenue.
The decrease in overall transactions is likely due to the shift from one-time gifts to recurring donations. While the average gift size was slightly lower in the treatment group ($31.16 vs. $33.76), recurring donors provide more sustainable support over time. The long-term value of recurring donations, as seen in similar experiments, outweighs the short-term decrease in revenue.
Next Steps:
Default to Recurring Gifts: We recommend that Focus on the Family default to recurring gifts on all acquisition donation pages to drive long-term donor retention and sustainable support.Refine Messaging: Continue testing variations of messaging that emphasize the broader impact of recurring donations to further encourage donor commitment.Optimize Recurring Gift Amounts: Experiment with different recurring gift amounts to determine which options are most attractive to donors and maximize recurring revenue.Monitor Long-Term Donor Impact: Track the performance of recurring donors to assess their long-term retention and contribution to Focus on the Family’s mission and goals.
