experiments.title: How a Clear Call to Action Impacted Monthly Donor Conversion on a Donation Page

rows.name: Clear 365 Days a Year Value Proposition

experiments.researchQuestion: Will stripped down value proposition with a clear impact CTA increase the number of monthly donors acquired on our donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/16/2019 - 08/13/2019
For several years, Care Net has used the same value propositional language for our monthly donor acquisition page.  Every January, we would send out a series of appeals seeking to raise new monthly donors, donors we called “Caring Partners.” As we prepared for the January 2019 campaign, we revisited the donation page and realized that the copy was full of organizational language that may add friction. We wondered if anyone really wanted to become a “caring partner” or even knew what that phrase meant. We also saw that we had copy dedicated to selling a list of benefits to becoming a monthly donor and wondered if those benefits really mattered. Did people become monthly donors because they wanted a “dedicated Care Net team member to serve them?” We stripped out these sections of the page and radically redesigned the copy. We boiled down the value proposition into one key statement: Save babies 365 days a year by becoming a monthly donor. We kept the copy focused on the donor and the impact of their gift of $1/day. We linked it to a concrete number of lives saved by monthly donors since 2008. We broke out the impact of the monthly gift using bullet points. We did not set up a straight AB test, however we had historic data from the page we used the prior campaign that we could use to compare to the new redesigned page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new page performed far and above the old one. It was so successful, that we kept using it in a wide variety of campaign offers which is why the traffic was so much higher than the control page. It is important to note that the variation had a conversion rate around 15% during the January campaign, the lower conversion rate reflects the average of all visitors to the page between Jan-August.
Few people are excited about becoming a monthly donor because of "benefits" Care Net provides. They want to save babies 365 days a year. This new value proposition was clearer and more appealing.

				gift).

0% increase in traffic
				× 51.0% increase in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How a recurring gift prompt at fiscal year end impacted conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a popup shown at the point of conversion increase recurring giving acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/11/2019 - 07/31/2019
As a means to increase membership to Hillsdale’s Liberty and Learning Society (recurring giving club) we decided to launch an experiment where visitors making a one-time donation would be prompted with a popup after hitting “Submit.” This popup would encourage them to convert their one time gift to a recurring gift in order to have a greater impact and to receive a few of the benefits.
This experiment was launched solely on the pay per click primary donation page to minimize the potential negative impact that could come from the increased friction. Any donor making a gift above $500 would also be excluded from seeing the popup.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for nearly two months, we saw that the recurring giving prompt resulted in a decrease in overall conversion of 16%. It also didn't significantly impact recurring giving either positively or negatively. What made this surprising is that this same experiment had seen 30% increase in recurring giving with no negative impact on one-time gifts with a similar organization. Based upon this observation, we spent a significant amount of time analyzing the results.
During our analysis, we discovered that the recurring gift popup exhibited two different behaviors at the beginning and end of the experiment. Specifically, when we compared June to July, we found that June saw the decrease in conversion while July saw no significant impact. We also saw a slight boost in recurring giving in July.
It would appear that the urgency that comes with a fiscal year end increased the perceived friction of this experiment. During times of lower urgency, like July, the popup seemed to have the intended effect of increasing recurring giving. To further validate this hypothesis, we will relaunch the experiment and expand the pages targeted to shorten the testing cycle.

				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:
0% increase in traffic
				× 16.4% decrease in conversion rate

experiments.title: How a monthly first ask affects one-time and recurring donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will presenting a monthly-first donation form with a one-time fallback option increase recurring donations or affect one-time revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/21/2019 - 07/06/2019
Leadership Institute had begun to ramp up their recurring donation program. They were curious to see if featuring the monthly giving option through a tabbed donation widget on their main donation page would increase recurring donations without affecting one-time donations and revenue. They wanted to monitor two metrics: overall donor conversion (with revenue) and monthly recurring donations generated.
experiments.keyLearnings: The tabbed, monthly-first donation form produced more than twice as many recurring donations as the one-time form with the option to make the gift monthly. Most importantly, the new form had no discernible effect on conversion, which meant that they did not dissuade donors who wanted to make a one-time gift from giving that gift. This appears to have created a user interface that prioritizes recurring giving while avoiding donor confusion, which is a big win in terms of long term revenue for LI.

experiments.title: How presenting the opportunity to make a recurring gift at the moment of a person’s one-time gift transaction affects recurring gift conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: Could we increase recurring donations by offering this as an option at the moment of a person’s transaction?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/10/2018 - 05/10/2018
On Focus on the Family’s main donation page, we hypothesized that we might be able to increase revenue and the number of recurring gifts by offering people the opportunity to turn their one-time gift into a recurring gift right at the moment of the transaction. To do this, we created a pop-up that appeared right after a person clicked on the “Make a Donation” button and before the gift was processed that asked them if they would like to make their donation a recurring gift instead. The pop-up had value proposition on it for why they should become a recurring donor and lowered the gift amount to be 60% of what their original one-time gift was. A person would either click, “No, make my original gift” or “Yes, make my gift recurring”.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment increased recurring gifts by 64%. What this tells us is that people are willing to make a recurring gift if we clearly communicate the even greater impact they can have through their gift and give them the opportunity to do it. When analyzing all data associated with this experiment we found that: 1. It did not significantly impact the likelihood to give a gift. 2. We were able to convert over 2% of donors that would have given a one-time gift into a long-term commitment.

experiments.title: How a recurring gift ask in a popup decreased donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Can a recurring gift popup, targeting single-gift donors, increase recurring donors and dollars, without negatively impacting single gift conversion rates and/or dollars?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/19/2019 - 05/03/2019
Americans for Prosperity was looking for a way to increase recurring gifts to their “Torchbearer” program, so as a part of the strategy to position and promote the program, we helped them install a recurring gift popup that appeared when any donor on any fundraising form chose to give a gift (that was not already marked as a recurring gift).
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although we saw a 68.2% decrease in donor conversions, with a 99.7% level of confidence, we learned a valuable lesson.
That lesson is: Recurring gift popups drastically decreased donor conversion rates when running on acquisition landing page forms. The majority of the difference between the control and treatment segments in the data shown as a part of this experiment was actually produced from those acquisition offer pages.
When removed from the sample, we actually generated a lift in recurring donors through traditional donation form funnels regularly available on the website (which, that single gift alone will cover the revenue difference created between these two segments of this experiment).
It is advised that we do not use recurring gift popup treatments on acquisition pages moving forward. Our next step is to properly isolate the primary donation funnels of the website (less any acquisition forms), then record the experiment again.

experiments.title: How “cost reduction” value proposition affects recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a “cost reduction” value proposition affect recurring donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/17/2019 - 04/23/2019
Hillsdale College sent a chase email as a follow up to a direct mail piece that asked donors to become a recurring donor as a part of their Liberty & Learning Society. The direct mail piece indicated that, as a member of this Society, donors would receive less direct mail and thus help Hillsdale reduce fundraising costs. They wanted to understand the impact of this “cost reduction” value proposition as opposed to a “benefits-focused” value proposition. They crafted two emails—one which included the “cost reduction and reduced direct mail” value proposition, and one which focused on the benefits of joining the LLS.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment, which removed the "cost reduction" value proposition as well as the promise to send less direct mail, produced a 55% decrease in overall revenue due to the combination of a decrease in traffic and a decrease in conversion rate. This shows two important learnings—first, that there may be a desire among donors to help Hillsdale be more efficient and reduce costs, and second, that donors who receive direct mail may have a desire to receive less of it. This learning can be refined in future testing—especially the dynamic between the two.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition copy to a recurring gift donation page impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a radical rewrite of the pledge donation page, emphasizing value proposition, increase pledge conversion?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/15/2018
The Focus on the Family recurring gift donation page had copy on it that was very transaction focused. It had been that way for years. They hypothesized that they might be able to increase conversion if the copy was more value proposition focused rather than transactional.  They developed a treatment that told the reader what their monthly gift would do and test this against their control.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment increased donor conversion by 31.5%. This has a significant impact on revenue since this was on the recurring gift donation page. 

When analyzing the data they did noticed that the average gift for the control ($60.51) was slightly higher than the treatment ($57.66). But because the donor conversion was significantly higher for the treatment, the treatment would still have a greater positive impact on overall revenue than the control. 

This experiment reinforces the idea that value proposition copy is a strong motivating factor to convert donors. When we are able to communicate what a person's gift will do, donors are more likely to give than when told what they will get if they become a donor. 

This experiment is a great start in understanding the motivation behind people becoming a monthly donor. Additional testing will be done to further understand the specific value proposition that motivates people to make a recurring gift as well as how we can increase average gift and optimize overall revenue even more.

experiments.title: How communicating the impact of a specific gift amount impacts conversion on a recurring gift pop-up
experiments.researchQuestion: Will communicating how many babies can be saved through a specific gift amount impact donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/27/2019 - 03/12/2019
Focus on the Family has found great success with their recurring gift pop-up feature on donation pages. During a pro-life campaign, they also found that using a value proposition specific to the particular campaign (pro-life), increased recurring gift conversion as well. To continue to build on those learnings, they hypothesized that communicating the number of babies a person could save with their one-time gift versus an ongoing monthly gift might increase recurring conversion even more.
experiments.keyLearnings:
When the motivating factor of the value proposition went from how a person could save babies from abortion by making a donation to how many babies a person could save with their donation, donor conversion significantly increased by 27%. The treatment copy was dynamic based on a person's gift amount and showed people that by converting their one-time gift into a recurring gift, they would have an even greater impact.
Based on these learnings, can we increase donor conversion even more by visually showing people the impact they can have with their recurring gift using images? We propose testing this approach next.

Focus on the Family had found great success with presenting a popup on their donation pages at the moment of a person’s transaction with an opportunity to turn their one-time gift into a recurring gift. This pop-up had resulted in a 64% increase in recurring gifts on their primary donation page. This has significant revenue opportunities for Focus on the Family. That said, we looked at the pop-up and hypothesized we might be able to increase motivation and conversion even more if we communicated the value proposition around the benefits. To do this, we extended the benefits section of the pop-up to communicate what the specific benefits would do for the end user.

experiments.title: How altruistic language impacts recurring gift conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: Would adding altruistic language to the benefits section increase recurring gift conversion?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/12/2018
In an effort to increase recurring gift conversion on the recurring gift pop-up feature, we hypothesized that adding altruistic language to the benefits section of the copy would increase donor motivation. Instead of just telling people the benefits that they would receive, this copy communicated a greater value that reinforced Focus on the Family’s value proposition and mission. We tested the altruistic version against the original pop-up on all devices and donation pages.
experiments.keyLearnings:
It would appear that the "Altruistic" version has a 21% decrease to recurring gift acquisition. However, after examining the two treatments side by side (Version 1 and Version 2), it may be that we removed too much text when we were trying to simplify the language and not cause the pop-up to be so long.

Our recommendation would be to re-launch this experiment with expanded value proposition but framed in the altruistic manner.

experiments.researchQuestion: Which recurring option (if any) will produce the most recurring donations?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/24/2018
With CaringBridge having little to no recurring donors, and with the majority of donors on file not engaged in recurring giving, we wanted to understand the effect of adding a recurring giving option to the Widget Donation Form. CaringBridge wanted to mitigate the risk of refunds and test into it slowly. They also wanted to test the right way to ask for a monthly recurring gift. Their gift software, by default, gave a two-option choice: one-time (preselected) or monthly. They wanted to test this base option against a binary checkbox ask with a short value proposition.
experiments.keyLearnings: Even though the validity of this experiment is debatable, the relative risk of adding the checkbox, and text, is low... so we decided to go forward with it. In the near future, we will be making other additions to try and further increase the desire for visitors to make a recurring gift.

experiments.title: How rolling out a recurring gift pop-out on all donation pages impacts donor conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: What kind of impact would rolling out the recurring pop-out have on donor conversion across the Focus on the Family website?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/31/2018 - 07/03/2018
Focus on the Family recently launched an experiment on their primary donation page that gave people the opportunity to turn their one-time gift into a recurring gift right at the moment of the transaction. To do this, we created a pop-up that appeared right after a person clicked on the “Make a Donation” button and before the gift was processed that asked them if they would like to make their donation a recurring gift instead. The pop-up had value proposition on it for why they should become a recurring donor and lowered the gift amount to be 60% of what their original one-time gift was. A person would either click, “No, make my original gift” or “Yes, make my gift recurring”. The addition of the pop-up on the primary donation page increased recurring gifts by 64%. We hypothesized that we might be able to increase recurring donors from the other donation pages on the Focus on the Family website as well with this approach. We tested rolling this pop-up approach across their entire site.
experiments.keyLearnings: When we rolled out the recurring gift option pop-up on all donation pages, we were able to increase recurring donor conversion by 24%. What we found was that 16% of all recurring gifts came specifically from the recurring gift pop-up. It is also important to point out that the pop-up didn't significantly impact the likelihood of a person to give a gift.  Pushing this approach to all donation pages will have significant impact on overall revenue for the organization.

experiments.title: How appealing to people as “heroes” can impact conversion for a recurring gift appeal

For Care Net’s recurring gift campaign, they wanted to see if they could increase donor motivation by taking a different approach in their value proposition. Traditionally, the email copy would ask people to give a monthly gift to become a Caring Partner. They developed a treatment for the campaign that portrayed the nondonor as a hero. They tested the two different appeals against each other.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment increased click-through by 184%! By making the case for support to show that the end-reader can be a hero, we are able to motivate more people to click-through to the donation page. It is important to point out that the copy continuously reinforced and strengthened this "hero" message. It is because of that strong and reinforced value proposition that there was such a significant increase in click-rate.

experiments.title: How emphasizing recurring giving impacts overall revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation page treatment will yield the greatest number of donors and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2016 - 12/07/2016
In the latest in the series of optimization experiments on the main Heritage donation forms, we implemented a new donation form and value proposition that emphasized the value of recurring giving. In addition to the expanded and more visual value proposition, the new form also laid out a cohesive product-level value proposition on why recurring giving is so important.
With this new treatment, we ran a test across several of the most highly trafficked donation pages to see which would result in more gifts.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment had interesting results. The new treatment resulted in a 54% decrease in the number of donors acquired. However, since the goal of the treatment was to acquire recurring donors given their increased value, we need to analyze the impact on that segment. In the case of recurring donors, the new form does see a 87% increase in acquisition.
Assuming that the average recurring donor will last 12 months, we then factored these two results and discovered that after 12 months, the new treatment would effectively break even on revenue compared to the original control. With a 54% drop in donors, continuing with the treatment would seem to be a pretty risky bet on long-term revenue potential. Based upon this analysis, we decided to end the test and continue with the current control.
experiments.fluxMetrics:
The Flux Metrics analyze the three primary metrics that affect revenue (traffic, conversion rate, and average
				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:
0% increase in traffic
				× 54.0% decrease in conversion rate
				× 142.3% increase in average gift
									11.5% increase in revenue

experiments.title: How pre-selecting a monthly gift and amount for a Meta donation form impacts conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that preselecting a gift amount for Meta donors will improve conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/29/2025 - 06/26/2025
Based on previous experiments, we had seen that a combination of pre-selecting monthly and $35 on the gift array had helped increase recurring conversions. We wanted to test whether this was the case across different channels; in this experiment, we focused on Meta specifically. The traffic was split 85% to control and 15% to treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test explored whether increasing the donation amounts shown on a Meta form would lead donors to give more. The hypothesis: higher displayed amounts would shift donor choices upward, raising the average gift.
Key Results:

What the Results Suggest

Next Steps:
Given the consistent directional lift across conversion, RPV, and average gift, and acknowledging that limited sample sizes reduce confidence in the findings, this test warrants further validation with a larger sample — particularly for desktop and recurring giving, where data was too limited to draw robust conclusions.

experiments.title: How Does a Monthly Ask Influence Recurring Donor Conversion?

rows.name: Control: Recurring Donations w/ One-Time Gift Default
Treatment: Recurring Donations w/ Recurring Gift Default

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that presenting a clear monthly giving ask to donors arriving from an urgency-driven Facebook ad will increase recurring conversion and long-term value because framing the gift as ongoing support encourages deeper commitment than a one-time ask.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/19/2026 - 03/20/2026
Human Coalition running a Facebook fundraising campaign built around a clear and compelling $26 one-time donation ask. This ad is one of our strongest performers, driven by a message that invites donors to step into a moment of urgent need and provide immediate, compassionate support.
While this approach is effective at generating initial gifts, we want to better understand how to increase long-term donor value from this traffic. Specifically, we are exploring whether donors who respond to this type of urgent, emotionally resonant appeal are open to making an ongoing commitment (monthly gift) rather than a one-time gift.
To test this, we ran a split URL experiment on the donation experience following the ad click. All users see the same Facebook ad and $26 framing, but are randomly directed to one of two landing page experiences:
Control: A standard donation page focused on a one-time gift
Treatment: A modified donation page that clearly and prominently asks for a monthly recurring gift, with language intentionally adapted to remove ambiguity and reinforce the ongoing nature of the commitment
This test is designed to isolate whether reframing the post-click experience, from a single act of giving to a sustained monthly impact, changes how donors choose to give.
At its core, this experiment explores a key motivational question: When someone is moved to act in a moment of urgency, are they more likely to give once—or to commit to ongoing support when that path is made clear?
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test did not reach statistical significance (93.5% confidence); however, the directional results provide meaningful insight into donor behavior.
The monthly-first experience increased recurring gift conversion from 0.86% to 1.7% (+100.5% relative lift). On its own, this suggests that when donors are explicitly and clearly asked to give monthly, more of them will choose to do so. The treatment successfully surfaced and captured latent willingness among a subset of donors to commit to ongoing support.
However, this increase came from a relatively small base and did not translate into enough total recurring volume to outweigh declines in overall conversion. This indicates that while the ask itself is effective at shifting behavior for some donors, the broader audience arriving from this ad is not primarily motivated by a recurring commitment.
Secondary Metrics
1. Overall Conversion Rate (Transactions)
Control: 10.87%Treatment: 7.01%
The treatment saw a significant drop in overall conversion rate. This indicates that introducing a monthly-first ask created friction at the point of decision, reducing the likelihood that donors would complete any gift.
2. One-Time Donations
Control: 9.66%Treatment: 5.28%
One-time giving declined sharply in the treatment. This is a critical driver of overall performance loss and suggests that the monthly framing may have interrupted donor momentum rather than redirecting it.
3. Revenue & Value per Visitor
Control: $5,220.50 total | $4.50 per visitorTreatment: $3,385.50 total | $2.93 per visitor
Revenue followed the same pattern as conversion rate. The lower value per visitor confirms that the monthly ask did not generate enough higher-value gifts to compensate for reduced participation.
Reviewing all metrics together:
The monthly ask works for a subset of donors (as seen in the lift in recurring conversion), but reduces participation across the broader audienceThere is a clear tradeoff between commitment and conversion: increasing upfront commitment decreases the number of people willing to actThe ad creates a moment of urgency and simplicity (“give $26 now”), and the monthly ask introduces cognitive and emotional friction that disrupts that momentumDirectional consistency across metrics increases confidence in the insight, despite not reaching 95% statistical significance
Next Steps
Test aligning the ask earlier in the journey (Test ad variants that frame the gift as monthly from the start to better match donor expectations)Test variations of a post-donation monthly upsell

experiments.title: How adding tangible benefits about monthly giving impacts monthly donor conversion on video series acquisition donation pages
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clear, tangible benefits for becoming a monthly donor on acquisition donation pages will increase monthly donor conversion, as it makes the offer to become a monthly donor clearer and appealing, thereby increasing motivation to commit.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/11/2025 - 09/23/2025
A nonprofit organization committed to supporting families through biblically based resources and guidance. As part of ongoing efforts to increase recurring donor conversion, we designed a test to evaluate whether highlighting exclusive monthly donor benefits would encourage more visitors to become monthly givers on high-traffic acquisition donation pages.
We ran this test across three acquisition funnels tied to popular video series: Proof You Need, Contagious Joy, and Becoming Unoffendable. Their donation pages are shown to users after they sign up for the video series. The pages request a gift of $30 or more, and the gift form defaults to a monthly option. The pages also show a yellow callout above the form to further promote monthly giving.
To build on that foundation, we tested the addition of a new message block near the bottom of the page that highlighted two concrete benefits for monthly donors:
A 40% discount at the  store, plus free shipping on all online orders
A free, hand-picked faith-building resource delivered monthly
We hypothesized that highlighting these tangible, recurring benefits would increase perceived value and commitment, ultimately leading to more recurring donor conversions.
This A/B test was structured as a 50/50 traffic split across all three donation pages, with the control maintaining the current design and messaging, and the treatment introducing the new benefits copy above the donation form. This write-up covers the aggregated results from the three campaigns.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Contrary to expectations, adding benefit language to promote monthly giving led to a decrease in both recurring and overall donor conversions. While not statistically significant at the 95% confidence level, the treatment showed a 24.4% drop in monthly donor conversions (48 gifts in the control group vs. 37 in the treatment group) at 80% confidence, and a 14.4% drop in total transactions (220 in the control group vs. 192 in the treatment group) at 89.4% confidence. These results fall below the standard threshold for statistical significance but indicate a consistent downward trend worth noting. This outcome was surprising, as we hypothesized that surfacing tangible donor perks would increase perceived value and encourage more visitors to become monthly givers. However, our interpretation is that introducing additional value proposition elements — even if well-intentioned — may have overwhelmed users on pages already rich in content. These acquisition donation pages serve multiple purposes: confirming video series sign-ups, offering a free book premium, explaining the mission, and prompting donations. By layering on monthly donor benefits, we may have inadvertently created cognitive overload, making the decision-making process harder rather than easier. This result suggests that in highly content-dense environments, more messaging is not always better — even when it adds value. In these contexts, simplicity and a focused message may be more effective than trying to incorporate every persuasive angle. It also reinforces the importance of considering where and how value propositions are introduced on a page. Next Steps:
Test placement and simplicity: Test simpler, more integrated monthly giving prompts (e.g., in the callout or post–one-time gift popup) to avoid competing with other key messages.
 	Explore benefit framing: Future tests could frame benefits around impact or mission alignment, rather than just transactional perks.

experiments.title: How a monthly first ask on a main donation page impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: A monthly first ask on the main donation page will increase recurring giving with minimal impact on overall transactions, as it highlights the ease and effect of becoming a monthly supporter. Meanwhile, the preselected option subtly reinforces that most donors choose to give monthly.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/19/2025 - 10/07/2025
For the American Cornerstone Institute, our primary focus has been on growing their recurring donor base. To build on that momentum, we tested adding a recurring gift ask directly on the main donation page, inspired by strong results from earlier acquisition offer tests using a similar approach.
This A/B test ran for just over a month, with equal traffic distributed between the control and treatment versions.
The control utilized the existing donation page without any modifications. The treatment mirrored the control’s copy but added details about the benefits of becoming a monthly partner and preselected the monthly giving option on the donation form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
1. Adding a monthly first ask on the main donation page led to a significant increase in recurring donations, with a 223.0% increase in recurring donations for All Traffic. This indicates that highlighting the benefits of becoming a monthly supporter and preselecting the monthly giving option can effectively encourage more donors to choose recurring donations.
2. The treatment also resulted in a substantial increase in overall Transactions, with a 543% increase. This shows that while the focus was on increasing recurring donations, the strategy also had a positive impact on overall donation transactions.
3. The statistical confidence level of 93.6% for the increase in recurring donations and 95% for the rise in transactions suggests that the results are approaching validity, but further testing would be needed for confirmation.
In future experimentation, we should:
1. Continue to explore the impact of emphasizing recurring donations on the main donation page. Further testing can help us validate the results and assess the long-term impact on donor retention and revenue.
2. Consider refining the messaging and design of the monthly giving ask to optimize its effectiveness. This could involve A/B testing different languages, visuals, or incentives to increase recurring donations further.
3. Monitor the overall impact on donation transactions to ensure that the focus on recurring donations does not negatively affect one-time contributions. Finding the right balance between the two types of giving is essential for maximizing fundraising outcomes.
Overall, these findings provide valuable insights into the potential of strategic messaging and design changes to increase recurring donations. By analyzing the results and refining our approach, we can continue to enhance our fundraising strategies and foster sustainable support for the American Cornerstone Institute.

experiments.title: How preselecting the gift frequency and gift amount on a giving form impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that preselecting and expanding the gift array for Today in the Word acquisition giving page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because it will help reduce decision friction for donors by clearly presenting the preferred giving frequency and gift size within the acquisition funnel .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/19/2025 - 08/24/2025
Moody Bible’s giving forms have historically been designed without pre-selected gift frequencies or amounts. While this has always been their standard setup, it differs from many other donation platforms where defaults are commonly used.
To test whether defaults could reduce decision friction and encourage giving, we introduced two pre-selections:
Gift frequency: defaulted to one-time
Gift amount: defaulted to the lowest option in the gift array ($25)
Our goal was to streamline the donor experience—minimizing effort and uncertainty—while also testing whether this change could increase overall conversion and potentially lift the average gift amount.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment demonstrated that pre-selecting gift frequency and amount—combined with expanding the gift array options—produced a significant lift in donor conversion metrics.
The treatment group outperformed the control with a 454.5% increase in Transactions for All Traffic (94.4% LOC) and a 1,015% increase in Revenue (100% LOC).
 	Notably, while the sample size was small, the treatment showed no negative impact on transactions or revenue, indicating a clear positive response from Today in the Word’s audience.
 	The average gift amount also increased by 116.7%, confirming our hypothesis that the treatment would encourage larger donations.

experiments.title: Will Defaulting to $50 monthly ask improve conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that preselecting the $50 gift amount in the gift array for the monthly donation page will achieve more conversions because by having it preselected there will be one less step for the user to do in the donation process.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/29/2025
In the past, we that having this organization break out the episodic and monthly giving pages perform better by having a ‘donate’ and ‘donate monthly’ button in the navigation bar.
For this test, we wanted to see if we could improve conversion and increase average gift on the monthly donation page by preselecting $50 in the gift array.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment indicate that preselecting the $50 gift amount in the gift array for the monthly donation page did not result in more conversions. In fact, the treatment group experienced a 44.9% decrease in donations compared to the control group. While this difference is not statistically significant at a 93.7% confidence level, it suggests that further testing is necessary to determine the true impact of preselecting the gift amount.

When digging into the results, we noticed that the average gift amount is $35 for this page. Coming in at $50 could have added more friction to this test because the amount was too high. In our next test, we are going to see if preselecting $25 will increase conversion.

experiments.title: How adding a monthly callout on the general donation page impacted transactions and recurring gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an Avila Army callout for Avila’s audience on the general donation page will achieve an increase in recurring gifts and increase transactions because highlighting the program’s mission and impact will inspire donors to see themselves as part of a dedicated community making a tangible difference.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/18/2025 - 03/04/2025
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
For this specific test, we tested whether adding a banner about their recurring program, the Avila Army, impacted recurring donors and transactions. The Avila Foundation encourages people to join their recurring program to be apart of the battalion of friends and followers actively deployed in the war for souls through the monthly support of the mission of the Avila Foundation.
We wanted to see if putting information and a testimonial about this program would encourage others to sign up as well as donate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Takeaways:
Recurring Callout Reduced One-Time Gifts – The Avila Army banner led to a 58.1% decrease in one-time donations, likely by distracting or overwhelming donors.
 	Minimal Impact on Recurring Giving – Only one new recurring donor was added, with low statistical confidence (68.4%), indicating the callout wasn’t compelling enough.
 	User Experience Issues – The callout might have disrupted the natural donation flow, making giving less intuitive.
Next Steps & Ideas for Promoting Recurring Gifts:
Balance One-Time & Recurring Giving – Test a softer recurring ask (e.g., an optional checkbox) rather than making it the focal point.
 	Refine Messaging – Emphasize impact, ease, and community benefits rather than duty-driven language.
 	Segment Audiences – Show recurring gift appeals only to engaged donors or past givers, rather than all visitors.
 	Test Avila Army's multiple giving levels – Offer tiered giving levels with exclusive perks (e.g., special updates, donor recognition, or impact reports) for recurring donors.
 	Use Social Proof – Feature multiple testimonials and highlight how recurring donors create lasting change.
 	Retarget One-Time Donors – Send personalized follow-ups thanking them and inviting them to upgrade to recurring.

experiments.title: Will adding a heart to the monthly tab increase monthly transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a heart on the monthly tab for the sustainer donation page giving form will achieve a higher number of recurring transactions because the heart will the users eyes to the monthly tab and lead them to giving a recurring gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/14/2025 - 02/18/2025
In the past we have tested adding different hearts to elements to catch the users eye and get them to take the action that we are wanting.
In this case, we wanted to see if we could add a heart on the monthly tab on the donation form would increase the number of monthly transactions.
We ran this test on the main sustainer donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the addition of a heart on the monthly tab for the sustainer donation page giving form resulted in a significant decrease in transactions for all traffic. The treatment group saw a 69.4% decrease in recurring transactions with a 92.4% statistical level of confidence, indicating that the control group performed better in this case.

While the results are not statistically significant at the usual 95% confidence level, they are approaching validity, suggesting that further testing would be necessary to confirm the impact of the heart icon on user behavior.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the potential impact of visual elements like hearts on donation forms, as they may not always lead to the desired outcome. It would be beneficial to conduct additional tests with different visual cues or design elements to see which ones are most effective in increasing recurring transactions. Additionally, it is crucial to analyze user feedback and behavior to understand the reasons behind the results and make informed decisions for future optimization efforts.

experiments.title: How asking for a one-time or monthly gift in the email and leading with a monthly tab on the landing page effects monthly donations during Giving Tuesday
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for a monthly or one-time donation and leading with a monthly tab on the landing page for members via an email solicitation during Giving Tuesday will achieve an increase in monthly gifts with no decrease in overall transactions.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/05/2024
During Giving Tuesday the Membership team designed an email and landing page test to see whether an ask for a one-time donation or monthly donation vs. an ask for a “best gift” made any difference on transactions or monthly gifts. The treatment which featured the one-time or monthly ask also led to landing page with the monthly tab first.
Here are the results breakdown:
Members: The experiment showed no statistical difference in donor acquisition between asking for a one-time or monthly gift vs. asking for their best gift. There was a 145% lift in monthly transactions with 90% LOC (5 vs. 10 gifts). There was a directional decrease in clicks on the link in the email for the treatment with a 5% decrease with 63% LOC.
Nonmembers: The test showed the treatment caused a statistically valid decrease in donor acquisition of -59% with 97% LOC. There was no added benefit in monthly acquisition, but the treatment did validate a 5% decrease in email clicks with 99.4% LOC.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that asking for a monthly or one-time donation and leading with a monthly tab on the landing page for members via an email solicitation during Giving Tuesday resulted in a significant increase in monthly gifts with no decrease in overall transactions.

Although the results showed a 145.6% increase in donations for all traffic, the statistical level of confidence was below the standard threshold of 95%. This indicates that while the treatment showed promise, further testing is necessary to confirm its effectiveness.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing this approach with a larger sample size to increase the statistical power and achieve a higher level of confidence in the results. Additionally, it could be useful to explore different variations of the treatment to optimize the ask for monthly donations and potentially further increase donation rates.

Overall, the experiment highlights the potential benefits of tailoring donation asks to members based on their giving preferences and the importance of ongoing testing and optimization to drive success in fundraising efforts.

experiments.title: How defaulting to recurring gift with a partner’s benefits call-out on a donation page impacts recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that emphasizing the exclusive benefits of becoming a FamilyLife Partner will increase the number of recurring donations because it provides added value to donors, strengthening their connection to the organization and making the recurring gift feel more rewarding and meaningful.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/08/2024
FamilyLife is a Christian organization dedicated to strengthening marriages and families through biblically-based resources, events, and guidance, supporting individuals as they build enduring, healthy relationships. Previously, we tested a recurring gift default on FamilyLife’s premium donation pages, which led to a 0.53% increase in recurring donations with a 99.9% level of confidence. Based on this success, the recurring default has been implemented as a permanent feature across the donation microsite. In this experiment, we aim to test a new approach to the recurring gift call-out, focusing on language that highlights the exclusive benefits of becoming a FamilyLife Partner, to assess if this approach will further encourage monthly giving.
If we emphasize the exclusive benefits of becoming a FamilyLife Partner (e.g., access to insider ministry updates, private events, and a curated library), then users will be more likely to commit to a recurring gift, as the added value and connection to the FamilyLife community will increase their perceived return on investment and loyalty.
The test involved two variations on the donation page. The Control version used a recurring gift call-out that stated, “Equip 140 families with practical insights to strengthen the relationships that matter most.” The Treatment version introduced a new call-out focused on membership benefits: “Receive exclusive benefits: insider ministry updates, invitation-only events, access to our curated library, and membership to our private Partners-only network.” This A/B test was split 50/50 and conducted on their best performing premium donation pages: Seeing Beautiful Again, 52 Weeks in the Word, and the Ferret Flush Couples Pack.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment aimed to determine if emphasizing exclusive benefits for recurring donors would increase the number of monthly donations on FamilyLife’s premium donation pages. However, partway through the test, two of the three offers (Seeing Beautiful Again, 52 Weeks in the Word, and Ferret Flush Couples Pack) were turned off due to budget limitations. As a result, traffic to the test was reduced, which impacted our ability to achieve a conclusive result.
Recurring Donations Results: The Control outperformed the Treatment in terms of recurring donations. The Control had a 0.59% conversion rate (13 recurring gifts from 2,210 samples), while the Treatment had a 0.30% conversion rate (7 recurring gifts from 2,366 samples), representing a relative decrease of 49.7% at an 86.1% level of confidence. This difference did not reach statistical significance, so the results are inconclusive.
Overall Transactions Results: When examining all transactions, the Control had a 4.8% conversion rate (107 total gifts from 2,210 samples) compared to a 4.9% conversion rate in the Treatment (115 total gifts from 2,366 samples). This represents a slight relative increase of 0.4% in the Treatment, with a very low level of confidence (2.4%). This result also did not validate, indicating that the new messaging had no meaningful effect on overall transactions.
Theories on Donor Response:
Mismatch with Motivation: FamilyLife donors may be motivated more by the immediate impact of their giving rather than exclusive, long-term benefits. The Control message, which directly highlighted the impact of equipping families, may have resonated more with donors’ desire to see their contribution create tangible change. The Treatment, which shifted focus to membership perks, may have unintentionally distracted from this primary motivation.Perceived Complexity: The mention of exclusive benefits and a private network may have introduced an element of complexity or commitment that was unappealing. Donors may respond best to messages that feel straightforward and immediately impactful. The “Partners-only network” or “curated library” might not have been clearly valuable in this context and could have made the ask feel less accessible.Different Expectations for Monthly Giving: Some donors may view recurring giving as a simple way to support the organization consistently, without needing additional incentives. This audience might value the ease and simplicity of monthly giving over exclusive access, making the Partner benefits message less motivating.

experiments.title: Changing the default payment option for recurring donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the default option to ACH/direct debit for donors considering a recurring gift will achieve an increase in donor conversions because the percieved greater ease for managing their ongoing gifts..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/30/2024 - 08/23/2024
Like most nonprofit organizations, Luther Seminary places a high value on acquiring new recurring donors. Their current donation form for recurring donations defaults to asking for a credit card payment. We wanted to measure the impact of asking for a direct debit or ACH gift. The donation page copy, format, form fields, and gift array all remained identical between the control and treatment. The only variable in this experiment was the default payment option.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the default payment option to ACH/direct debit for donors considering a recurring gift resulted in a significant increase in donor conversions. The treatment group saw a 458.8% increase in Recurring Donations for All Traffic compared to the control group.
However, the statistical level of confidence was only 92.6%, slightly below the 95% threshold for standard significance. This means that while the results are promising and indicate that the treatment was successful, further testing is needed to confirm the effectiveness of this strategy.
While this was not the primary metric we measured, the treatment version also had a slight decrease in one-time donation conversions through this form. Since the donation page copy was focused on recurring donations, and the result was well below the level of confidence for validation, this result should not discourage future testing and optimizations for default ACH payments for recurring giving pages.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the default payment options for recurring donations to see if the initial results hold true - would offering additional methods of payment increase the conversion rate? Additionally, considering other variables such as the messaging around the payment options, the design of the donation form, and value proposition language throughout the page could affect the conversion rate. It is essential to continue experimenting and iterating to ensure that the organization is maximizing donor conversions and ultimately increasing support for Luther Seminary.

experiments.title: How does adding premium language to the donation page affect recurring donor conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding premium language to the donation page for potential donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion because of the additional clarity and continuity between the language used in the email and the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/05/2024
During the Navigators February Sustainers Appeal campaign, we decided to run a 50/50 split A/B test on the donation page. The test ran for emails 4-8 before we rolled it out for the last two emails of the campaign.
The control focused on the impact of monthly gifts and the way in which monthly sustainers are central to the organization.
The treatment was almost identical to the control other than that it also included the premium of becoming a monthly partner. This premium was included in all of the emails leading up to this. Therefore, our hypothesis was that adding the premium to the donation page would create continuity between the email and the donation page, reinforcing the incentive.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that premium language included on the donation page may have a positive impact on The Navigators audience. The treatment saw a 55.5% lift in recurring donations at an 86.3% level of confidence. While this donation page was a recurring only donation page, it is possible due to privacy concerns and certain browsers that VWO blocked a portion of their audience from the test because we did have some one-time donations also coming through.
We did see a drop in revenue at 38.6% with a 62.9% level of confidence. However, due to the fact that this was a campaign targeted at activating more recurring donors, this drop was anticipated.
Although these results did not validate, the results, in terms of number of recurring donors, were headed in a positive direction. Therefore, we chose to rollout the treatment to the entire audience for the last two emails of the campaign.
While the findings of this experiment are promising in indicating a potential increase in donor conversion by adding premium language to the donation page, further testing with a larger sample size or different variations may be needed to confirm these results. It is also important to consider other factors that may have influenced the results, such as external events or changes in donor behavior.

experiments.title: How yes/no “autonomy” language on a recurring ask pop-up impacts recurring conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a yes and no option for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in recurring conversion rate because  the autonomy to make a micro-decision allows the visitor to feel in control before being asked for the gift. because  the autonomy to make a micro-decision allows the visitor to feel in control before being asked for a gift.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/05/2024
For The Navigators February Sustainers Appeal campaign, we hypothesized that “yes/no” autonomy language would provide the user with a micro-decision therefore increasing the donor conversion rate.
The control features the standard single call-to-action. Whereas, the treatment offers the call-to-action to donate as well as a “No, I’m not interest option.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including a yes and no option for homepage visitors led to a significant increase in recurring donation conversions. The treatment group saw a 126.1% increase in donations for all traffic at an 87.8% level of confidence.
Although this experiment did not validate, the results suggest that The Navigators audience appreciates the autonomy provided by both a "yes" and "no" option, and in turn, they are more likely to give a gift.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing variations of autonomy language to see if the trend of increased conversion rates holds true. Additionally, exploring other ways to empower visitors and make them feel in control of their actions could further enhance donation outcomes for The Navigator's audience.

experiments.title: How a more generalized ask in a sticky bar for Giving Tuesday impacted click-through rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that framing the message in terms of a broader impact will resonate with more people and lead to a higher click-through rate because it is more emotionally engaging.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/27/2023 - 11/28/2023
On Giving Tuesday, Dallas Theological Seminary made a concerted effort to acquire more monthly donors, called Cornerstone Partners. They’ve typically taken an approach that outlines the number of new Cornerstone Partners needed before the midnight deadline.
This year, they wanted to see if a more general ask would lead to higher click-through rates because the goal number could be perceived as overwhelming for someone reading.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment version of the message, which framed the ask in terms of a broader impact, did not lead to a higher click-through rate compared to the control version. In fact, the treatment version saw a significant decrease in clicks by 61.1% with a 93.1% level of confidence.
These results suggest that the hypothesis was incorrect and that framing the message in terms of a broader impact may not resonate with more people or be more emotionally engaging. Therefore, it may not be an effective strategy for increasing click-through rates and acquiring more monthly donors.
In future experimentation, it would be necessary to further test different messaging strategies to determine what resonates most with the target audience. It is important to take into account the specific characteristics and motivations of the donors to understand what messaging would be most compelling to them.
Additionally, it would be advisable to conduct experiments with larger sample sizes and run the experiment for a longer period to increase the statistical validity of the results. This would help ensure that the findings are more reliable and generalizable.
Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of testing different messaging approaches and continuously refining strategies to optimize donor engagement and acquisition.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign impacted monthly giving for membership renewals
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a radical redesign with customized value proposition for returning members looking to renew will achieve an increase in donor conversion because the enhanced appeal and exclusivity focus on what the user wants to do rather than the organization’s internal goals. because  the enhanced appeal and exclusivity focus on what the user wants to do rather than the organization’s internal goals.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/09/2023 - 07/02/2023
For visitors looking to renew their membership from the homepage, we wondered if the existing donation page language for renewals was too focused on the organization. We developed a radical redesign language with customized value proposition for existing members who are already familiar with the organization and had shown historical motivation to make a gift. We hypothesized that increasing the value prop elements of appeal and exclusivity would frame a significantly more attractive way of describing what the user desires. Even if the internal goal is to get a user to renew their membership, the user wants to help protect our nation’s founding principles and keep their membership active. We wanted to communicate this clearly on the page to see if it would increase conversion. We updated the language to recognize that we were talking to visitors who had previously supported the organization and had arrived on this page to make an additional gift to support the organization. We also tested the presentation of the monthly ask, changing it from a checkbox after the gift amount had already been selected to a tabbed form with “Join the Leaders Club” as the text for monthly gifts.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the experiment for two months, the radical redesign treatment of the donation page with a customized value proposition for returning members looking to renew resulted in a 97% increase in Leaders Club Member conversion with 92% level of confidence. Although the statistical level of confidence is below 95%, the results are directionally approaching validity. This suggests that the enhanced appeal and exclusivity focused on what the user wants to do rather than the organization's internal goals successfully attracted more donors. The treatment version also removed an additional step that asked the visitor to select which area their donation should go towards, so this reduced friction could have also played a role in the increased conversion.
We also saw flat results for one-time donations on this page, so there is opportunity to isolate the value proposition copy and do further testing to increase one-time gifts as well as monthly. We believe future iterations could include additional clarity around the benefits of being a Leaders Club member in addition to the tabbed form design and title to help more visitors choose this option.
Overall, the key learning is to focus on the user's desires and motivations, enhancing the value proposition in alignment with what we already know about the intended audience, in order to increase donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: How a Donation Interruptor Impacts Monthly Giving on an Offer’s Instant Donation Page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that donation interruptor  for users who pre-select a one-time gift will achieve an increase monthly donation conversion because psychologically it lowers the amount of their original gift, while providing value prop copy about how that smaller amount each month has a far greater impact..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/26/2023 - 07/02/2023
The Donation Interruptor is an experiment that we’ve tested on IMB’s general donation page in the past. For a greater collection of data, we wanted to test the experience on one of their offers, a Course on Prayer. The tabbed form on the donation page pre-selects One-Time, we hypothesized that a pop-up appearing directly after they submit their gift amount (but before they provide their payment info) will increase monthly gifts. The reason being that the popup will present the user value copy around how a lower amount (but each month) can make a greater impact even though a lower gift upfront.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that implementing the Donation Interruptor on the Course on Prayer offer resulted in a significant increase in monthly donations for all traffic. Although the statistical level of confidence is below the standard threshold of 95%, and the results are approaching validity.
This suggests that the interruptor pop-up, which highlights the impact of smaller monthly donations, has the potential to influence donors to choose the monthly giving option instead of a one-time gift. However, further testing is necessary to confirm these findings and ensure their validity.
One observation is that although this increased monthly gifts, the treatment does appear to lower the total number of gifts. This however, did not validate as a statistical result. It is something to continue observing, while also keeping in mind that repeated monthly gifts can still be an exponential revenue gain even despite a small decrease off in total gifts

experiments.title: How positioning match oriented language impacted recurring gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity around a matching gift campaign for recurring donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/26/2023
During a recurring gift campaign, we wanted to test which message would be more motivating to donors to upgrade from a one time to a recurring gift. On the control page, we highlighted that a matching gift was in place which would double their donation. For the treatment donation page, we went into more detail about the match and focused the value proposition more around doubling the impact of their monthly donation. We split traffic to each page 50/50 for the duration of the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learnings from this experiment are that adding more clarity around a matching gift campaign for recurring donors did not achieve the expected increase in donor conversion. In fact, the treatment page resulted in a 26.5% decrease in donations with a statistical level of confidence just below the acceptable threshold. Therefore, further testing is necessary before making any definitive conclusions about the effectiveness of messaging related to matching gift campaigns for recurring donors. We learned that the match language—on its own—was less effective in converting donors than keeping the values-based language and mentioning the match as one additional incentive to give. In future experimentation, it may be helpful to focus on more targeted messaging and testing smaller changes in order to better understand the impact on donor behavior. Additionally, it may be worthwhile to test other types of incentives beyond matching gifts to determine which strategies are most effective in motivating donors to convert to recurring gifts.

experiments.title: How does a tabbed donation form affect recurring conversion rates on a primary donation page?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a tabbed donation form for donation page visitors will achieve higher recurring donation rates by making this giving option and its impact more visible.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/10/2023 - 02/10/2023
We wanted to know how a tabbed donation form (featuring single and monthly gifts) would affect recurring donations on the primary donation page for a public policy organization.
In this experiment, the control presented the option to make a recurring gift by featuring a checkbox below the gift array that asked the visitor if they would like to make their gift on a recurring basis. Beneath the checkbox, an impact statement communicated the impact of making a recurring gift.
The treatment instead presented the option to make a recurring gift in a tabbed form format, with “single gift” and “monthly gift” shown side-by-side above the gift array and the impact statement shown above the tabs.
For this experiment, ~50% of traffic was shown the control and ~50% of traffic was shown the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for one month, we observed that the tabbed donation form produced an 11.0% decrease in recurring gifts with a 25% level of confidence, a 25.4% decrease in overall donations with a 99.2% level of confidence, and a 32.1% decrease in traffic with a 94.4% level of confidence.
These decreases were most severe among returning visitors where total donations decreased by 29.5% with a 99.7% level of confidence and revenue decreased by 40.6% with a 97.6% level of confidence indicating.
For new visitors, we observed a large increase in revenue (177%) -- but because this increase was a result of just one additional donor, it is apparent that this is being skewed by a large gift and not indicative of overall performance.
These results disprove our hypothesis and indicate that in the context of visiting a primary donation page (high donor motivation), a tabbed donation form with the impact statement above the gift array may have distracted the donor or created friction by presenting multiple options before they made the decision to give.
It also suggests that it is more persuasive to ask someone to "upgrade" their donation after they have already made a micro-commitment by selecting their donation amount from the gift array.
Based on these results, we will look to test a monthly giving checkbox against the tabbed donation forms currently running on this organization's instant donation pages tied to acquisition offers.

experiments.title: How adding a monthly callout to a main donate page affects recurring gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a monthly call out to the main donate page for prospective donors will achieve a higher recurring donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/15/2022 - 07/09/2022
The Navigators had an initiative to increase the acquisition of monthly donors. At the heel of their recurring donor campaign, we wanted to implement a simple way to acquire more recurring donors outside of campaign season. When looking at their main donation page we noticed that the monthly option blended with the other options presented for giving, among “Anonymous Gift” and “In memory of.” It was our hypothesis that the monthly option was not prominent enough for the prospective user to select this option. Furthermore, there was no value proposition language that positioned monthly giving as a better option for the prospective donor. We decided to implement a monthly callout with two variables. The first treatment simply stated what their monthly gift would do – the core value proposition: “Help believers become disciplemakers with your monthly gift today.” The second treatment gave the prospective donor a goal and identified the person as a believer who could help others mature in their faith: “We’re looking for 250 believers to give monthly to help others mature in their faith. Will you be one?” Traffic to this main donate page was primarily driven organically.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running this experiment for nearly a month we saw the most impact on desktop users. For the first callout we saw a nearly valid 50% lift in recurring donors with a level of confidence of 94%. In the second callout we saw a 11% increase in recurring donors with a level of confidence of 34%. When looking across all channels we did see a 40% increase in recurring donors with a level of confidence of 91%. Given that the highest and most statistically valid lift was seen from the first callout, we had decided to rollout this variant to 100% of their traffic. These results indicate that the Navigators' audience are more motivated by their core value proposition to create more disciplemakers. They did not respond nearly as much to the second, goal-oriented variant. It could be that the goal of 250 seemed too large for the end-user and could've deterred them from making their monthly contribution. We are proposing to test this same concept on the Navigator's staff pages as well.

experiments.title: How adding a personalized monthly call out to staff donation pages affects recurring donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a personalized monthly call out  for prospective staff donors will achieve a higher recurring donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/06/2022 - 07/15/2022
The Navigators recently ran experiment where they added a monthly call out to their main donation page. When they saw promising results through this experiment, we decided to run this same type of experiment on their staff pages. In an effort to increase monthly donations we drew attention to the monthly giving option on the donation page by adding a callout and adding a personalization. This personalization pulled in their first name in the call out so as to humanize the giving experience. Our hypothesis was that if we highlighted giving monthly as an option and we reminded the donor that they were in fact giving to a person, we would convert more monthly donors.
We tested this concept on a select group of staff members who were actively fundraising and were receiving a high amount of traffic on their pages.
experiments.keyLearnings:
When looking at all recurring gifts acquired through this experiment we saw a 22% decrease in recurring gifts with a level of confidence of 66.5%. However, when examining a specific segment, new visitors, we saw a nearly valid decrease of 43.9% with a level of confidence of 94.6%. These were surprising results compared to what we experienced on the main donate page.
We concluded that:
The main donate page's callout was actually much more focused on the impact of the monthly gift "making disciplemakers" as opposed to just stating they should make a monthly gift like this callout. New site visitors may not be as swayed to see a specific person's name on the callout. Adding the personalization in third person, i.e. "Support Joe", may seem to organizationally-centric and could deter potential donors.
Our recommendation is to run this test again across all staff pages, change the statement so that it once again focuses on the value and impact of monthly giving, and change the personalization so that it reads "Support me/us."

experiments.title: How a radically redesigned donation page impacted monthly and one-time gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a radical redesign focused on recurring giving for instant donation page visitors will achieve an increase in monthly recurring donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/19/2021 - 01/07/2022
Ahead of their calendar year-end campaign, DTS sent out a digital Christmas Devotional as a cultivation piece to their housefile. The control donation page used their standard appeal language with a paragraph outlining the benefits of monthly giving. To increase recurring gifts, we wanted to test a donation page treatment that specifically asked for a monthly recurring gift emphasizing a microask goal of 10 new daily monthly donors. In addition to the microask goal, we made several other optimizations to the page. We swapped out the headline to reflect that their monthly gift would have an impact 365 days a year and introduced the concept of donor autonomy and value alignment throughout the copy by asking conversational questions such as “Would you consider becoming a Cornerstone Partner?” and “Will you make a monthly gift today to help share biblical truth with the world?” We split traffic 50/50 between the control and the treatment for visitors after they signed up to receive their copy of the digital Christmas Devotional.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we did not receive enough monthly gifts to validate the experiment's impact on recurring giving, we did see other interesting trends in overall giving as a result of running the test. We did not reach statistical significance overall, but we identified that the treatment page was showing a 29.4% lift in donor conversion with 86% level of confidence. Furthermore, as you'll see below, when we analyzed traffic by channel, mobile visitors saw a valid increase of 116% in donations with a 98% level of confidence. The treatment copy is significantly shorter than the control, so we will need to do additional testing to see if reducing the volume of text and limiting the amount of user scrolling contributed to the lift for mobile visitors. Again, even though we did not see movement in monthly giving as the experiment was intended to produce, we identified learnings to assist in improving conversion rate and overall revenue moving forward for future content offers.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 62.2% increase with a 90% level of confidence.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Email visitors had a 33.2% increase in donations with an 88% level of confidence.Female visitors had a 116.0% increase in donations with an 89% level of confidence.25-34 visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with a 92% level of confidence.Male visitors had a 53.3% decrease in donations with a 94% level of confidence.Mobile visitors had a 116.9% increase in donations with a 98% level of confidence.New visitors had a 64.4% increase in donations with a 90% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding a tabbed donation form impacted recurring gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a tabbed donation form for visitors to /renew and the main donation page will achieve an increase in recurring donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/22/2020 - 02/28/2020
As a follow up to a previous experiment, we wanted to see if using a tabbed recurring donation form for visitors to the two primary donation forms would improve recurring donor acquisition. A previous experiment found a significant books in recurring gifts but many of these ended up being mistakes that had to be refunded. We wanted to see if we could have the same impact if we started the form on one-time gift instead of recurring.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for a full month, we discovered there was no statistically significant impact to recurring gifts. While we did see a directional lift, it would require a significantly higher volume of gifts to validate. We decided to hold off on rolling out this treatment until it could be fully validated with a high urgency campaign.

experiments.title: Impact of reducing mental anxiety by adding, “cancel anytime” to a monthly donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that Adding “cancel anytime” language to our monthly donation page for visitors will achieve a greater conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/20/2021 - 11/14/2021
We know that decreasing mental anxiety leads to an increase in donor conversion. One potential point of anxiety when asking for a monthly donation is the concern that the donor is then “locked into” a recurring transaction that they can’t get out of. We decided to test adding “cancel anytime” language to the donation page for our Fall monthly donor campaign to see which converted more donors.
experiments.keyLearnings: Due to the limited duration of the test (one two-week campaign) we couldn't validate the results on all audiences, however we had enough mobile traffic to validate based on that segment. Here, we found a 38% increase in donor conversion. Most other segments also reported an increase in donor conversion by adding the "cancel anytime language." ( the overall average was a 12% increase). This means that by reducing this mental anxiety we can convert more visitors into monthly donors. We will roll this out to other pages.

experiments.title: How communal language on a high-urgency campaign sticky bar affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Communal language will increase revenue during a high urgency campaigns.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/07/2021 - 05/30/2021
FamilyLife was running a matching gift campaign with a focus on recurring giving. Since communal language—copy that shows the reader that they are united with the organization in the most important beliefs and causes that they care for, all spoken with a familiarity of friends—works well for other faith-based organizations. We’d seen lifts of donations and revenue during high-urgency campaign and wanted to test if this would benefit FamilyLife as well. We tested a 50/50 split in traffic on their website.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Overall, there was a directional lift in revenue. This did not fully validate because of the 23% decrease in revenue for new visitors that pulled down the full validation of a 59% increase in revenue for returning visitors.
Our hypothesis is that communal language, invoking the tone of friendship and care, works very well with returning visitors because they are more closely acquainted with the organization. Whereas a intimate tone with a new acquaintance will not resonate.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
