Organic Search visitors had a 21.5% increase in revenue with an 86% level of confidence.Paid Search visitors had a 57.7% decrease in revenue with a 98% level of confidence.Referral visitors had an 89.5% increase in revenue with an 88% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How the homepage callout influences recurring donations

rows.name: One Button w/ New Value Prop

experiments.researchQuestion: Will using the homepage callout to emphasize recurring giving increase the number of recurring givers acquired?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/17/2018 - 01/26/2018
During the month of January, there was a concerted effort to acquire new monthly donors through the Friends of Focus on the Family program. Included in these efforts was radio programming that pushed listeners to go online to become a recurring donor. To take advantage of this visitor motivation, we wanted to see if focusing the callout on the homepage on recurring giving would help influence the visitors’ likelihood to become a recurring donors. Along with this experiment, we wanted to validate if a value proposition that spent more time laying out the benefits of ongoing support could further increase acquisition.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for 2 weeks, this experiment did not produce a statistically valid result. When evaluating the conversions, we realized that the recurring gift offer may be the wrong offer for this section. While percentages may seem wildly different, the number of recurring gifts made, regardless of the treatment, was not high enough to get close to validating. We also saw that a donor is 6x more likely to click on the one-time gift option in the navigation than they are the recurring gift option. With this finding, it may make sense to repurpose that homepage callout section to focus on individual gifts in the short term. Concurrently, we can test new value propositions on the recurring gift donation page and, once validated, we can retesting the new value proposition on the homepage.

experiments.title: Does a personalized (smart content) donation page bring in more monthly donors from an email campaign?
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a donation page containing personalized text perform better as part of an email donor campaign than a “static” donation page that was the same for each email recipient.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/27/2020 - 04/04/2020
We have been looking at possibly switching to a donation platform that is built on Hubspot, allowing us to use smart content on the donation page (names, prior giving history, etc). Our goal being to reduce as much friction as possible.
The first test we launched of this new donor solution provided by DepositFix was to test our short monthly donor campaign. We were going to send 3 emails to our file seeking to activate 100 new monthly donors. For each of these emails, we split the list 50/50 and one set got a personalized email with a link to a static (non-smart) donation page in RaiseDonors, our current donor platform. The other half of the audience got a link to the smart donation page in DepositFix.
experiments.keyLearnings: In addition to a 50% higher donor conversion rate, the Deposit Fix page had an 8% higher average gift ($29.16 vs 26.97). While we didn’t quite hit 97% significance, the smart content page outperformed the static one in every email appeal of the campaign. We will continue testing this in future appeals.

experiments.title: How emphasizing a recurring gift impacted revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Will emphasizing the impact of a recurring increase the overall revenue per visitor?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/03/2017 - 06/06/2017
A past analysis of The Heritage Foundation donor file found that recurring donors provide the highest donor value to the organization and have the best chance of being retained into future years. Based upon this analysis, we decided to launch an experiment to increase the number of recurring givers.
We crafted value proposition copy around the reason to make a recurring gift and included near the recurring gift option on the donation form. We also emphasized making the gift via their Bank Account as these donations tend to have a longer lifetime.
experiments.keyLearnings: After a month of collecting data, we did not see any significant impact on the donor’s likelihood to make a recurring gift, select their Bank Account, or increase overall revenue to the organization. This is still a priority for the organization so we will go back to the drawing board to develop new hypotheses to increase someone’s likelihood to make a recurring gift.

experiments.title: The impact of making a monthly donor appeal in a homepage hero image (Hint: 1,392% increased revenue)
experiments.researchQuestion: Which would raise more money, a hero image making a one-time donation appeal, or one that would appeal for new monthly donors?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/28/2019
Every two weeks, we would change out our homepage hero image featuring a donation CTA as well as our Top Ad donation CTA. We would swap these out to match the language of the most current bi-monthly digital appeal. These hero images were almost always for a one-time donation page.  We hypothesized that, instead of making a one-time donation appeal, we should try acquiring new monthly donors through the hero image. We did not set up a strict AB test, but rather replaced the hero and top ad images with a new monthly appeal and tracked the results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The Control had a higher donor conversion rate, but much fewer clicks. The variation attracted far more visitors to click and, since the appeal was for a monthly appeal, rather than a one-time appeal, resulted in significant more revenue. We ran the test for 72 days. Here are the results:
Control: 327 clicks, 13 donations, $981 raised.
Variation: 1057 clicks, 24 donations, $1,220 raised in monthly revenue, $14,640 in yearly revenue.
The variation had a 227% increase in clicks,  84% increase in donations, and a 1,392% increase in revenue! So, while fewer visitors to the donation page were prepared to become monthly donors, the significant increase in clicks and revenue more than made up for the lower conversion rate.

experiments.title: How recurring gift language impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will including value proposition language specific to The Leaders Club on the main donation form increase Leaders Club Acquisition? And how will it impact overall donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/29/2018 - 06/05/2018
The Heritage Foundation was looking for ways to increase donor acquisition for their recurring giving club, The Leaders Club. As part of a series of experiments, we tested out a few different hypotheses to impact this metric. The first hypothesis we had was that simply explaining the recurring gift option and the potential benefits would increase the perceived value of the offer enough to increase acquisition for the club. We decided to test this hypothesis on the main donation form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for 2 months, we did not reach the desired level of confidence to definitively identify how this experiment impacted both the likelihood to make a gift and the likelihood to make a recurring gift. However, the impact on the likelihood to make a gift was approaching significance (93% LoC) with a 13% decrease by having the Leaders Club language on the page. The impact on the chances to make a recurring gift was a long way from statistical significance (65% LoC).
Given the likelihood that the additional value proposition was hurting conversion and that there was no definitive proof it was helping recurring gift acquisition, we decided to end the experiment and look for alternative options to increase recurring gifts.

experiments.title: How adding a monthly callout to a tabbed form donation page affects recurring donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a callout on the monthly gift tab for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in recurring donations because describing what kind of impact the user could make with a monthly gift would encourage the user to give a recurring gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/21/2025 - 10/31/2025
Time of Grace’s donation pages feature a tabbed form, allowing users to switch between one-time and monthly donations. We wanted to test adding a monthly callout to the monthly tab to see how that would affect recurring transactions.
The language described how the user could make a tangible impact by sharing Jesus’ love with people all year round through a monthly gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key takeaway from this experiment is that adding a callout to the monthly gift tab on the donation page for visitors did not have the intended effect of increasing recurring donations. In fact, the treatment resulted in a 49.4% decrease in recurring donations across all traffic, with a 43.0% statistical significance level. However, since the statistical level of confidence is below the desired threshold of 95%, these results cannot be considered valid or reliable.
There was an increase in the overall number of transactions.
In future experimentation, it is crucial to carefully analyze the results and assess statistical significance before drawing conclusions or implementing changes based on the findings. It is also essential to consider factors such as the potential impact of the proposed changes on user behavior and donation patterns. Additionally, conducting A/B experiments for a longer duration and with a larger sample size may help in obtaining more reliable and conclusive results.

experiments.title: How including a button in an email appeal affects click-through rate for recurring donors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a button to an email for recurring donors will achieve an increase in click-through rate because it provides a clear, visually distinct call-to-action that reduces friction, draws the reader’s eye, and makes it easier for people to take the intended next step.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/26/2025
The Heritage Foundation is running its annual Leader’s Club campaign. The second email of the campaign offered a free ebook called “Flags of Our Fathers.” Within this email, they wanted to test how changing the raw URL to a button would affect the click-through rate to the landing page. This experiment ran on one email, and this segment included donors to The Heritage Foundation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a button to an email for recurring donors did not lead to an increase in click-through rate as hypothesized. In fact, the treatment group saw a 13.6% decrease in clicks compared to the control group. The statistical analysis showed a 63.4% level of confidence, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to provide reliable results and that any conclusions drawn are statistically significant. Additionally, it is crucial to carefully consider the design and implementation of changes such as adding a button, as they may not always have the desired effect. It is also recommended to conduct further testing and analysis to understand the reasons behind the unexpected outcome and to inform future decision-making. Experimentation is a valuable tool for learning and refining strategies, and it is important to approach it with a curious and open mindset to adapt and improve based on the findings.

experiments.title: How adding a sticky stacked navigation bar with a “Donate Monthly” button affects recurring donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a “donate monthly” button on a stacked navigation bar for mobile users will achieve an increase in recurring donations because having a separate button would draw more attention to it, resulting in users clicking on the button and making a donation.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/08/2025
For this experiment, we added a sticky stacked navigation bar for mobile, but we also added a “Donate Monthly” button to see how that would affect transactions. We had previously tested this with just a donate button, and we had seen that there was an increase in clicks to the donate button when in a stacked navigation bar. This experiment was run for mobile users.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a "donate monthly" button to a stacked navigation bar for mobile users can potentially increase donations. The treatment group saw a significant increase in donations, indicating that having a separate button for monthly donations could draw more attention and encourage users to donate regularly.

However, the statistical level of confidence is below the recommended threshold of 95%, which means that these results should not be considered reliable for future decision-making.

We did see an increase in clicks to "donate monthly button". We saw a valid increase in clicks.

But ultimately, we saw a decrease in overall transactions, therefore indicating that this had a negative effect on transactions. We saw a 6.3% decrease in transactions. This was not a valid decrease in transactions.

For future experimentation, it is essential to run further tests with a larger sample size or different variations to validate the impact of adding a "donate monthly" button. It is also important to consider other factors that may influence donation behavior on mobile devices, such as the placement of the button, messaging, and design. Additionally, conducting usability testing and gathering user feedback can provide valuable insights for optimizing the donation process on mobile platforms.

experiments.title: How defaulting the monthly tab on an acquisition offer as apart of a high urgency campaign affects recurring conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting to the monthly tab will achieve an increase in recurring conversion rates because it reduces friction by aligning with the donor’s default behavior, subtly encourages sustained giving through visual anchoring, and reframes the donation as an ongoing commitment rather than a one-time gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/14/2025 - 07/19/2025
The Heritage Foundation is running their annual President’s Match Challenge campaign to members and non-members of Heritage. In this experiment, they wanted to test how defaulting to the monthly tab on the acquisition donation page of an ebook offer as apart of the campaign would affect the rate of recurring giving.
50% of traffic was randomly assigned to each variation (Control vs Treatment). The primary goal was to determine whether defaulting the donation format to monthly giving (rather than one-time giving) would influence the recurring conversion rate.
The idea to run this test stemmed from two core insights:
Behavioral Economics Research:
People tend to go with the default option presented to them. When monthly giving is presented as the default tab, it subtly frames that behavior as the norm or recommended path.
Organizational Goal: Boost Recurring Revenue:
Like many nonprofits, Heritage is invested in growing their base of recurring donors. Monthly gifts provide more predictable, long-term revenue.
By making “Make a Monthly Gift” the default selection (as seen in the treatment version), the hypothesis was that donors would be more likely to choose that option simply because it required no extra clicks or decisions to get there.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. Defaulting to the monthly tab on the donation page resulted in a 0.2% increase in Transactions - Monthly for All Traffic, indicating a potential positive impact on recurring conversion rates.

2. The increase in Revenue by 134% with a 100% level of confidence suggests that while the primary metric did not meet the desired level of significance, there was a significant improvement in the total amount of revenue generated.

3. The results should be cautiously interpreted due to the statistical level of confidence being below the recommended threshold of 85%, indicating that the findings may not be 100% reliable.

4. Future experimentation should consider adjusting the duration of the experiment or increasing the sample size to ensure more robust results.

5. Despite the inconclusive nature of the primary metric, the significant increase in Revenue implies that there may be value in further exploring the impact of defaulting to the monthly tab for donation offerings.

6. It is essential to continue testing this hypothesis in future experiments to validate the findings and determine the true impact on recurring conversion rates.

In conclusion, while the experiment did not provide a definitive answer regarding the impact of defaulting to the monthly tab on recurring conversion rates, the increase in Revenue suggests that there may be potential benefits to implementing this strategy. Further testing and optimization are recommended to gain a deeper understanding of the effects and to make more informed decisions in the future.

experiments.title: How an animated heart and monthly callout impacts giving on Non-Tribute donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an animated heart and monthly callout for users on the non-tribute donation page will achieve an increase in monthly giving because it draws attention the monthly option, while preserving the donor’s autonomy, then after being clicked reinforces the decision with an impact callout.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/02/2025 - 05/16/2025
On CaringBridge’s main donation page (Non-Tribute), we tested an animated heart over the monthly tab, which, once clicked, would display callout copy reinforcing the impact of a recurring gift. We hypothesize that drawing attention to monthly giving, while still preserving the donor’s autonomy, will increase monthly giving. In this experiment, the monthly tab is not pre-selected and the callout is only shown after a user clicks the monthly tab—breaking the steps into two distinct actions: first encouraging the donor to select this tab, then reinforcing the action they’ve taken.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment, which included an animated heart and a monthly callout on the non-tribute donation page, did not lead to an increase in monthly giving. In fact, there was a 15.3% decrease in transactions for all traffic compared to the control group. Additionally, the statistical level of confidence was only 72.6%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid.

It is worth noting that this same experience was conducted on both the Non-Tribute donation page as well as CaringBridge's Tribute donation page. In the latter, this experience did not increase monthly giving, but it did result in greater Tribute gifts overall, whereas here the same experience has decreased transactions overall.

This encourages further consideration around how the two donation pages are different, the diverging motivations of traffic on both to where the same experience could have a reverse effect depending on the page.

experiments.title: How adding in more copy on a recurring donation page affects recurring donation rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding in more copy for recurring donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donations because the copy will provide some clarity about why they should donate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/15/2025 - 03/27/2025
For this test, we tested adding in some more copy on the monthly donation page as a way to provide more clarity and more value proposition to the user.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding more copy to the recurring donation page did not have a positive impact on donation conversions. In fact, the treatment group saw a significant decrease in recurring donations compared to the control group.

It is important to note that the results of this experiment are not statistically significant, with a confidence level below 95%. Therefore, we cannot draw reliable conclusions from these findings.

In future experimentation, it is crucial to continue testing hypotheses and variations on donation pages, but with larger sample sizes and longer testing periods to ensure more valid and reliable results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to consider qualitative feedback from users to understand their preferences and motivations when it comes to donating.

experiments.title: How introducing a new email sender in the beginning of an email appeal affects donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that introducing a new sender at the beginning of an email appeal for email subscribers will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it will create a sense of novelty and engagement, capturing the recipient’s attention more effectively.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/26/2025
This experiment was conducted as part of The Navigators’ kickoff email for their Sustainers Campaign, which aimed to acquire new recurring donors.
The test was designed to evaluate whether introducing a new sender at the beginning of the email appeal would increase donor conversion rates. Given that email engagement plays a crucial role in fundraising success, we wanted to explore whether a fresh or recognizable sender could capture attention, build trust, and drive more donations.
The decision to run this experiment was driven by the need to optimize email performance and maximize donor acquisition at the start of the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. **Introducing a new sender at the beginning of an email appeal can lead to an increase in donor conversion rates**: The treatment, which featured a new sender introducing themselves at the start of the email, resulted in a 41.8% increase in donations. This suggests that changing up the sender in email appeals can have a positive impact on donor engagement.

2. **Confidence in the results**: While the increase in donations was significant, the statistical confidence level was only 64.7%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered completely reliable or valid for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the following:

1. **Continue testing with different sender introductions**: The positive impact of introducing a new sender indicates that further experimentation with different sender personas, introductions, and stories could continue to be beneficial. Testing a variety of approaches can help identify the most effective ways to connect with donors and drive donations.

2. **Increase sample size for higher confidence**: To ensure more reliable results in future experiments, it may be necessary to increase the sample size. This can help boost the statistical confidence level and provide clearer insights into the effectiveness of different email strategies.

3. **Iterate and refine based on learnings**: While the results of this experiment may not be conclusive, the learnings can still be valuable in shaping future email campaigns. Take the insights gained from this experiment, such as the impact of a new sender introduction, and incorporate them into future iterations of email appeals to continue optimizing donor engagement and conversion rates.

experiments.title: Will having shorter copy on the monthly donation page hurt or help recurring transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shortening the copy for the monthly donation page will achieve more recurring transactions because the shorter copy reinforces the need to give without the extra copy that may create decision friction in the process.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/17/2025 - 01/29/2025
Historically, the homepage monthly and episodic donation pages for Focus on the Family have been hard to get a valid test to help increase transition and/or revuene. Last Spring and through the summer we found that mentioning “Biblical Reosurces” on the pages had a positive impact on donations and revenue. We wondered, if we could build off of that finding to improve the donation page copy even more.
For this test, we shortened the copy on the monthly donation page to only communicate about the impact that a donor wold have in empowering families with biblical resources. Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that shortening the copy on the monthly donation page did not result in an increase in recurring transactions. In fact, the treatment group saw a 7.8% decrease in transactions compared to the control group.

Since the statistical level of confidence is only 20.7%, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making. It is important to run future experiments for a longer period of time to gather more data and ensure the results are statistically significant.

In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to test different messaging strategies on the donation page to see what resonates most with donors. Additionally, it could be helpful to explore other factors that may impact donation behavior, such as the design of the page, the suggested donation amounts, or the overall user experience. By continuing to test and iterate on different elements of the donation page, Focus on the Family can optimize for increased donations and revenue.

experiments.title: Will adding a donor testimonial to the sustainer donation page increase transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donor testimonal for the campaign donation pages will achieve more transactions because because the donor testimonial will lend to social proof and increase trust.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/11/2024 - 12/19/2024
This CYE we wanted to see if we could present a donor testimonail on the donation page to increase social proof and trust, in turn increasing transactions.
For this test we added a box right above the giving from that had a donor testimonal in it. We added this to all of the main campaign giving pages. Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a donor testimonial to the campaign donation pages did not lead to an increase in transactions. In fact, the treatment group saw a significant decrease in transactions compared to the control group. However, since the statistical confidence level is below 95%, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

It's also important to note that this test only ran for a couple of days due to the stark decrease in conversions. In the future, it would be worth testing this again to see if it just got off to a slow start.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing different elements on the donation page to see what resonates best with donors. It may be worth exploring other forms of social proof or trust-building elements, such as testimonials from beneficiaries, impact stories, or trust seals. It is also important to ensure that experiments run for a longer period of time in order to gather more reliable and valid data for decision making.

experiments.title: Does mentioning the reach potential of a recurring gift impact donor conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that mentioning the reach value for email subscribers will achieve a increase in donor conversion because people will get a tangible impact of their gift and know the number of lives they are impacting each month.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/23/2025
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
For PTV’s January recruitment of Pathway Partners, PTV’s recurring donor program, we decided to test mentioning the reach value of a monthly gift for a year.
We calculated, based on Facebook CPM rates for 2024, that it costs PTV approximately $25 to reach 1,000 people. Therefore over the span of 12 months, a recurring gift of $25 could reach 12,000 new people for PTV’s mission.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that mentioning the reach value of a monthly gift can lead to a significant increase in donations for Pathway to Victory. The treatment version, which included information about how a monthly gift of $25 could reach 12,000 new people over the span of a year, saw a 61.2% increase in donations for All Traffic compared to the control version. While the statistical level of confidence was only 52.2%, indicating that these results may not be entirely reliable, the overall trend suggests that highlighting the impact of a donation can resonate with donors and encourage them to give.

For revenue, while $25/month was the amount mentioned in the email. The treatment saw a 43% increase with a 70% level of confidence. This is primarily due to a $50 gift given to the treatment version. Implying that this donor wanted to reach twice as many people mentioned in the email.

experiments.title: How Positioning a Monthly Ask Effects Conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that positioning a monthly ask to be more about the donor for the giving tuesday ask will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because positioning the ask to be more donor focused instead of transactional will create a sense of community.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/08/2025
For our CYE campaign this year we decided to have Giving Tuesday be it’s own micro goal.
To make Giving Tuesday stand out we created a micro goal to get 50 monthly donors in one day. Since this was strickly a monthly ask, we wondered what the best way to position the ask would be. Will having the ask be more inclusive or transactional make a difference?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that positioning a monthly donation ask to be more about the donor for Giving Tuesday can potentially lead to a lower donor conversion rate. While the treatment did see a 1.3% decrease in donations for all traffic, the statistical level of confidence was only 3.9%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for valid results.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing with different messaging and positioning strategies to see what resonates best with donors. It would be beneficial to conduct further experiments with larger sample sizes to increase the statistical power and confidence level of the results. Additionally, considering factors such as the timing of the ask, the target audience, and the overall campaign messaging can also impact the effectiveness of the donation appeal.

Overall, the learnings from this experiment should be used as a starting point for further testing and optimization of donation asks to maximize donor conversions and support for the organization's mission.

experiments.title: How asking for a one-time or monthly gift in the email and leading with a monthly tab on the landing page effects donations during Giving Tuesday
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for a monthly or one-time donation and leading with a monthly tab on the landing page for members via an email solicitation during Giving Tuesday will achieve an increase in monthly gifts with no decrease in overall transactions.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/05/2024
During Giving Tuesday the Membership team designed an email and landing page test to see whether an ask for a one-time donation or monthly donation vs. an ask for a “best gift” made any difference on transactions or monthly gifts. The treatment which featured the one-time or monthly ask also led to landing page with the monthly tab first.
Here are the results breakdown:
Members: The experiment showed no statistical difference in donor acquisition between asking for a one-time or monthly gift vs. asking for their best gift. There was a 145% lift in monthly transactions with 90% LOC (5 vs. 10 gifts). There was a directional decrease in clicks on the link in the email for the treatment with a 5% decrease with 63% LOC.
Nonmembers: The test showed the treatment caused a statistically valid decrease in donor acquisition of -59% with 97% LOC. There was no added benefit in monthly acquisition, but the treatment did validate a 5% decrease in email clicks with 99.4% LOC.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment include:

1. The treatment asking for a monthly or one-time donation and leading with a monthly tab on the landing page did not lead to an increase in monthly gifts for all traffic.

2. Despite seeing a decrease in donations for all traffic, the statistical level of confidence was only 8.3%, which is below the 85% threshold for results to be considered valid.

3. The experiment did not achieve the hypothesized increase in monthly gifts with no decrease in overall transactions among members during Giving Tuesday.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to produce statistically significant results with a higher level of confidence. Additionally, it may be beneficial to revisit the hypothesis and consider potential adjustments to the treatment based on the insights gained from this experiment. Further testing with larger sample sizes and potentially refined treatment variations can help validate findings and inform decision-making for future fundraising campaigns.

experiments.title: How does a slide out impact recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a slide out for homepage traffic will achieve a higher recruitment of recurring donors because the slide is a more visible method of recruitment.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/24/2024 - 12/03/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
PTV would like to improve its recruitment of recurring donors (known at Pathway Partners) so we decided to test out a slide out on the homepage.
With the amount of traffic to the homepage we decided to make this a 3 way test:
control is no slide out
treatment 1 is slide out to become a Pathway Partner and receive monthly resources
treatment 2 is slide out to become a Pathway Partner by making a mission gifts
Currently to access the Pathway Partner page is a bit buried on the PTV site. Our hope is that the slide out on the homepage will achieve better results due to the homepage visibility
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a slide out for homepage traffic led to a 30.8% increase in Pathway Partner Revenue (Recurring) for All Traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 35.5%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a high level of statistical confidence. Additionally, it may be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer period of time to gather more data and increase the reliability of the results.

Overall, while the initial results showed a positive impact of adding a slide out for homepage traffic, it is important to use caution in drawing conclusions from the data until a higher level of statistical confidence is achieved.

experiments.title: How changing the gift array options for monthly gift frequency affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the gift array options for a monthly gift and moving the frequency options for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donations because having different options for a one-time and monthly gift will reduce their decision friction and give clarity on the type of gift and amount they want to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/16/2024 - 11/19/2024
For Reasons to Believe, they typically have the same gift array options for both one-time and recurring gift frequencies. For this test, we wanted to test changing the gift array options for the monthly frequency to have options that would be more suitable for a monthly gift. We also changed the placement of the options to select the gift frequency to be above the gift array.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that changing the gift array options for a monthly gift and moving the frequency options above the gift array did not have the desired effect of increasing donations. In fact, the treatment group saw a 1.8% decrease in Transactions for All Traffic with a 5.9% statistical level of confidence, indicating that the control group performed better.

It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was below 95%, meaning that these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making. Therefore, it is inconclusive whether the changes made had any impact on donation behavior.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the design of the gift array options carefully and ensure that any changes made are based on solid research or data-driven insights. It may also be beneficial to conduct further testing with a larger sample size or different variations to determine the most effective approach for increasing donations.

experiments.title: How presenting a daily donor goal in a recurring campaign affects email click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that presenting a daily donor goal instead of a full campaign goal will achieve an increase in conversion rate because it creates a sense of urgency and allows donors to feel they are part of a tangible, short-term impact.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/05/2024
The Navigators ran their bi-annual sustainers campaign in October with the goal of acquiring new recurring donors. Their campaign goal was 90 new recurring donors, but we wanted to see how their file would respond to presenting a daily donor goal instead of an overall campaign goal. To do this, we replaced any instance of the campaign goal with a daily donor goal of 4 new recurring donors. This change was made in emails 3, 7, 8, and 12.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the presentation of a daily donor goal instead of a full campaign goal did not lead to an increase in conversion rate as hypothesized. The treatment group saw a 1.1% decrease in Clicks for All Traffic compared to the control group, with a statistical level of confidence of 15.5%.

Since the statistical level of confidence is below the standard threshold of 95%, these results cannot be considered valid or reliable for making decisions. Therefore, it is important not to draw any definitive conclusions from this experiment.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to explore other strategies for creating urgency and a sense of immediate impact for donors. Additionally, it is essential to ensure that the sample size is adequate and that the statistical analysis can provide reliable and conclusive results. Experimentation should continue to be a core part of The Navigators' strategy for optimizing their fundraising efforts, but conclusions should only be drawn from experiments with valid and statistically significant results.

experiments.title: How giving a monthly gift to a person instead of a fund impacts overall click through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that giving the option to give a monthly gift to a person rather than a fund will increase conversion rates because it creates a personal connection and fosters a sense of ongoing partnership.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/06/2024
The Navigators ran their bi-annual sustainers campaign in October with the goal of acquiring new recurring donors. Their campaign goal was 90 new recurring donors. In the last email of the campaign, they wanted to test how their file would respond to having the option to give to a person rather than a fund of the organization.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that providing the option to give a monthly gift to a person rather than a fund may have a positive impact on click-through rates for acquiring new recurring donors. This suggests that creating a personal connection and fostering a sense of ongoing partnership could potentially increase donor engagement.

However, it is important to note that the results of this experiment are not statistically significant enough to be considered valid for future decision making. The 16.6% level of confidence falls below the 95% threshold typically used to determine the reliability of A/B test results.

In the future, it would be beneficial to conduct further experiments with a larger sample size to determine if offering the option to give to a person indeed has a significant impact on conversion rates. It is also important to consider other variables and factors that could influence donor behavior, such as messaging, design, and timing of the campaign.

Overall, while the initial findings are promising, further testing and analysis are necessary to make informed decisions about implementing this strategy in future fundraising campaigns.

experiments.title: How presenting a daily donor goal in a recurring campaign affects recurring donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that presenting a daily donor goal instead of a full campaign goal will achieve an increase in conversion rate because it creates a sense of urgency and allows donors to feel they are part of a tangible, short-term impact.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/05/2024
The Navigators ran their bi-annual sustainers campaign in October with the goal of acquiring new recurring donors. Their campaign goal was 90 new recurring donors, but we wanted to see how their file would respond to presenting a daily donor goal instead of an overall campaign goal. To do this, we replaced any instance of the campaign goal with a daily donor goal of 4 new recurring donors. This change was made in emails 3, 7, 8, and 12.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that presenting a daily donor goal instead of a full campaign goal did not lead to the desired increase in conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a 5.8% decrease in donations compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was below 85% (17.8%), which means that these results cannot be considered valid or conclusive.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to provide statistically significant results. Additionally, it might be worth exploring other ways to create a sense of urgency and make donors feel part of a tangible impact without solely relying on presenting daily goals. This could involve testing different messaging strategies, visuals, or other forms of engagement to see what resonates best with donors and drives the desired outcomes.

experiments.title: How including giving handles on a donation page during a recurring giving campaign impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including giving handles for the Recurring Giving donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because the gift’s impact will be more tangible for the donor. because the gift’s impact will be more tangible for the donor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/24/2024 - 11/04/2024
During IMB’s Fall Recurring Giving Campaign, we decided to test including giving handles within the donation page copy.
Both the control and the treatment had the same gift array and the gift array aligned with the amounts within the giving handles. These giving handles ranged from $5 – $75.
This donation page did default to monthly, but one-time giving was still an option.
It is worth noting that this campaign was shut down early, so this test did not get enough traffic to validate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that including giving handles in the donation page copy led to a 29.3% decrease in Revenue for All Traffic with a 26.4% statistical level of confidence and a 10.1% decrease in Transactions for All Traffic at a 28.3% level of confidence. While neither of these results are trending in the direction we intended, we did see a 210.8% lift in recurring donations at a 93.4% level of confidence.

The recurring donation metrics was extremely close to validating which does suggest that the giving handles resonated well with monthly givers. It is worth testing further into how giving handles can compel IMB's donor base to give.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that there is enough traffic to validate the results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to further test the impact of including giving handles with different messaging or positioning to see if there is a way to communicate the impact of donations effectively without negatively impacting conversion metrics. It is also crucial to consider the potential effects of messaging on donor behavior and to continuously iterate and test different approaches to optimize donation page performance.

experiments.title: Will adding in the value proposition copy from the donate button to the monthly donation page increase transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding biblical resources for the monthly donation page will achieve will increase transactions because their is congruency between the donate button and the copy on the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/04/2024 - 10/29/2024
During our testing travels with the duel donation buttons in the main navigation bar we found that the value proposition of providing biblical resources led to an increase in clicks to the donation page.
We wanted to see what would happen if we adding that same value proposition to the donation page copy.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding the value proposition of providing biblical resources to the donation page copy significantly increased revenue for all traffic. The treatment group, which included the added biblical resources copy, saw a 157.6% increase in revenue with a 97.6% level of confidence, indicating that this change had a substantial impact.

This indicates that there is a strong congruency between the messaging on the donation button and the copy on the donation page, which likely contributed to the increase in transactions. In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue exploring ways to align the messaging throughout the donation process to maintain this congruency.

Additionally, it would be worthwhile to test other potential value propositions or messaging strategies on donation pages to see if they also lead to an increase in donations. By continuing to test and optimize the messaging and value propositions on donation pages, and can further enhance its donation performance and maximize its impact.

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including giving handles  for the Recurring Giving donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because the gift’s impact will be more tangible for the donor. because  the gift’s impact will be more tangible for the donor.

While neither of these results are trending in the direction we intended, we did see a 210.8% lift in recurring donations at a 93.4% level of confidence.

Additionally, it may be beneficial to further test the impact of including giving handles with different messaging or positioning to see if there is a way to communicate the impact of donations effectively without negatively impacting conversion metrics. experiments.title: How a one-time vs monthly ask impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for a monthly gift for October Scripture Memory participants will achieve an increase in monthly gifts because the donation page will default to the monthly form and the visitor will be more likely to complete what is asked of them.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/31/2024 - 11/01/2024
Every month, The Navigators run a Scripture Memory text campaign. At the end of the week long campaign, the participants receive an ask.
We tested this for the month of September, but the test was slightly biased in terms of value prop, so we retested it in the month of October.
Control: one-time ask in the donation page copy & one-time default on donation form
Treatment: monthly ask in the donation page copy & monthly default on donation form
The text copy was identical for both groups and they were asked to “make a gift” generally.
The donation page copy was identical except for a single statement asking for a one-time vs monthly gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that asking for a monthly gift in the donation page copy and defaulting to a monthly donation form resulted in a 58.3% increase in one-time donations for all traffic, but oddly had no impact on monthly giving. While this result is statistically significant at a 52.8% level of confidence, it falls below the generally accepted threshold of 95%.

These results suggest that The Navigators audience preferred being asked for a monthly gift, but having the autonomy to make the decision to make a one-time gift.

Due to the invalid results and for the short nature of how long this test ran, we should not make decisions based on these results. However, it does open the door for more testing in terms of what motivates The Navigators audience to give whether that be one-time or monthly.

Overall, the results of this experiment should be viewed with caution due to the lower statistical confidence level, but they provide a valuable insight that can be used to inform future testing and optimization strategies for donation campaigns.

experiments.title: How adding copy about giving monthly on a donation page impact recurring gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding copy about why somebody should become a monthly donor for people who take the survey will achieve more recurring donations because it explains the value of becoming a monthly donor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/20/2024 - 05/08/2024
Care Net has a survey we were running in Facebook. The instant donation page for this offer didn’t have any language around why a donor should give monthly instead of once. We wanted to test how adding language about giving month would impact the recurring gifts made on this page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment version, which added copy about why someone should become a monthly donor, resulted in a 32.1% decrease in Recurring Donations for All Traffic with a 33.1% statistical level of confidence. However, since the statistical level of confidence is below 95%, these results cannot be considered valid for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is essential to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to provide reliable and statistically significant results. Additionally, it is crucial to consider the language used in donation appeals and how it may impact donor behavior. Further testing with different messaging strategies and potentially adjusting the copy to better communicate the value of becoming a monthly donor may help in identifying the most effective approach for increasing recurring donations.

experiments.title: How adding a thermometer on a recurring campaign donation pages impacts monthly donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a monthly donor counter thermometer for DTS’s North Texas Giving Day donation page will achieve an increase in monthly donor conversion because the thermometer adds clarity on the number of people still needed to become a monthly donor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/11/2023 - 09/21/2023
Each year, Dallas Theological Seminary (DTS) participates in the local giving day, North Texas Giving Day. During previous years, they’ve focused on increasing their number of monthly donors—Cornerstone Partners.
This year, they continued in this tradition, but wanted to see what optimizations they could make to their donation page to see if they could increase recurring donor conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. The treatment version of the donation page, which included a monthly donor counter thermometer, resulted in a 23.2% increase in recurring donations compared to the control version.

2. The statistical level of confidence for this result was 64.4%, which is below the accepted threshold of 85%, indicating that the results cannot be considered valid.

3. The hypothesis that adding a monthly donor counter thermometer would increase monthly donor conversions was supported by the observed increase in recurring donations.

4. Future experiments should consider further optimizing the donation page by including a monthly donor counter thermometer as it showed promising results in increasing recurring donor conversions.

5. It is important to have a larger sample size and a higher level of statistical confidence to validate the findings and make informed decisions for future experimentation.

experiments.title: How adding a thermometer on a recurring campaign donation pages impacts monthly donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a monthly donor counter thermometer for DTS’s North Texas Giving Day donation page will achieve an increase in monthly donor conversion because the thermometer adds clarity on the number of people still needed to become a monthly donor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/11/2023 - 09/21/2023
Each year, Dallas Theological Seminary (DTS) participates in the local giving day, North Texas Giving Day. During previous years, they’ve focused on increasing their number of monthly donors—Cornerstone Partners.
This year, they continued in this tradition, but wanted to see what optimizations they could make to their donation page to see if they could increase recurring donor conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. The treatment version of the donation page, which included a monthly donor counter thermometer, resulted in a 23.2% increase in recurring donations compared to the control version.

2. The statistical level of confidence for this result was 64.4%, which is below the accepted threshold of 85%, indicating that the results cannot be considered valid.

3. The hypothesis that adding a monthly donor counter thermometer would increase monthly donor conversions was supported by the observed increase in recurring donations.

4. Future experiments should consider further optimizing the donation page by including a monthly donor counter thermometer as it showed promising results in increasing recurring donor conversions.

5. It is important to have a larger sample size and a higher level of statistical confidence to validate the findings and make informed decisions for future experimentation.

experiments.title: Will a sticky bar with a micro goal increase monthly giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that creating a microgoal of monthly donors for giving tusday will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because there will be a specific deadline involved.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/05/2024
For Giving Tuesday this year we wanted to see if we could achieve a higher number of monthly donors by creating a micro goal for the day. On Giving Tuesday we asked people to be one of seventy five monthly donors needed.
For this experiment we wanted to see if changing the copy to say “Today is the day” would be more compelling to convert a monthly donor.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that changing the copy to say "Today is the day" did not result in a higher conversion rate for monthly donors on Giving Tuesday. The treatment group actually had a 6.7% decrease in donations compared to the control.

experiments.title: How adding emphasis on recurring giving impacts recurring gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that emphasizing recurring giving for donation page vistors will achieve more recurring gifts because it encourages donors to give monthly.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/14/2024 - 07/18/2024
Luther Seminary was running their Fiscal Year End campaign. While writing copy for this campaign, we decided we wanted to test how emphasizing recurring giving on their FYE donation page giving impacted recurring giving.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that emphasizing recurring giving on the donation page led to an increase in recurring donations for Luther Seminary. Despite the positive outcome, the results are not statistically significant with only a 43.3% level of confidence. Therefore, these results should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to produce statistically significant results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer period of time to gather more data and increase the confidence level of the results. This will help in making informed decisions based on reliable data.

experiments.title: How a recurring interrupter with a dynamic ask impacts recurring donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for a smaller, recurring donation for one-time project givers will achieve an increase in recurring giving because it reduces the perceived financial burden, making it easier for donors to commit regularly.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/21/2024 - 07/16/2024
In an effort to increase recurring donors for The Navigators, we originally ran a recurring interrupter on their main donation page. The interrupter only showed for individuals who initiated a one-time project gift under $100. The interrupter then asked the individual to upgrade their giving to a $10 monthly gift. Our hypothesis was that asking for a gift smaller than what was originally initiated would lead to an increase in recurring giving.
This experiment saw a 7.8% lift in recurring donations with a 35% level of confidence. Additionally, we tracked the amount of “yes” clicks on the donation interrupter and found that 4.98% of people who originally initiated a one-time gift of $100 or less converted their gift to a $10 recurring gift.
In this new experiment, we wanted to test adding a dynamic ask amount to the interrupter to capture more donors who were giving gifts larger than $100. The dynamic interrupter ran as follows:
$100>: $10 ask
$100-$199: $15 ask
$200-$299: $30 ask
$300-$399: $40 ask
$400-$500: $50 recurring ask
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the addition of a dynamic ask amount to the interrupter did not achieve an increase in recurring giving as hypothesized. In fact, there was a 2.5% decrease in Recurring Donation for All Traffic with a low statistical level of confidence of 15.6%. This means that the control group actually performed better in terms of recurring donations.
Despite the decrease in recurring donations, we saw that 3.47% of people converted their one-time gift to a monthly gift based on the number of "yes" clicks on the pop-up.
Although this conversion rate it lower than what we saw in the first experiment, it is to be expected. We hypothesize that the conversion rate is the same as experiment 1 for donors who initiated a gift lower than $100, but expect the conversion rate to be a lot lower for people who initiated a gift larger than $100.
We believe this is because people who were making a gift larger than $100 had the intention to give a significant one-time contribution rather than committing to a smaller recurring donation. This suggests that while the dynamic ask amount may not have been effective in increasing overall recurring donations, it did still manage to convert a notable percentage of one-time donors to monthly contributors, particularly among those giving smaller amounts.

experiments.title: How adding a flag icon to a donation form tab affects recurring donation rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a small American flag in a donation form’s monthly gift tab and submit button to build affinity for direct appeal prospects will achieve an increase in conversion rate because the flag image will help align their motivation with the intended action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/30/2024 - 06/11/2024
The Heritage Foundation had previously run a test on their membership renewal page where they added a small flag image to a tabbed donation form’s monthly gift tab in order to appeal to the patriotism of their donors, which led to an increase in donations.
Wondering whether this concept would appeal to donors on another direct appeal donation page, they also tested this flag image in the donation form’s $100 gift array button and in the form submit button, which led to a decrease in donations at a near-valid level of confidence.
As a follow-up experiment on the direct appeal donation page, they wanted to test whether the flag icon in the monthly gift tab and the form submit button would lead to an increase in donations, namely recurring donations.
They launched an A/B test where the control had no flag icon anywhere on the form, and the treatment had the flag icon in the monthly gift tab and the form submit button.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a small American flag image to a donation form's monthly gift tab and submit button did not lead to a valid increase in donations, specifically recurring donations. In fact, the treatment group saw a 40.3% decrease in transactions for all traffic compared to the control group. However, it is important to note that the statistical confidence level of 78.8% is below the valid threshold of 95%, indicating that these results may not be reliable.

The treatment also resulted in just a 0.17% increase in recurring donations at a 68.3% level of confidence. With such a small sample size, these results cannot be relied upon to inform future action.

In future experimentation, it is crucial to ensure that the sample size is adequate to achieve a statistically valid result. Additionally, it is important to carefully consider the impact of visual elements such as flag icons on donor behavior. Other ideas for follow-up testing include testing a different icon than an American flag or testing this concept on a donation page following a content offer.

experiments.title: How a recurring donation interrupter affects recurring donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a donation interrupter to ask users to convert their one-time gift to a monthly gift for potential donors will achieve a higher recurring donor conversion rate because   they will be presented with the opportunity to know what impact a monthly gift can have.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/23/2024 - 05/09/2024
The Heritage Foundation has a page on their website where people can join the organization as a member by making a one-time or recurring donation, and they wanted to test how a donation pop-up interrupter would perform to increase the amount of recurring donors they get through new memberships.
On the donation page, users have the option to make a one-time or monthly gift, but if the user chooses one-time and makes a gift under $100, they would receive a pop-up after hitting “Make my gift” asking them to convert their gift to a $10 monthly donation.
The pop-up included value proposition as to what impact a monthly gift will have.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that implementing a donation interrupter to ask users to convert their one-time gift to a monthly gift had no effect on conversion rates for monthly or one-time giving. The treatment group saw a 32% decrease in Transactions - Monthly, but this decrease is only based on 5 recurring transactions and only had a 33% level of confidence. This means that the results cannot be considered statistically significant and should not be used for future decision-making.
Additionally, we saw a 15% lift in one-time giving but with only a 38% level of confidence.
The key takeaways are that the recurring interrupter will most likely not hurt one-time or monthly giving conversion rates, but we do not have enough data to know if it will produce a lift of any kind.
In future experimentation, it is essential to continue testing the effectiveness of the donation interrupter with a larger sample size and over a longer period to determine its true impact on recurring donor conversion rates. Additionally, considering other factors such as user experience, messaging, and timing of the interrupter could provide valuable insights for optimizing donation processes and increasing donor engagement.

experiments.title: How a recurring interrupter impacts recurring donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for a smaller, recurring donation for one-time project givers will achieve an increase in recurring giving because asking for a smaller gift is appealing to the donor. . because it reduces the perceived financial burden, making it easier for donors to commit regularly.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/26/2024 - 05/07/2024
In an effort to increase recurring donors for The Navigators, we decided to run a recurring interrupter on their main donation page. This test ran for a week and a half split 50/50 among traffic.
The interrupter only showed for individuals who initiated a one-time project gift under $100. The interrupter then asked the individual to upgrade their giving to a $10 monthly gift.
Our hypothesis was that asking for a gift smaller than what was originally initiated would lead to an increase in recurring giving.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the interrupter is converting some donors to monthly giving. Based on the results above, 11 people clicked "YES! Convert my gift to a $10 monthly gift." out of the 192 people who were shown the interrupter. This means that the interrupter converted 5.73% of people who saw it.
While we can't validate these results, it's clear that it is effective for at least 5% of individuals who see it. We also saw a 7.8% lift in overall recurring donations at a 35% LOC.
In future experimentation, we plan to extend the threshold of individuals who qualify to see the interrupter. Since the current cutoff is $100, we plan to extend this up to $500 with tiered levels of recurring asks accordingly and aim to get a valid lift in recurring donations.
Overall, it is encouraging to see the interrupter have a positive impact on gifts, and we hope to continually optimize this experience through testing.

experiments.title: Will Adding a Give Monthly Option to the Site Navigation Help with Monthly Donor Conversion During Calendar Year End?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a give monthly button to the main navigation on the website for people who land on the homepage will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because there will be less friction in the process.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/21/2023 - 12/06/2023
For Giving Tuesday we wanted to test having monthly ask set up as a mini campaign within CYE.
To make it easier to make a monthly gift we wanted to test adding a monthly giving button in the main navigation next to the original give button.
experiments.keyLearnings: In conclusion for this experiment, adding a give monthly button decreased donor conversion by 34% with a 78% LOC. This could be due to several reasons. The button copy itself could use further testing between give and donate. Another possibility is people saw the monthly and that was too big of a commitment so instead of giving monthly, they gave a one-time gift.

experiments.title: Adding a Callout Above the Monthly Gift Frequency Button on the Campaign Giving Page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a brief but impactful message above the monthly gift frequency button on our sustainer campaign giving pages will increase the donor conversion rate because of the social proof and added exclusivity.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/22/2024 - 03/26/2024
By adding a brief but impactful message above the monthly gift frequency button on our sustainer campaign giving pages, will this element help add additional direction to those motivated to give and who will have already landed on the campaign giving page? By adding a donor countdown animation within the message, we hope to add an additional layer of “encouragement” to give during the giving process by showing donors can be part of the answer and help reach an impactful goal through their gift by “becoming one of” the donors needed. We believe adding a handwritten, donor countdown callout above the monthly gift frequency button will insight more donors to get involved and make a monthly donation.
experiments.keyLearnings: The addition of a handwritten, donor countdown callout above the monthly gift frequency button showed a 9.7% increase in recurring transactions at a 23% level of confidence. This test overall was pretty flat. In the future, we would like to rerun this test on a campaign-giving page for a longer amount of time to see if we can reach validity.

experiments.title: How a tabbed donation form impacted monthly gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an animated tabbed form for instant donation page visitors will achieve an increase in recurring donor conversion because there is additional clarity in the process by adding frequency choice before the gift array choice.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/16/2024 - 03/18/2024
When sending pledge signers to an instant donation page, we wondered if emphasizing the monthly gift option by creating a tabbed form experience would increase recurring donor conversion. We split traffic between the two versions and ran for the test for one month.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the animated tabbed form for the instant donation page resulted in a 50.3% increase in recurring donation for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 47.5%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95%. As a result, these results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the statistical level of confidence is at least 95% before drawing conclusions or making decisions based on the results. Additionally, it is essential to continue testing and iterating on different strategies to determine the most effective ways to increase recurring donor conversion. It may be beneficial to explore other design and messaging variations in future experiments to see if they have a significant impact on donor behavior.

experiments.title: How a Monthly Pop-Up on a Main Donation Page Impacts Donor Conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that presenting a monthly popup with a lowered gift amount for donors submitting their one-time donation will achieve an increase in monthly gifts because the popup reinforces the impact of a smaller amount than their original gift made on a consistent basis. .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/03/2024 - 01/12/2024
To increase monthly gifts on CaringBridge’s main donation page, we hypothesized a pop-up that presents the user with an opportunity to turn their original one-time amount into a lowered monthly gift would increase recurring donations. This pop-up would appear when the user submitted their one-time donation. We hypothesized that by making this conversion easy, suggesting a lower amount than what the user was originally going to donate, and reinforcing the impact of that smaller amount on a consistent basis it would improve conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

The treatment, which involved presenting a monthly pop-up with a lowered gift amount for donors submitting their one-time donation, resulted in a 25.0% decrease in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical confidence level of 77.2% is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%, so these results cannot be considered valid.

Our hypothesis for why this popup not only didn't increase monthly donations but in fact decreased donations overall is due to the way CaringBridge's form is preset. Monthly is already pre-selected as the default giving option, so to submit a one-time gift a user would have to intentionally click it, fill out the form, and press submit. Targeting that audience, who already has a specific motivation for making a one-time donation, with another popup likely made the experience more disruptive rather than presenting them with something they hadn't already considered.

In future testing, we will focus our efforts on that initial action — selecting one-time rather than monthly — at the top of the form so that we are meeting the donor at the point of consideration rather than at the end where they've already made a decision.

experiments.title: How do the perceived benefits of a premium affect recurring donor conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that explaining the benefits of a premium book for potential donors will achieve an increase in donor conversions because the additional clarity on the premium will motivate them to click the link and learn more.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/04/2024
For The Navigators February Sustainers Appeal campaign, we decided to test calling out the perceived benefits of the premium in an effort to pinpoint motivating factors for their audience. The control email called out the impact that their monthly gift would have on the organization whereas the treatment called out the benefits of reading the book that they would receive as a premium for becoming a monthly donor.
This test ran as a 50/50 split to their housefile audience in an effort to acquire more recurring donors as well as test what motivates their audience to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that emphasizing the benefits of a premium in a fundraising appeal may not necessarily lead to an increase in donor conversions. In this case, the treatment that focused on the benefits of receiving a book as a premium for becoming a monthly donor actually resulted in a decrease in donations compared to the control, which highlighted the impact of the monthly gift on the organization. The treatment saw a drop of 44.1% in transactions at a 67.7% level of confidence, which is below the validity threshold.

This copy test saw a valid increase in clicks, but an invalid drop in transactions. Therefore, indicating that the copy was persuasive in getting potential donors to click the link, but their audience was less motivated to give.

In future experimentation, it would be important to continue testing different messaging strategies to understand what truly motivates the audience to give. This could involve testing different types of premiums, highlighting various impacts of donations, or even testing different audience segments with tailored messaging. By continuously testing and learning from these experiments, we can gain a deeper understanding of our audience and optimize our fundraising strategies for better results.

experiments.title: How do the perceived benefits associated with becoming a recurring donor impact click-through rate?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including the benefits of becoming a monthly partner  for the donor will achieve an increase in click through rate because  of the perceived exclusivity and incentives associated with becoming a monthly partner.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/27/2024
For The Navigator’s February Sustainers Appeal, we hypothesized that calling out the benefits of becoming a monthly partner would increase our click-through rate as well as our donor conversion rate. The control email emphasized the importance and need for monthly partners, focusing heavily on the difference it would make for the organization. Whereas, the treatment focused on the benefits for the donor themselves.
This test ran as a 50/50 split to their housefile audience in an effort to acquire more recurring donors as well as test what motivates their audience to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment conducted for The Navigator's February Sustainers Appeal tested the impact of emphasizing the benefits of becoming a monthly partner for the donor, as opposed to focusing on the organization's need for monthly partners.
The treatment email, which highlighted the benefits for the donor, showed a 7.4% increase in Clicks for All Traffic compared to the control email. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 52.7%, meaning the results cannot be considered statistically valid.
However, the treatment greatly outperformed the control in terms of transactions. The treatment saw a 161.3% lift in transactions at a 99.6% level of confidence, meaning that the results are valid!
Therefore, the key learnings from this experiment are that The Navigators housefile is much more motivated to give when exclusivity and incentives are coupled with an appeal. While the results showed that readers were slightly more inclined to click the link when benefits to the donor were included, the individuals who did take the next step within the treatment, were much more motivated to give.

experiments.title: How adding additional value proposition to navigation giving buttons impacts recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding statements below the main donate and give monthly buttons for the site navigation will achieve an increase in recurring donations because it increases the appeal and clarity of giving on a recurring basis.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/18/2024 - 02/23/2024
Care Net tested into adding a “Give Monthly” button in their main navigation next to their main donate button. While this increased clarity, we wondered if we could increase the appeal of giving while adding even more clarity by adding additional copy below the main button text.
For the Donate button, we added the text, “Save a baby today.” For the Give Monthly button, we added the text, “Save babies 365 days a year.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding statements below the main donate and give monthly buttons in the site navigation can potentially increase recurring donations by making the appeal and clarity of giving on a recurring basis more prominent. The treatment, which added the text "Save a baby today" below the Donate button and "Save babies 365 days a year" below the Give Monthly button, resulted in a 67.0% increase in donations for all traffic.

However, the statistical level of confidence for this result was 78.1%, which is below the commonly accepted threshold of 95% for valid results. Therefore, while the treatment showed a promising outcome, it cannot be considered statistically significant and should not be used for future decision making.
