In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to further test the impact of adding additional copy below the main donation buttons to confirm if it consistently leads to increased donations. It is important to ensure that any findings have a higher level of statistical confidence before implementing changes based on the results. Additionally, exploring different variations of the copy or placement of the text could help optimize the impact on donation rates.

experiments.title: How a recurring tab impacted conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a monthly option for web donors will achieve higher conversion rate because there is additional give options and clarity in the type of gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/10/2024 - 01/26/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
PTV’s offers are primarily various books and premiums with donations. Our January emphasis was to increase the number of recurring donors. With this in mind, we decided to try adding a recurring tab to normal offers to see if we could passively acquire recurring donors by just having the option available.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Note: treatment generated 0 recurring gifts

The key learning from this experiment is that adding a monthly option for web donors did not achieve a higher conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in transactions compared to the control group. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, meaning the results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making.

Based on this, it is necessary to conduct further experiments with a larger sample size or different variations to determine the effectiveness of adding a recurring tab for web donors. It is also important to consider other factors that may have influenced the results, such as the timing of the experiment or the specific offer being presented.

Future experimentation should focus on gathering more data and conducting rigorous A/B testing to determine the impact of different variables on conversion rates. It may also be beneficial to analyze other aspects of the donor journey, such as the effectiveness of different messaging or marketing channels, to gain a deeper understanding of donor behavior and preferences. Additionally, seeking feedback from donors and analyzing their motivations and decision-making processes can provide valuable insights for future experiments.

experiments.title: How a recurring donation interrupter affects recurring donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a donation interrupter to ask users to convert their one-time gift to a monthly gift for potential donors will achieve a higher recurring donor conversion rate because they will be forced to think of the impact and what they receive in return to convert the one-time gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/07/2023 - 01/05/2024
MSLF is running a petition through paid Facebook advertising, and they wanted to test how a donation pop-up interrupter would perform to increase the amount of recurring donors they get through acquisition efforts.
Once users finish the petition, they proceed to an instant donation page where they’re asked to make a gift to support this case and the work MSLF is doing. There are options to make a one-time or monthly gift on the donation form, but if the user chose one-time, they would receive a pop-up after hitting “Donate” asking them to convert their gift to a $10 monthly donation.
The pop-up included value proposition as to what they will receive if they make a monthly donation and become a member to MSLF.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a donation interrupter to ask users to convert their one-time gift to a monthly gift can significantly increase the conversion rate for recurring donations.
The treatment group experienced a 154.5% increase in recurring donation conversion rate, indicating that the interrupter was effective in prompting users to consider the impact of their donation and what they would receive in return as a member of the organization. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 75.1%, which falls below the recommended confidence level of 85%.
In addition, the experiment showed that the one-time donation rate decreased by 326% with a 97% level of confidence. This means that the interrupter influenced users to opt out of giving all together instead of making a one-time gift or recurring gift.
Future experimentation can involve refining the design and messaging of the interrupter to maximize its impact. It is also important to consider the potential impact of the interrupter on user experience and whether it could potentially deter users from making any donation at all. A/B testing with different variations of the interrupter can help in identifying the most effective approach.
Overall, the key learnings from this experiment highlight the potential of using a donation interrupter to increase recurring donations. However, further experimentation and validation are needed to make informed decisions and implement effective strategies.

experiments.title: How a recurring tab impacted conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a monthly option for web donors will achieve higher conversion rate because there is additional give options and clarity in the type of gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/10/2024 - 01/26/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
PTV’s offers are primarily various books and premiums with donations. Our January emphasis was to increase the number of recurring donors. With this in mind, we decided to try adding a recurring tab to normal offers to see if we could passively acquire recurring donors by just having the option available.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Note: treatment generated 0 recurring gifts
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a monthly option for web donors did not achieve a higher conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in transactions compared to the control group. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, meaning the results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making.
Based on this, it is necessary to conduct further experiments with a larger sample size or different variations to determine the effectiveness of adding a recurring tab for web donors. It is also important to consider other factors that may have influenced the results, such as the timing of the experiment or the specific offer being presented.
Future experimentation should focus on gathering more data and conducting rigorous A/B testing to determine the impact of different variables on conversion rates. It may also be beneficial to analyze other aspects of the donor journey, such as the effectiveness of different messaging or marketing channels, to gain a deeper understanding of donor behavior and preferences. Additionally, seeking feedback from donors and analyzing their motivations and decision-making processes can provide valuable insights for future experiments.

experiments.title: How does referencing a recurring donor’s status in email copy impact additional giving?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that referencing a donors’ status for recurring donors will achieve a change in giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/01/2023 - 07/01/2023
Pathway to Victory is the international ministry for First Baptist Dallas. They have 2 major match campaigns throughout the year-one during December, the other in June.
For this June match campaign, we wanted to see whether we could get additional one-time gifts from Pathway Partners–who are recurring donors–by referencing their status in email copy and writing to them accordingly.
This test ran over 4 separate email sends.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
1. Referencing a donor's status as a recurring donor in email copy has the potential to increase one-time donations.
2. The treatment group, which received emails referencing their status, saw a 20.0% increase in donations compared to the control group.
3. However, the statistical level of confidence is only 23.7%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85% for valid results.
Given the very small donation sample size, this experiment requires further refinement and re-testing during future campaigns.

experiments.title: How a Monthly Callout in the Gift Array Impacts Recurring Giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlighting the monthly giving option for users on the donation page will achieve an increase in recurring gifts because the copy reinforces the value of a monthly gift to the reader and drives home what a monthly gift accomplishes..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/15/2023 - 05/14/2023
The Sustainers appeal is The Navigators recurring giving campaign. With users arriving on the donation page from a variety of emails, ads, and onsite assets, we hypothesized that by calling out the monthly gift option on the donation page itself would help increase recurring gifts. We believed that a simple value prop callout reminding the user of need for monthly gifts specifically would help convert those who would have originally given a one-time gift or neither at all.
experiments.keyLearnings: The A/B experiment conducted for The Navigators' Sustainers appeal showed that highlighting the monthly giving option on the donation page did not result in an increase in recurring gifts with validity. More experimentation is needed, it also prompts questions about more effective ways to test this like having the value prop callout more prominently in the copy itself or a headline.

experiments.title: How adding a recurring giving pop up during the giving process impacts monthly gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a recurring giving interruptor  for those making a one time gift on the main donate page will achieve an increase in monthly giving because we are adding clarity as to why someone should make a recurring gift instead of a one-time gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/02/2023 - 05/18/2023
In an effort to increase the number of monthly donors, we wanted to prompt more people to give a monthly gift on the IMB’s main donation page. After the first step of the giving process (selecting one time + gift amount + selecting “Give Now”), they would receive a pop up that asked people to change their gift frequency to monthly. They would receive a custom ask amount based off of their original one-time gift amount. If their gift was over $200, they did not receive this prompt.
Our hypothesis is that this prompt would draw more attention to the monthly option and provide additional value proposition language as to why someone should make a monthly gift as opposed to a one-time gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment aimed to increase the number of monthly donors on IMB's main donation page by adding a pop-up interruptor that prompted people to change their gift frequency to monthly after the first step of the giving process. The hypothesis was that this would draw more attention to the monthly option and provide additional value proposition language as to why someone should make a monthly gift as opposed to a one-time gift.
The experiment ran for 15 days and showed that the treatment group saw a 27.6% increase in Monthly Donations with a 39.6% statistical level of confidence. This means that this resulted in virtually no difference in monthly gifts.
But, we did see a valid decrease in overall gifts. A 35% decrease with a level of confidence of 99%. This data point tells us that the pop up was not motivating enough  for people to make a monthly gift, but disruptive enough for people to potentially abandon the giving process.
This experiment will be retested in a different channel - an instant donation page in an offer funnel. It could be that motivations are different enough for prospective donors that we would see different results.

experiments.title: How an animation on the monthly giving option impacts conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an animated heart on the monthly donation tab for people who land on a video series acquisition donation page will achieve a higher number of monthly sustainers because the heart will draw the prospective donors attention to the monthly tab. because  the heart will draw the prospective donors attention to the monthly tab.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/24/2023 - 04/13/2023
Focus on the Family (FOTF) is a global ministry that receives tons of traffic through its acquisition video series. We wanted to see if we could increase the number of monthly sustainers that come from a video series by placing an animated heart on the monthly tab button on the donation form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding an animated heart on the monthly donation tab on a video series acquisition page resulted in a 7.2% increase in revenue for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 76.6% which means the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making.
Further testing will need to be done to see how we can increase the number of monthly sustainers from an acquisition offer.

experiments.title: How adding a high-urgency sticky bar to a recurring donor campaign page affects recurring donation rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a high-urgency sticky bar on the donation page of a recurring donor campaign will achieve higher recurring donation rates by defining a tangible goal and more clearly communicating the problem being solved.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/10/2023 - 03/31/2023
During a recurring donor campaign, we wanted to know how adding a high-urgency sticky bar to the donation page would affect recurring donation rates. The sticky bar combined high-urgency language with a dynamic countdown which automatically refreshed to display how many donors were still needed to reach the campaign goal. We set this experiment up as an A/B test with traffic split roughly 50/50 between the control and the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running this experiment for 21 days, we observed a 5.6% increase in recurring donations produced by the treatment but with a low statistical level of confidence (36.1%). Despite the modest lift in recurring donations, we also observed that the treatment produced a sizable 21% decrease in overall revenue (with a 75.5% level of confidence), indicating that by putting more emphasis on the number of donations, the treatment may have unexpectedly had a negative impact on the amount that donors were willing to give. It may therefore be worth testing this tactic against a monetary goal in the future.

experiments.title: How an exit-intent popup affects overall donation rates on a donation page during a recurring-giving campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that showing an exit-intent popup with a one-time ask for traffic attempting to exit a recurring-giving donation page will achieve higher overall donation rates by presenting a lower-commitment offer to leverage the reject and retreat principle of persuasion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/07/2023 - 03/17/2023
During a high-urgency campaign focused on acquiring new monthly donors, we wanted to know how an exit-intent popup would affect overall donation rates.
As the name applies, the exit-intent popup would populate only as a prospect attempted to leave the donation page without completing a donation. The specifics of the popup were designed to leverage the persuasion principle of reject and retreat. This principle is based on the idea of reciprocity and states that when someone is presented with a perceived large commitment and rejects the offer, they may be more likely to accept a subsequent offer if it has a perceived lower level of associated commitment—due to the fact that the prospect will be motivated to reciprocate the retreat by accepting the smaller offer.
In this case, the exit-intent popup would provide prospects who are attempting to leave the page without becoming a recurring donor the opportunity to still make an impact by instead giving a one-time gift of the same amount.
For this experiment, 50% of donation page traffic was shown the exit-intent popup while the other 50% did not see anything when they closed the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the treatment showing the exit-intent popup did produce a slight lift in overall donations, we are unable to draw any definitive conclusions about this experiment based on how the results were tracked.

Because the experiment session "fired" based on page load (rather than popup load) we are unable to determine whether it was the popup that persuaded more people to give or if these results occurred merely by chance. We were also only able to achieve a 39% level of confidence in these results. This does not mean that the tactic itself is invalid, just that in order to retest we will want to make sure the experiment "fires" only once someone see the popup and attempt to obtain a larger sample size to validate the results.

experiments.title: How asking existing donors for a recurring donation on an instant donation page affects recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the value proposition to a recurring donation ask on an instant donation page for existing donors will achieve a higher recurring donor conversion rate because these people are donors and have shown that they care about this organization in the past.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/03/2023 - 04/01/2023
Luther Seminary is wanting to increase the number of recurring donors they have. In order to try and get existing donors to convert to recurring donors, we decided to target their existing donors with Facebook ads that advertised their free content offers. They typically offer study guides or online courses.
We decided that 50% of traffic would see the regular instant donation page that includes value proposition around funding the next resource, and the other 50% of traffic would see an instant donation page with value proposition centered around becoming a monthly donor with the recurring tab preselected.
We made sure to only target donors by setting the audience targeting to include traffic when the utm_content contains “donors” (the name of the asset).
experiments.keyLearnings:
The recurring ask instant donation page saw a 3.8% lift in recurring conversion with a 69.2% level of confidence. But this is only factoring in one gift.

We did see a 74.4% decrease in overall giving with a 97.6% level of confidence for one-time donations.

Due to this experiment running for over a month, we decided to turn it off. The traffic to this experiment was very low because this ad was in a multi-offer campaign specifically targeting existing donors.

Although we did not see a valid increase or decrease in recurring giving, we do believe the recurring ask negatively affected giving all together. We would like to test this experiment again for an offer that is receiving more traffic.

experiments.title: How a premium affects recurring conversion rate on a main donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a premium for main donation page visitors will achieve an increase in recurring donations because it provides an incentive to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/05/2023 - 02/02/2023
CaringBridge saw strategic value in growing their recurring donor program, and wanted to run an experiment on their main donation page. Through a partnership with Bicycle cards, they had the chance to offer a unique deck of playing cards in exchange for a recurring gift.
They wanted to test this concept to see if it would increase recurring gifts.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment ran for 27 days and the results indicate that overall conversion saw a significant increase of 3.4% when adding the premium incentive. However, this result is too small to be considered statistically valid due to a 5.9% level of confidence.
Overall, the key learning from this experiment suggests that recurring donors do not respond positively when provided with incentives. Future experiments should focus on better understanding the motivation as to why people want to give recurring gifts, attempting to tailor incentives in a way that resonates most with their movement. This, in turn, will lead to sustainable increases in conversions and eventually, customer loyalty.

experiments.title: How “rally together” messaging impacted donations for a recurring giving program
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that “rally together” messaging for potential donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because donors will want to unite against the threats to Texas independence and be a part of the efforts to combat those threats.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/11/2023 - 02/17/2023
A public policy client has struggled to get traction on their recurring giving donation page. The page is producing a 0.29 instant donor conversion rate heading into this experiment. Assessing the current donation page copy, we wondered if it is too general and does not do a good enough job of inciting emotion among potential donors. We also wondered if page visitors would convert at a higher rate if we apply relevant language to the current public policy landscape in Texas and the threats to the people of the state.
We’ll develop a donation page treatment that uses “rally together” language, highlighting specific threats to Texas and its citizens and making it clear that these threats won’t seize unless we come together as one to fight for better policy and safeguard our personal liberties. The copy will reference current threats and conditions that are close to home and close to the heart of conservative Texans.
We expect this messaging to increase motivation and urgency to join the Come and Take It Society as a monthly donor and make a continuous effort to fight for independence in Texas.
experiments.keyLearnings: Neither the control nor the treatment reached significance. The treatment led to 5 donations to 4 for the control. Revenue was a tick higher for the treatment compared to the control, but the increase in revenue only had a 67.1% level of confidence. It seems the "rally together" messaging did not motivate potential donors to convert to donors in the way that we hypothesized it would. The messaging could be worth testing through a regular offer in which someone can make either a one-time or monthly donation. However, for the recurring ask, the messaging did not do enough to make potential donors give recurring gifts at a rate in which we could trust rolling out the treatment.

experiments.title: How a lower monthly ask impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by decreasing the monthly amount to $10 for web traffic will achieve an increase in transactions because the lower amount is more attainable and less of a financial sacrifice.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/01/2023 - 02/04/2023
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Church Dallas. Through radio, tv, digital efforts, and resources Pathway to Victory shares the truth of God’s word with people worldwide.
In January 2023, we emphasized Pathway to Victory’s Pathway Partner program. This is a recurring gift program where the donor makes a monthly or annual gift to PTV and receives resources each month and exclusive access to a library of sermons and other incentives.
Traditionally the entry Pathway Partner monthly amount has been $25. We decided to test an entry of $10 to see if this would increase the number of donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Overall we only saw a 9.4% increase in conversions with a 47.2% level of confidence.
In revenue, we saw a small decrease in the average gift but that is not surprising due to the lower ask of the treatment.
The average PTV audience falls within the 65+ age range, however we saw that 35-44 visitors had a 415.9% increase in donations with a 91% level of confidence (1 vs 5 donations). While this was not the purpose of this test, activating a younger audience, it is interesting to see that impact.
We will continue testing into how we can convert more people to become Pathway Partners.

experiments.title: How anniversary language and monthly donor language affect conversion rate on a monthly giving popup
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding anniversary language and changing “partner” to “monthly donor” for returning visitors will achieve an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/08/2022
CaringBridge had used popups to ask repeat visitors to consider giving a monthly gift. While the performance had built a file of recurring donors, they wanted to see if they could increase the clickthrough rate and conversion rate to monthly donor. They formed two hypotheses:
Using language highlighting their 25th anniversary would increase relevance and clickthrough rate and conversion rate
Using “monthly donor” rather than “CaringBridge partner” would increase the clarity of the ask and increase conversion rate.
They decided to test this with an A/B/C test. Their first treatment introduced the 25th anniversary language, but kept the “partner” language. The second kept the 25th anniversary language, but introduced “monthly donor” in place of “partner”.
They launched this to determine a winning variant.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While there weren't enough monthly transactions to validate the experiment, the second treatment (which included both the added relevance of the 25th anniversary language and the clarity of the term "monthly donor") showed a non-valid lift of 42%.
The low conversion rate made this unlikely to validate, as the difference was 0.00008205212361% for the control and 0.0001166588894 for the second treatment. However, it was clear that the first treatment underperformed, so they decided to run this experiment with only two variants to see if they could deliver a valid result.

experiments.title: How a tabbed donation form affects recurring giving rates on an acquisition offer
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a tabbed donation form will increase recurring giving rates on the donation page of an acquisition offer by increasing the visibility of the option to give monthly.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/27/2022 - 12/02/2022
In an effort to increase the recurring giving rate on an instant donation page of an acquisition offer, we wanted to know how increasing the visibility of the monthly giving option earlier in the donation process, with a tabbed donation form (which in the past has shown success in this regard), would affect recurring monthly giving.
We ran this experiment for 30 days as an A/B test splitting traffic 50/50 between:
A. the control that features a monthly giving checkbox beneath the gift array, and
B. the treatment that removes the checkbox in favor of a monthly giving tab above the gift array
In addition, the impact statement featured below the monthly giving checkbox in the control was moved above the monthly donation form in order to keep it in close proximity to the monthly giving option.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 30 days, the treatment featuring the tabbed donation form produced a 16.6% DECREASE in recurring giving rates with a 47.7% LoC. While the low statistical validity of this experiment prevents us from drawing definitive conclusions about these results one way or another, we did observe that perhaps the checkbox in the control may have offered a more logical "flow" during the donation process, prompting users to first select their donation amount, then select how often they want to give, then view the impact that monthly donation will make.
But because the results of this experiment were well below the 95% LoC that would make them statistically valid, and because we have seen tabbed donation forms produce lifts in recurring giving for other clients in the past, we will want to look for opportunities to test this treatment on a donation page that has an opportunity to produce a larger sample size and validate the results one way to the other.

experiments.title: How adding a monthly donation button to the website’s main navigation menu impacts onsite donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a monthly donate button to the website’s main navigation menu will increase donor conversion
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/10/2022 - 10/24/2022
KCBI is a large Christian radio station in the DFW area with a worldwide listening audience. They wanted to see if they could improve donor conversion on their website by adding a monthly donation button to their main navigation next to their one-time donate button. Monthly donors are an important part of their program and they hoped that this additional callout would increase monthly donor conversion.
When they first tested this approach (see prior experiment here), they found a significant increase in donor conversion. However, upon further analysis, that experiment changed multiple variables, so its results were not reliable. They then tested each of the individual variables:
Test the position of the one-time gift button (see experiment writeup here)
Test the copy on the one-time gift button (give vs donate) – see experiment writeup here
And finally, they tested the addition of a monthly donation button in the main navigation menu. This test ran more than two months.
experiments.keyLearnings:
At no point did the additional button increase donor conversion. For several weeks, it appeared that there would be a valid decrease in donor conversion measured, but that too never reached a point of statistical validity. Ultimately, we were unable to measure a positive impact from adding the button so we will not be changing the navigation menu.
For future tests, we will not let an experiment run as long as this did because we will begin experiencing data pollution past 4-weeks of testing. This experience also showed the importance of not just taking an initial win in an experiment at face value, but ensuring that we are measuring each variable correctly and, if not, intentionally testing each and every variable so we know what is in fact impacting conversion rates.

experiments.title: How adding images of associated premiums impacted conversion rate and recurring giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that putting the ebook premiums on display for potential donors to physically see will achieve an increase in donations and specifically recurring donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/22/2022 - 10/01/2022
TGC has a recurring giving funnel in which they offer premiums as an incentive for someone making a recurring gift. The premiums are clearly stated in the copy, however, there were no images of the premiums on the page. We thought that adding images of the premiums would perhaps increase donations and recurring donations as users would be able to see the premiums that they would be receiving when making a recurring donation. We added the images after mentioning the premium incentive and above the donation form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The outcomes of the experiment were negligible. Neither the control nor the treatment led to a recurring donation, and the treatment resulted in a 20.5% increase in one-time donations (3 donations vs. 2) but with only a 16.5% level of confidence. The addition of the premium images carried led to a small and invalid impact.

We may want to push the experiment a step further by adding some sort of preview of the ebooks. This could consist of an image and a summary of the ebook (on-page) since we're trying to stress the premiums as an incentive to make a recurring gift.

experiments.title: How Calling Out The Name of a Monthly Giving Program on Tabbed Donation Form Affects Monthly Giving Rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that replacing generic monthly donation form tab copy with program-specific copy will increase recurring donor rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/13/2022 - 10/02/2022
In an effort to combat ‘messaging fatigue,’ we wanted to know how replacing the generic monthly donation tab copy with language that specifically calls out the name of their recurring giving program will affect monthly giving. We replaced “Recurring monthly gift” with “Become a Cornerstone Partner” to see if we could increase exclusivity and a feeling of community by calling out the program by name. We ran this experiment during DTS’s annual North Texas Giving Day campaign, and split traffic 50/50
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 16 days, we saw a 155% increase in monthly donation rates with a 75% level of confidence. Although there was a low volume of monthly gifts (restricted by the shorter length of a campaign), we were encouraged to see a significant lift in recurring giving via the treatment which included the "Become a Cornerstone Partner"--the name of Dallas Theological Seminary's monthly giving program.
We believe that calling the monthly giving program out by name was able to add value in the eyes of donors by conveying exclusivity and a sense of community/belonging. We also believe that breaking away from more generic 'give monthly' language helped the monthly giving option stand out by reducing "messaging fatigue" and thus doing a better job of catching the donor's attention.
Based on these results, we will look to validate this hypothesis by rolling the same treatment out to higher-traffic donation pages that are not as restricted by the truncated timeframe of a high-urgency campaign.

experiments.title: How adding a visual call-out to for a monthly gift ask impacted conversion rates.
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a visual monthly call out for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/01/2022 - 05/16/2022
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate serve poor and needy people in the United States and more than 70 countries around the world. The recruitment of monthly donors makes up a large part of their digital program; new monthly donors are offered the opportunity to have a candle lit at the shrine of our Lady of the Snows every month.
For this experiment, which we ran during the Easter season, we wanted to test whether having a visual call-out for monthly gifts made an impact on monthly donor sign-ups.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For this experiment, there was a decrease in the number of monthly gifts that came in during this testing period of 28%, with a 78% level of confidence. While the total number of gifts is just barely over the number we like to have to validate an experiment (50), there were a couple of differences in the two forms that contributed to mixed testing results.
On the control version, there is a check box one needs to select in order to make your gift become monthly; for the treatment version we not only introduced the visual call out, but also added in a tabbed form. We believe that this contributed to the change in conversion rates.
For future tests, we will try and isolate just a single change on the form to get a cleaner test result.

experiments.title: How adding a ‘handwritten’ call out for monthly donations impacted donation conversion rates
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate

rows.name: Add handwritten note focusing on monthly donations

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a graphic call out for potential monthly donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/09/2021 - 04/22/2021
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate serve poor and needy people in the United States and more than 70 countries around the world. The recruitment of monthly donors makes up a large part of their digital program; new monthly donors are offered the opportunity to have a candle lit at the shrine of our Lady of the Snows every month.
For this experiment we tested whether a graphic call-out, in the form of some ‘handwritten’ text, focusing around the impact monthly donations would make, had an impact on monthly donor sign-ups.
experiments.keyLearnings: By adding in the 'handwritten' font emphasizing what an impact a monthly gift could make, there was a 21% increase in the number of new monthly donations converted on this experiment. While the level of confidence was only 56% due to the lower volume of recurring gifts, this result did indicated that further testing and refinement was needed.

experiments.title: How would ordering the giving tabs with the monthly giving option showing first (the left tab) impact recurring giving?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that swapping the giving tabs to show monthly giving first for page visitors will achieve an increase in monthly giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/28/2022 - 08/26/2022
With the goal to increase recurring giving to PFI’s Lamplighters program, we conducted an experiment in which the monthly giving tab (“Monthly Gift”) would show as the left tab and the one-time giving tab (“Give Once”) would show as the right tab on the Lamplighters giving widget. This order is the reverse of the existing setup (control variant) in which monthly giving shows on the right and one-time giving shows on the left. We ran the experiment on the Lamplighters landing page giving widget.
Lamplighters historically receives a lot of one-time gifts, so we want to see if this change will result in an increase in monthly gifts, as monthly gifts are much more valuable over time. The idea is that page visitors would read and notice the “Monthly Gift” option first, indicating greater importance and making it resonate with them ahead of the one-time giving option – prompting them to make a monthly gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment variant resulted in a 34.4% increase in recurring giving conversions for all traffic with a level of confidence of 64.8%. Strictly for desktop users, the treatment resulted in a 187.2% increase in recurring giving conversions with a level of confidence of 89.4%.
Our hypothesis proved correct, that visitors noticing and reading the monthly giving option first instead of one-time giving would prioritize monthly giving in their minds and result in more monthly giving conversions.
Interestingly enough, total donations (one-time and monthly) showed 21 donations through the treatment and 18 through the control. So, despite the treatment placing a greater focus on monthly giving, users still made more donations overall when seeing the monthly giving tab on the left and the one-time giving tab on the right.
This concept is certainly worth testing elsewhere given the results and the long-term impact that recurring giving has on revenue.

experiments.title: How splitting out a monthly donate button impacted monthly donor donations
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that calling attention to a monthly donor program for potential donors will achieve a change in donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/18/2022 - 05/16/2022
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate serve poor and needy people in the United States and more than 70 countries around the world. The recruitment of monthly donors makes up a large part of their digital program; new monthly donors are offered the opportunity to have a candle lit at the shrine of our Lady of the Snows every month.
For this experiment, which we ran during the Easter season, we wanted to test whether splitting out a donate and monthly-donate-specific button would have any impact on new monthly donor conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment had a 23% increase in the number of new monthly donors who were converted using the monthly call out button, but only had a 46% level of confidence given the lower total number of new monthly donors that came in during that time period.
Despite this experiment running during what is a traditionally a high-impact giving season (Easter), the number of donations was not enough to give us a valid result.
Nevertheless, this is a concept that we will continue testing on the Oblate's main website, especially given the importance of new monthly donors on their program overall.

experiments.title: How a recurring gift pop-up impacted recurring gifts on Giving Tuesday
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a popup with a recurring ask for one-time donors will achieve an increase in monthly donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2021 - 01/07/2022
For previous giving day campaigns, we had seen an increase in overall donor lifetime value and retention for monthly donors. We wanted to find a way to convert one time donors to monthly donors on Giving Tuesday and decided to test a recurring giving pop-up at the point of transaction for one time gifts. The popup intercepted the user before their gift was processed and urged them to convert their one-time gift to monthly instead and reiterated the benefits of giving a monthly gift. We displayed the pop-up to 50% of traffic during the giving day campaign to evaluate its effectiveness.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Because the campaign had a very limited window to run, we did not receive enough sessions or transactions to validate the experiment. We will want to test this again with a longer runway to capture a proper amount of traffic in order to more thoroughly analyze how this impacts recurring giving in the future.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Mobile visitors had a 215.6% increase in recurring donation with an 90% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How continuity-based messaging affects recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing continuity-based messaging for people interested in becoming a recurring donor will achieve increased donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/08/2020 - 12/10/2020
CaringBridge had seen experiments where adding a banner with the value proposition “Support 365 days a year” had increased recurring gift conversion on donation pages. This type of language helped the donor understand the continuity of their impact, and helped make the value proposition for a monthly recurring donation by reminding the donor that their impact went far beyond that initial gift.
Since CaringBridge had a recurring-specific funnel triggered by a popup for engaged visitors, they decided to launch this experiment. They added a banner at the top of the monthly page to express this “continuity-based” message to the visitor, and split the traffic to see how it affected conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment with the banner had a directional increase of 16% in converting page visitors to recurring donors. However, this sample size was too small due to the overall low conversion rate, and it did not reach statistical validity.

experiments.title: How expanding the value proposition in a monthly donation ask email affects click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that expanding the value proposition for a monthly donor ask will achieve a higher click rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/11/2021
In this monthly donor ask email, Live Action tested an expanded value proposition to become a monthly donor over a shortened version that left out a lot of the benefits of becoming a Life Defender.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was a slight lift in clicks from the expanded value proposition treatment, but ultimately this experiment proved invalid.

experiments.title: How continuity-based messaging affects recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing continuity-based messaging for people interested in becoming a recurring donor will achieve increased donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/08/2020 - 12/10/2020
CaringBridge had seen experiments where adding a banner with the value proposition “Support 365 days a year” had increased recurring gift conversion on donation pages. This type of language helped the donor understand the continuity of their impact, and helped make the value proposition for a monthly recurring donation by reminding the donor that their impact went far beyond that initial gift.
Since CaringBridge had a recurring-specific funnel triggered by a popup for engaged visitors, they decided to launch this experiment. They added a banner at the top of the monthly page to express this “continuity-based” message to the visitor, and split the traffic to see how it affected conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment with the banner had a directional increase of 16% in converting page visitors to recurring donors. However, this sample size was too small due to the overall low conversion rate, and it did not reach statistical validity.

experiments.title: How does focusing on a specific ministry over the full suite of programs affect donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Focusing in on one ministry that the donor will be supporting rather than all of the organizations outreach will allow for a deeper explanation of value prop as well as a simpler ask, and that will increase donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/24/2021 - 03/29/2021
Prison Fellowship International launched its first content offer, Where Love & Justice Meet. Since this was a new initiative for them and they didn’t have many learnings from the market yet, we wanted to test which of their ministries were more likely to convert a donor. They have a mix of programs—prison bible studies, sponsorships for children of prisoners, and restorative justice projects. So to find out what converts a donor better, we did a 50/50 redirect split toward two different donation pages—The control, a donation page that showed how the reader’s donation would support their many prison ministries and 2. The Treatment, a page solely focused on the ministry and impact of a their prison bible study called The Prisoners Journey. The goal was to see which inspired more gifts. Because the copy on the donation page for The Prisoner’s Journey bible study was geared toward a monthly recurring donation ask, we made that form a tabbed donation form to highlight the recurring giving option, which was another variation in the test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After a full month of testing, the results are inconclusive. Neither donation page outperformed the other in donor conversion at any level that was valid. Our hypothesis is that both pages had equally strong value prop and simply delving into one ministry is not enough to move a needle on an acquisition offer.
Another key metric we were aiming for was acquiring recurring donors. The TPJ page acquired three monthly donors in that time span and the general ministries donation page acquired 2, so no meaningful findings there either.
Our next step is to focus on the page specific to the Prisoners Journey and learn how we can use their Lamplighter's program for monthly donors to TPJ as a greater incentive to give a recurring gift.

experiments.title: How adding benefits of monthly giving affected clicks in an appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding benefits of monthly giving for a housefile appeal will achieve a higher click rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/03/2021 - 03/11/2021
For a high-urgency campaign, we wanted to test if including the benefits of becoming a monthly donor would increase clicks to the donation page. This was the last email in the campaign and it was set up a personal note forwarding the previous email from earlier in the day with one final request to give before the deadline. The treatment version listed the benefits of becoming a monthly donor and included an image of the premium for monthly donors.
experiments.keyLearnings: Since this email was sent to an audience limited in size, we were unable to validate the results of the experiment. We saw a 24.1% increase in traffic on the treatment but with only a 43.8% level of confidence so we cannot claim statistical significance. Moving forward, we'll plan to retest this same concept in future emails—as well as launch the test in other channels that may reach a wider audience —to see if there are any learnings to be applied.

experiments.title: How a “monthly-only” form affects recurring gift conversion during a recurring gift campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a “monthly-only” form affect conversion during a recurring gift campaign?
experiments.summary:
Ended On:
Hillsdale College was running a campaign to increase membership to their recurring gift club, the Liberty and Learning Society. Their call center had noted that many people in the past who made recurring gifts did so believing that they were one-time gifts, and called to cancel. Therefore, they suggested that the form be a “tabbed” donation form that they defaulted to a monthly gift, but still allowed one-time gifts. The team wanted to test this against a “monthly-only” form to see if the lack of a one-time option moved more people towards one-time gifts. Or, put another way, they wanted to see if having a one-time option acted as a bailout mechanism that gave people an “out” to give a short-term gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment, with the monthly-only option, had no effect on the amount of recurring gifts submitted through the form. This indicated that, at least in the near term, there was no confusion about usage of the tabbed form. As an additional bonus, many people who did not make recurring gifts did make one-time gifts as well, leading to another source of revenue for the campaign, though it was not aligned with the intent of the campaign.
The caveat to this test comes in that it will take some time to see if the tabbed form generated as many downstream donor cancellation calls as the monthly form, though that data can be tied back to the original source form.

experiments.title: How adding a recurring donor ticker affected a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: Will including the live total number of donors acquired towards the overall goal increase conversion rate on a donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/15/2020 - 09/24/2020
With a specific goal set for a high urgency campaign, we wanted to test if adding a ticker to the donation page in a sticky bar to count the number of monthly donors would increase donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we did not reach the minimum level of confidence needed for validity of the experiment and saw a slight decrease in the number of transactions, we did notice that the average gift increased on the treatment, which is something we would want to test further to evaluate. We also hypothesized that the results could have been negatively influenced since the campaign met its monthly donor goal ahead of schedule, there may have been a reduced sense of need or urgency when visitors arrived on the page and saw the goal had already been met before they decided to contribute. For future tests, we want to launch the sticky bar earlier in the campaign to see how it affects motivation and we also want to test replacing the text-only version sticky bar with additional visuals like a progress bar or thermometer to further add clarity to the progress of the goal and what is being counted.

experiments.title: How a post-donation popup ask affects recurring donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a recurring popup ask after a one-time gift convert more donors to recurring donors?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/07/2019
Jerusalem Prayer Team wanted to get more recurring donations to their Survivor Hope Initiative to give it more regular funding so they could feed more Holocaust survivors. One tactic they wanted to try was asking donors, as soon as they had made a gift, to consider converting their onetime gift to a recurring gift with a single click. They created a popup that fired if someone
experiments.keyLearnings:
Ultimately, the popup did not have any impact on recurring donations. This could be because a higher-than-average percentage of donors selected a recurring gift in the initial step, or because of technical issues with the popup.
Future testing will focus on treatments that cater to the largely mobile audience.

experiments.title: Impact of changing the design of a banner ad and directing it to a monthly donation page

rows.name: Dear Reader to pledge to monthly gift

experiments.researchQuestion:
We wanted to learn two things:
Would changing the design of the ad so it was conversational and matched the look/feel of the site decrease visual friction for visitors
Would directing people to a monthly donation page increase donor revenue on the ads.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/17/2019
For several years, we have been running banner ads on a popular pro-life website to try to drive traffic to become donors. Our most successful ads have been ads for our pledges, the most recent being one against infanticide. We wondered if making our ad match the look and feel of the site, rather than have its own design, would decrease friction for visitors. Additionally, we wanted to see how well conversational language would work in the ad. Our final desire was to see how well directing people to a monthly donation page vs a one-time donation would impact the revenue generated from the ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We ran the test for a month. Both ads had great engagement, however the ad on the sidebar got far more clicks than the dear reader variation. We also, as expected, got far more donations on the sidebar ad as it led to a one-time donation page. We brought in 19 donations and raised $633 on that ad. However, through the dear reader variation ad, we brought in 4 new monthly donors and raised $195.70 in their first months gifts. This will result in $2380 over the first year of their giving, 369.7% higher revenue than the control ad. 

Moreover, while the sidebar ad had far more clicks than our variation, it had a lower conversion rate to donors, even with it using a one-time donation page. .27% of clicks on the ad became one-time donors while .31% of clicks on the dear reader ad became monthly donors. While we didn’t have enough samples to validate this portion of the test, it is encouraging to see that we got higher donor conversion on the dear reader variation.

Our next test will be to change the sidebar ad so it drives traffic from a pledge to a monthly donation page. This will let us see which ad brings in more monthly donors.

experiments.title: How a recurring gift pop-up presented at the time of a donation being processed impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would the recurring gift pop-up increase recurring donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/20/2019 - 06/21/2019
Back to the Bible wanted to increase the number of recurring donors coming from their primary donation page. To do this, we developed a treatment that presented a pop-up at the moment a gift was being processed with the opportunity to convert their one-time gift into a recurring donation. The pop-up had value proposition on it for why they should become a recurring donor and lowered the gift amount to be 60% of what their original one-time gift was. A person would either click, “No, make my original gift” or “Yes, make my gift recurring”.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we weren’t able to validate the experiment, we did see a directional increase in recurring gift conversion of 38% with the recurring gift pop-up. What this tells us is that people are willing to make a recurring gift if we clearly communicate the even greater impact they can have through their gift and give them the opportunity to do it. When analyzing all data associated with this experiment we also found that it did not significantly impact the likelihood to give a gift.

experiments.title: How using exclusivity on a recurring gift page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will donor conversion increase by appealing to potential recurring donors through exclusivity?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/31/2019 - 02/26/2019
There has been significant testing done on Focus on the Family’s recurring gift donation page. The majority of this testing has been around strengthening the value proposition. We hypothesized that increasing the exclusivity on the page might increase donor conversion. To do this, we created a treatment that was more conversational and tried to appeal to recurring donors as a select, exclusive group of people, “the few”, that have been chosen or stepped up to give on an ongoing basis.
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment ran for over a month and we weren’t able to validate the results. The treatment had neither a positive or negative impact on donor conversion. When we analyze the experiment, we see that the donor conversion is really low on the page. What this tells us is that we would need a more radical version of the page to impact conversion, or look at testing higher in the funnel to get MORE people to the recurring donor page in an effort to increase recurring donors from this page.

experiments.title: How using altruistic language for the benefits of a recurring gift impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will altruistic language on the recurring gift page impact donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/27/2018 - 01/23/2019
We had previously tested using altruistic language on the recurring gift pop-up but we weren’t able to impact donor conversion positively or negatively with it. Understanding this we hypothesized that the altruistic language might have a greater impact on the actual recurring page where the motivation isn’t quite as high and we might be able to impact donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment ran for eight weeks and we weren’t able to validate it. What this tells us is that the altruistic language really had no impact on conversion. We hypothesize that the majority of people aren’t getting to the part of the page that communicates the altruistic benefits. Because of this, we propose a more radical experiment for the page that tests the overall value proposition.

experiments.title: How personalization and value proposition affect recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How does personalization (and the presence of value proposition copy in the first place) affect conversion for recurring donors?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/31/2018
CaringBridge had recently reintroduced the recurring gift option in their tribute page donation process. While a previous test had showed that a single checkbox with value proposition copy beat a binary (monthly/one-time) option, they wanted to test the impact of personalizing the copy to the subject of the journal. While they were doing that, they wanted to also test a version that had no additional value proposition copy to establish a baseline conversion rate, since their first test was a radical redesign. They designed two treatments—one with personalized copy and one with just “Make this a monthly recurring donation” and launched an A/B/C test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: While there weren't enough conversions to validate any of the treatments, the final treatment with no value proposition copy showed a directional 40% decrease in conversion, with a growing level of confidence. This implies (though not statistically valid) that any amount of value proposition copy is better than no value proposition copy. There was no difference at all in conversion by adding personalization (which goes against the grain of previous testing), but also shows that we must work harder at this ask to increase results, as it is a secondary value proposition—asking the donor to "lift" a little more (with a monthly donation) when they are already making a lift as it is (with a single donation).

experiments.title: How addressing a person’s fear to become a recurring donor impacts conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: Would addressing a person’s potential fear of not being able to cancel their recurring gift increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/23/2018 - 05/09/2018
On Focus on the Family’s recurring gift donation page, we hypothesized that people may have a fear of commitment when it came to becoming a recurring donor. To address this potential concern, we added copy to the page that clearly communicated to people that they could cancel their recurring gift at any time.
experiments.keyLearnings: We had hypothesized that addressing a person’s fear in becoming a recurring donor could dramatically impact donor conversion. What we found was that addressing this element of fear had no impact on conversion. This tells us that there is a different element of friction causing the low conversion rates. We propose potentially discovering this through an exit-intent survey asking users why they didn’t become a recurring donor.

experiments.title: How pre-selecting the monthly recurring gift option impacts recurring donor conversion.

rows.name: Added Call to Action + Recurring Gift Default (1)

experiments.researchQuestion: Would pre-selecting the monthly gift option increase recurring donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/17/2018 - 06/02/2018
FamilyLife launched a recurring gift campaign. On the donation page they pushed people to, donors were given two options for their gift – a one-time gift, or a monthly gift. The primary goal of this campaign was to get more recurring donors. We hypothesized that we might be able to increase recurring donors if we get rid of the one-time gift check box and pre-select the monthly gift option.
experiments.keyLearnings: We weren’t able to validate the experiment, but we did get a directional 33.7% lift in recurring donor conversion. This experiment ran during a high urgency campaign and what we found was that the increase in recurring donor conversion was much higher at the beginning of the campaign

experiments.title: How an open gift field for a recurring donation impacts conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: Would an open giving field increase donor motivation and conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/01/2018 - 06/29/2018
National Breast Cancer Foundation launched a recurring gift campaign asking donors and nondonors to become an ongoing supporter or their organization. Specifically, the recurring gift ask was about providing a mammogram to a woman in need. It costs $13 a month to provide one woman her mammogram for the year. Traditionally, NBCF used suggested giving arrays on their donation form. They hypothesized that an open giving field might inspire more generosity and allow the donor to decide exactly how much they wanted to give. In the body copy before the giving array and open gift field they had strong value proposition for the recurring ask and specifically mentioned the $13/month figure. One additional element tested with the open gift field was they reminded donors what their gift would do after they plugged in their gift amount. The copy would dynamically change depending on the amount of the gift. For example, if a donor typed in $13 for their gift amount it would say, “Your $13 monthly donation provides 1 woman with her mammogram this year.” Or if a donor typed in $40, it would say, “Your $40 monthly donation provides 3 women with their mammograms this year.” They ran this experiment for the duration of the recurring gift campaign that last three weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we weren’t able to validate the experiment, we did see a directional lift of 60.5%. This approach didn’t have a significant impact on gift amount, but it did increase donor conversion. What we learned is that by allowing the donor to decide how much they wanted to give and then reinforcing the value proposition of their gift, we were able to motivate more people to become a recurring donor.
To have a more clear understanding of the specific variable that increased conversion, you could isolate each variable to see if the open giving field or the reinforced value proposition was the motivating factor for donor conversion.

experiments.title: How asking for a recurring gift vs. a single gift affects conversion in a “Dear Reader” experiment
experiments.researchQuestion: Does asking for a recurring donation affect conversion in a Dear Reader experiment?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/16/2018 - 01/25/2018
Through previous testing, Care Net found that by inserting a “Dear Reader” feature at the end of their blog posts, they were able to increase the number of people to their donation page. During the month of January, Care Net made an asserted effort to acquire more recurring donors. To support this effort, they tested adding a recurring focused ask in the “Dear Reader” feature instead of a one-time donation.
experiments.keyLearnings: You can see in the data that we weren’t able to validate the experiment in the short time that we ran it. We needed to turn off this experiment earlier than planned resulting in a small sample size. Because the Dear Reader feature gets more people to the donation page, we recommend testing this recurring gift focus again because of the potential revenue impact it could have.

experiments.title: How the gift array impact a recurring giving campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: How does a blank gift array impact average gift and conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/05/2017 - 07/19/2017
Unfortunately, with this experiment, we did not see enough conversions to draw any meaningful analysis from the results. However, this does seem like an interesting test given the low average gift for recurring donors. It would be worth repeating with a future campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: For the Liberty Brigade acquisition campaign, we wanted to see what kind of impact the giving array had on the likelihood to join and the average gift. We had the hypothesis that using a blank array would allow people to “choose their own generosity” which would result in a higher gift.
